proudProject:
Last co-op term, I was the sole developer on a parking ticket digitization tool for the City of Waterloo; a system to turn handwritten tickets into structured data. I owned the whole thing end to end, which was exciting just as it was terrifying, because I had no background in computer vision, OCR, or model training when I started.
The hardest part wasn't any single technical problem — it was that the problem was simply too big to hold in my head at once. Early on I'd stare at model outputs and documentation with no idea where to even start. But eventually, I was able to break it into stages: instead of "build a handwriting recognizer," it became ten small, attackable problems chained together. By the end, I got that pipeline hit over 92% per-field accuracy in production.

The lesson that stuck with me was that almost any problem that looks impossible from the outside is just a problem I haven't decomposed yet. I also learned to stop polishing and ship, as a model that works at 92% and exists beats a perfect one that never leaves my laptop.

proudProject:
I am incredibly proud of designing a platform framework for Velocty's Future Cities Innovation challenge called the AI Civic Nexus. The vision behind this project was to tackle a fundamental flaw in modern urban planning: standard municipal policies and technological upgrades are usually "one-size-fits-all," inadvertently alienating marginalized, vulnerable, or niche communities.
As the Lead Visionary and UX Designer, I created a comprehensive Figma prototype for an AI-driven governance hub. The platform acts as a smart community connector, designed to intercept zero-sum neighborhood conflicts across all demographics—ranging from neurodivergent individuals and senior citizens to families, youth, and physically disabled residents—and automatically synthesize empathetic, balanced middle grounds.

The main challenge was designing an interface that could take complex human friction and translate it into a unified, actionable resolution. To test the framework, I mapped a tough, multi-demographic case study: an infrastructure dispute between senior citizens requiring maximum pathway visibility to prevent physical injury, and neurodivergent residents suffering severe sensory overload from high-intensity city lighting glare.
It was a classic representation of a zero-sum urban design flaw. In traditional city planning, administrators are forced to choose one community's well-being over another, inevitably making someone feel excluded or unseen in their own neighborhood. My hurdle was figuring out how a digital platform could gracefully map these conflicting human variables into a single, cohesive experience.
How I Solved It (The Figma Prototype)
Instead of choosing a side, I used Figma to map out an end-to-end user experience that engineered a hidden middle ground, demonstrating how cross-community needs can be met simultaneously:
The Policy Mediation Interface: I mapped a conversational dashboard that visualizes conflicting community requirements being parsed by the system.
Adaptive Simulation: The prototype illustrates a smart infrastructure transition—simulating a late-night lighting shift to downward-focused, warm-toned spectrums that preserve window darkness for sensory-sensitive residents, while maintaining motion-triggered brightness strictly on the pavement for walking seniors.
Cross-Community Integration: To completely bridge the gap, I designed a feature linking this infrastructure resolution to a localized volunteer portal. If any resident needs extra support during these altered late-night hours, they can instantly request a community escort.
Lessons Learned
This project taught me that true inclusion cannot be built with a one-size-fits-all mindset. I learned that as designers, our job isn't to look for a compromised baseline that leaves everyone slightly unhappy; it is to use empathetic design to uncover creative alternatives where multiple communities can thrive together. Building this prototype proved to me that thoughtful UX can transform rigid municipal friction into an opportunity for deep community connection and mutual care.
chooseOneAnswer:
An ideal ramble for me would be the deep, almost poetic intersection of pure mathematics and computer science. Most people look at programming and think it's just about memorizing syntax, writing loops, or building apps, but underneath the hood, computer science is really just math that we taught a piece of silicon to calculate incredibly fast.
I could go on forever about discrete mathematics, which acts as the hidden nervous system for everything we build. Take graph theory, for example; it is the exact math Google Maps uses to solve the shortest path problem across a massive grid of city streets in milliseconds, treating intersections as nodes and roads as edges. There is also something deeply fascinating about cryptography and how modern data privacy hinges on number theory, specifically the asymmetry of prime numbers. A computer can multiply two massive primes instantly, but reversing that process to find the original factors takes billions of years, meaning the security of global banking literally relies on a math problem being too "hard" to solve backwards.
If you want to get truly philosophical, the theory of computation proves mathematically that there are some problems a machine can never solve, no matter how much memory or time you give it. Even the current wave of artificial intelligence isn't some magical, thinking sci-fi brain—it is just linear algebra and multi-variable calculus running on an unimaginable scale. When an AI parses an image or a sentence, it converts everything into numerical vectors and performs billions of matrix multiplications to predict what should come next. Realizing that writing code is actually just orchestrating a symphony of ancient mathematical rules makes the entire field feel less like engineering and more like art.

One project I’m proud of is an AI-powered sustainable home design app I built during a hackathon. The idea was simple: a user describes their dream home, and the app generates a sustainable house concept with a 3D model, 2D floor plan, and design recommendations based on climate, materials, and energy efficiency.
My role was mainly on the AI and product side. I worked on shaping the core user flow, connecting the user’s prompt to the AI output, and helping turn the idea into something demo-ready instead of just a cool concept. It was supposed to be the “wow” factor of our demo, but close to submission it started breaking badly: houses had windows on roofs, missing doors, and chimneys floating in the air. chooseOneAnswer:
One issue I run into often is how difficult it is for students and young developers to know whether they are actually learning or just producing things with the help of AI tools. AI can help you build faster, but it can also make you lazy without realizing it. You may understand the final code at a high level, but still struggle to recreate it from scratch.
I would solve this by building a learning tool that turns AI-assisted coding into active practice. Instead of only generating code, the tool would ask the user to explain the code, predict what each section does, fix small bugs, and rebuild parts of the project without help. It could track which concepts the user keeps depending on AI for, such as API routes, database queries, authentication, or state management.
The goal would not be to shame people for using AI. That is unrealistic. The goal would be to make AI a better teacher instead of a crutch. The best version of this tool would help students build real projects while still forcing them to develop actual independence.

proudProject:
The one that keeps coming back to me is the SAR ADC tapeout with C2S2.
Not because it sounds impressive on paper — though 4.44 MS/s at 8 bits in TSMC 180nm is genuinely cool — but because of what the process actually felt like. I joined Cornell Custom Silicon Systems as an undergrad with basically no industry experience, and suddenly I was doing real chip design. Cadence Virtuoso, the full analog flow, layout, verification, the whole thing. No safety net.
My role was on the analog side: the comparator, the CDAC, the switching logic. What I didn't expect was how much of chip design is just... detective work. You build something, simulate it, watch it not work, and then spend three days figuring out why a net label was in the wrong place or why your .ic statement was fighting your .op point. The first time the ADC sim converged and the output FFT actually looked like an FFT — not noise soup — I literally stared at the screen for a second like, wait, did that just work?
The hardest part wasn't the technical depth, though that was steep. It was building intuition from scratch in a domain where mistakes are expensive and feedback loops are slow. When you're doing firmware, you flash and test in seconds. In analog IC design, a wrong assumption in your schematic hierarchy can cost you weeks. I learned to slow down and actually think before touching a tool, which was an adjustment.
What made it work was the team culture. C2S2 runs entirely on undergrads, and everyone is figuring it out more or less simultaneously. We'd pull up Cadence manuals together, poke AMD and Analog Devices engineers with questions, and write our own documentation so the next cohort wouldn't have to start from zero. That knowledge-sharing instinct — leaving the project better than you found it — is something I'm genuinely proud of being part of.
And then the chip came back from the fab. Post-silicon: 7.43 ENOB, 47.5 dB SINAD, comparator offset under 3 mV. It matched the simulations. I had to re-read the measurement results twice.
I think the lesson I keep returning to is that the scariest part of a hard project is usually right at the beginning, before you've built any fluency. The gap between "I don't know how to do this" and "I'm doing this" is shorter than it feels when you're standing at the edge of it. You just have to start, ask questions without embarrassment, and document everything so the next person doesn't have to be as scared as you were.
Also: always check your net labels. Always.
chooseOneAnswer: The problem I keep running into is this: I'll be deep in a design session — schematic open, simulation half-configured, three browser tabs of datasheets — and I need to quickly cross-reference something from a paper I read two weeks ago. I know I read it. I remember the figure. I have absolutely no idea where it is. This sounds trivial until you realize how much of technical work is actually retrieval work. The insight doesn't disappear; it just becomes inaccessible at the exact moment you need it. And the tools we have for managing this are genuinely bad. Folders are a lie you tell yourself. Search only works if you remember the right keyword. Bookmarks are a graveyard. Notion databases require you to have already decided your organizational system before you knew what you were organizing. The deeper issue is that human memory is associative — we remember things by context, by feeling, by what we were thinking about at the same time — but our filing systems are taxonomic. We force nonlinear knowledge into hierarchical folders, and then wonder why retrieval feels like archaeology. I've started thinking about what a better system would actually look like, and I think the key property it needs is that capture has to be frictionless and retrieval has to be semantic. Those two things are in tension with every current tool. Obsidian gets close on the retrieval side but the capture friction is real. Voice memos are great for capture but useless for retrieval. Highlights in a PDF reader are burial, not storage. My actual proposed solution, which I've been sketching out, is something like a personal knowledge layer that lives across your tools rather than being another tool. Concretely: anything you highlight, screenshot, or tab-switch to for more than thirty seconds gets quietly logged with a vector embedding of its content. No folders, no tags required. Then retrieval is conversational — "that paper about comparator offset I was looking at during the ADC project" — and it surfaces the thing. The organizational burden shifts from capture-time to query-time, which maps much better onto how memory actually works. The hard part isn't the technical implementation — embedding pipelines exist, local LLMs exist, the pieces are there. The hard part is making it invisible enough that you actually use it. Any system that requires you to think about the system fails. The best version of this would feel less like a tool and more like a slightly better version of your own memory. I'd probably build it as a browser extension plus a local daemon that indexes your filesystem activity, with a simple natural language query interface. Privacy-local by default, no cloud unless you opt in. Is this a niche problem? Maybe. But I suspect anyone who does work that requires synthesizing information across time — research, design, writing — has felt some version of it.

rambleTopic:
Geoguessr meta-strategy and the semiotics of infrastructure.
Hear me out. Every country leaves fingerprints on its built environment — the angle of utility poles, the typeface on road signs, the specific shade of yellow on median lines, the way guardrails are bolted. Finland has these little reflective road markers that look like nothing else on earth. Bolivia's road paint degrades in a very particular way. The Netherlands has a distinct style of brick-laid bike path. Once you start seeing it, you can't stop. It's basically applied cultural anthropology via Google Street View.
The reason I love it is that it's pattern recognition as a skill, fully self-taught, with no practical application whatsoever, which makes it the purest form of learning. Nobody assigned it. There's no grade. It's just the satisfaction of knowing that a certain blue-gray paint color means you're probably in rural Chile.
bearOrMuffin:
Bear with shark arms, and I want to be clear that this is the obviously correct answer.
The muffin-baby situation has no ceiling on how bad it gets. The ambiguity would follow you everywhere. Every café. Every bakery. Every time someone hands you something saying "here, hold this." The liability alone. And the psychological weight of perpetual uncertainty, you'd never eat a blueberry muffin with confidence again. That's not a life, that's a horror premise.
The bear with shark arms, by contrast, is a finite problem. It's scary, yes. The reach is a concern. The additional bite vectors are a concern. But it's one bear, one encounter, one very bad afternoon. Survivable. Possibly. There are documented bear encounter survival strategies. There are no documented strategies for navigating a world where baked goods and infants are perceptually identical.
Shark bear. Final answer.

One project I'm really proud of was Reparo, my team's project at Hack Canada that won $5k from Reactiv (they make iOS app clips) and we were also finalists for the Most Complex AI track!

On IOS, without downloading an app, you can open Reparo with a QR code or NFC tag, take a picture of a broken household item, and an AI agent diagnoses it, finds the exact repair part or reiterates on the plan if no reasonably priced parts are available, and checks it out from live Shopify inventory in one click. I worked on the agentic pipeline, including diagnosis, part retrieval, checkout, and also scraping Shopify storefronts to build the product catalog.

Our biggest challenge was hallucination. The agent would normally parts that didn't exist. We fixed it with RAG over the scraped catalog, so the LLM could only act on real, in-stock items.

Biggest lesson: extending our basic idea to what companies and people would actually want. Like companies packaging their products with a QR code, so our app could generate repair plans using that company's own parts for extra profit when items break down, and also making it easier for companies to profit from right to repair laws.

proudProject:
GreenLens AI is the one I keep coming back to when people ask me this. I built it at Data Minds Challenge 2.0, a 6 day hackathon, and we placed first overall. 

The idea came from a real frustration I had reading about ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) auditing. Companies spend weeks manually pulling data from different sources, auditors comb through everything by hand, it's slow and inconsistent. I figured multi-agent AI systems were finally capable enough to automate most of it.

My role was mainly the technical architecture. I designed a 6-agent pipeline where each agent handled a different part of the audit, carbon footprint, water usage, license waste, ROI analysis. Getting them to hand off context cleanly to each other was way harder than I expected. I rewrote the orchestration logic probably three times before it felt right.

I also integrated Microsoft Graph, Google Workspace, and OpenAI APIs to pull in live data and built a z-score anomaly detection layer with OLS regression to surface actual signals in the ESG data rather than just spitting out raw numbers. That was the part I was most proud of because it pushed the project from "cool API wrapper" to something that could actually be useful.

The hardest part was time. 6 days is less than it sounds and we spent the first few days chasing a version of the idea that was way too broad. Taking 30 minutes to sit down and clearly nail the scope saved us so much time later.

I also learned that the boring parts are what make or break something. The anomaly detection wasn't flashy but it was what made the outputs trustworthy. Making insights interpretable is a harder problem than chaining APIs together and it's the one most people skip.

We generated real ESG reports from live data with actual anomaly detection running. Nothing was mocked. That's still the standard I try to hold myself to.
chooseOneAnswer:
I use AI tools pretty much every day so I've probably thought about this more than most people have.

My take is that AI doesn't diminish creativity. What it does is change who gets to participate in it.

For most of history, being "creative" in any real sense meant you needed years of technical training first. Learning to paint, compose music, produce anything at a professional level, there was always a steep skill floor before you could even get the thing in your head out into the world. A lot of genuinely creative people never got to express that because they didn't have access to the tools or the time or the training to close that gap. AI lowers that floor by a lot and I think that's mostly good.

I've seen people use generative tools to prototype ideas they never could have explored before. Non-designers building things that actually look great. Writers trying structures they'd never attempt from scratch. That's not creativity dying, that's creativity spreading to people who were locked out before.

The part that deserves real conversation is attribution and consent. When a model generates something clearly pulling from the style of a living artist who never agreed to be trained on, there's something uncomfortable about that. It's not that the output is uncreative, it's that the process has ethical gaps nobody has really resolved yet.

But there's a real difference between AI-assisted creativity and replacing creativity. When I use AI to explore an idea, I'm still making every decision that actually matters. The vision, the taste, the judgment of what's good. The model is just a faster way to externalize things. The direction still comes from me.

If anything, AI has made something interesting visible. A lot of what we called "creativity" was actually just technical skill. The person who could render a hyperrealistic portrait was impressive but not necessarily more creative than someone with an incredible vision and no fine motor skills. AI separates those two things, which is pretty revealing when you sit with it.
Where I think human art still stands alone is intentionality. A song written because someone was going through something at 2am carries something with it that a generated song doesn't, even if the outputs sound the same. That layer of "this came from a real experience and a real person" still matters to people and I think it always will.

So AI doesn't diminish art. It expands access, which is mostly good. But it forces us to re-examine what we were actually valuing when we said someone was creative, and that conversation is overdue.

rambleTopic:
Badminton. I've been playing competitively for over 10 years and I genuinely think it's one of the most misunderstood sports out there.

People hear badminton and picture a plastic shuttlecock in someone's backyard. What they don't realize is it's one of the fastest racket sports in the world. Shuttle speeds in elite play can exceed 300 km/h. The footwork is insane, you're covering the full court laterally in under a second, constantly reading and reacting and resetting.

What I love about it is that it rewards athleticism and strategy equally. Positioning, deception, reading your opponent's patterns. A slower, smarter player can absolutely beat someone way more athletic if they play smart. It's kind of like chess at 300 km/h.

Ask me about the difference between a flick serve and a drive serve and I won't stop talking.
bearOrMuffin:
Going with the bear with sharks for arms and I've genuinely thought about this.

The muffin/baby situation is the more catastrophic option here by a mile. The consequences of mixing those two up even once are irreversible. There are no do-overs. The social, legal, and existential fallout is incalculable and I would spend every waking moment second-guessing my own perception of reality. That is not a life I want.

The bear with shark arms is a physical threat but it's a known physical threat. I can prepare for this. I can develop a strategy. Sharks are built for water. On land, the biomechanics are genuinely questionable. No grip, no dexterity, significant mobility problems. That bear is losing a ton of capability by having fins instead of claws and I'm exploiting every bit of that.

I would rather fight something I understand than distrust my own eyes forever.

proudProject:
The current project that I'm working on involves using a SOTA (state of the art) model called TABM. It's a very lightweight model that represents an ensemble of MLP (multi-layer perceptrons), and the benchmarks show that for tabular data cases, it out performs a lot of the larger LLM/Transformer-based and tree-based model architectures. Since I'm pretty new to the project and it's been running for a while, I had to onboard quickly and effectively. Additionally, since the project is heavily research based, there wasn't a lot of documentation.

Onboarding involved reading multiple published papers, digesting them myself, and then sifting through uncommented code to try to get a baseline level of comprehension. From there, I had to use my understanding of the model and the current code set up (validation loop, data sources, data-pull pipeline, model enhancements/alternations) and wrap the entire model to convert it to ONNX (this was my role). ONNX is a very niche model development framework (think PyTorch) that can be serialized in a very friendly way - the details are unimportant but just know that barely anyone uses it. There was a challenge where a part of the data pre-processing didn't convert properly to ONNX, so I had to also read the ONNX operations and write the equivalent procedure by hand.

From here, I also had to apply our validation loop to ensure that the ONNX representation of the model outputs similar enough outputs, when given the same input. The ONNX model ended up being different by a factor of 10^{-9}, which is acceptable enough (probably just FP issue). 

The entire project is deeply technical, and while I do have experience reading papers I mostly just do it for leisure, never acting upon the knowledge that I consume. This project was different because there was a urge to complete my onboarding as fast as possible, and using the knowledge to apply changes to an exiting system. I learned a lot about the research process in the industry, and faced multiple challenges such as collaborating with non-technical stakeholders, sifting through undocumented archaic code, and also having to manage older versions of the ONNX library with very restricted/finicky operations. Thankfully these were all able to be overcome with helpful guidance from my manager and the other researchers on my team, and my passion for interesting problems.

Answering #1:

At my current internship, there is a flavoured water machine. They have a variety of 0 calorie waters, and I am ADDICTED. I LOVE flavoured water - the machine can even give carbonated beverages. I've basically replaced soda in my life with this machine (but work also has free Coke Zero so I dabble in that). The downside of having a machine that can dispense flavoured and carbonated water on a whim is that I end up needing to pee a lot. This is my day to day issue. I have a foolproof solution that is only not being implemented due to shame.

The solution begins by keeping multiple 2L soda bottles under my desk. The solution should be easily interpretable from here. The desk has a side cabinet with a lock, so these bottles can be kept out of anyone's line of sight. This can be further enhanced by bringing in a blanket to be discrete, and by  adding a funnel and a plastic bendy tube. A flaw in this plan would be the noise - it's impossibly to discretely perform the action, but using a long tube will reduce the kinetic energy of the incoming liquid, reducing the noise. The smell will be dealt by wearing heavy fragrances every day, and purging the bottles every night at 5pm.

Now, you could argue that "getting up to go to the washroom is a good way to introduce natural breaks to your workflow and also lets you socialize with your coworkers, and also sitting for long periods of time is detrimental to your health." To anything who believes that, I do not think that they are as locked in as I am, and I feel pity that these people have never felt such a strong urge to be productive that they would rather create a portable urination solution than take 2min to go to the washroom.

proudProject: I am incredibly proud of CORnet-Mouse (CORM), a biological neural network I developed to model the mouse visual system using reinforcement learning. My role involved architecting the network topology and bridging the gap between biological processes and computational logic. I then tested this using the ML-agents library in Unity, and essentially simulated evolution to see the mouse "learn" how to escape predators and reach shelters. The biggest challenge was translating chaotic, real-world biological constraints into a structured, mathematical RL model that actually learned. It was a massive trial in debugging complex systems. The biggest lesson I learned was the importance of stepping back from the code to rethink the underlying architecture; sometimes, the bug isn't in your script, it’s in your fundamental mental model of how the system should behave.
chooseOneAnswer:
I could ramble endlessly about bacteriophages! They are some of the coolest entities on the planet. As the world becomes increasingly frightened by the looming threat of antibiotic resistance, these phages could genuinely be our silver bullet. They naturally hunt and destroy specific bacteria without harming human cells, and the best part is the evolutionary tradeoff: we've found that as bacteria become more resistant to antibiotics, they actually become less resistant to bacteriophages.

To me, this is just one amazing example of how we can use nature to solve complex problems. I recently saw another example in robotics: prosthetic limbs that use fluid and spindles to mimic how a human muscle operates, which ended up being incredibly stronger and more efficient than traditional mechanical ones. I just love the idea of using nature's blueprints to drive innovation.

proudProject:
ALFRED is one of the projects I am most proud of. It is an assistive navigation device that my team and I built for a client with Usher's Syndrome, a condition that affects both vision and hearing. The goal was to create a hands-free prototype that could recognize objects in real-time and convert them into audio feedback, giving the user greater environmental awareness independently.

I led the end-to-end development. I wrote C++ on an ESP32-CAM to capture images, send them to an image detection API, and convert the labels back into text-to-speech audio output. It was my first time working at the intersection of hardware and software, so there was a steep learning curve in getting the embedded systems side right.

What I'm most proud of is that it wasn't just a cool technical challenge — it was built for a real client with a real need. That changed how I approached every design decision. My team and I sat with the client to understand her needs and plan out the constraints, which forced both creativity and a sense of respect for what we were building.

proudProject:
One project I am proud of is rebuilding an old donated server with my friends for our school’s computer science student union.

Some alumni had donated a server to us, but it was incomplete and not immediately usable. It was missing parts, we did not fully understand the hardware, and getting it to boot was much less straightforward than just plugging things in. We had to inspect the internals, figure out what components were missing or incompatible, source replacements, assemble everything carefully, and debug the system whenever it failed.

There were a lot of failures along the way. Parts that seemed like they should work did not. The machine blue-screened repeatedly. Sometimes we would think we had found the issue, only for a new one to appear. But each failure narrowed the search space. We kept testing different configurations, swapping parts, checking assumptions, and slowly building a better mental model of how the system fit together.

Eventually, we got the server running and used it to host a Minecraft server. That was not the most technically impressive use case in the world, but it made the project feel real. Something that had started as a pile of old, unreliable hardware became a working machine that people could actually use.

What I liked most about the project was the mindset it taught me. It forced me to be persistent, resourceful, and comfortable with messy systems where there is no clean tutorial to follow. I think about it through the idea of jugaad: finding a way to build something useful with whatever constraints and imperfect materials you have. That project made me more confident that even when a system keeps failing, there is usually a path forward if you keep testing, adapting, and refusing to give up too early.
chooseOneAnswer:
I would define creativity as the ability to give form to something that is hard to express directly: a feeling, memory, perspective, or intuition that ordinary language cannot fully capture. To me, art is valuable because it lets people communicate parts of themselves that would otherwise remain vague or invisible.

From that perspective, I do not think generative AI inherently diminishes human art. I think it can enable more people to express themselves, especially people who have ideas but may not have the technical skill, time, or resources to bring those ideas into a polished visual form. In that sense, AI can lower the barrier between imagination and expression.

At the same time, I understand why many people react negatively to AI-generated art today. A lot of what people see online feels repetitive, shallow, or made in poor taste. But I do not think that is the natural endpoint of the technology. I think it reflects the current “middle stage” of generative AI: many tools are built to be consumer-friendly, fast, and broadly appealing, so they often produce familiar-looking images instead of encouraging users to develop a more specific artistic vision.

The interesting future of AI art is not just generating pretty images on command. It is using AI as a medium: something people can experiment with, constrain, remix, and push against. I think there is a large unexplored space of art that becomes possible when people use these tools with real intention, not just as shortcuts. AI may make creation easier, but it does not replace taste, judgment, or the human need to say something meaningful.

proudProject: One project I am proud of is a pitch I did for my grade 10 English class. The task was to convince the audience (my classmates, who played the role of potential investors) to invest in a TV show I was proposing. I worked individually for this project, and my role was to present my idea in a convincing and engaging manner. The primary challenge I faced was bridging the space gap between me and my audience. I was presenting in a large room and the audience sat at the very back of it. I knew that if I really wanted to make my presentation engaging, if I really wanted to connect with my audience, my pitch would have to be more dynamic. So, I applied various techniques to bridge the space gap between me and my audience. These included making eye contact, using hand gestures directed towards my audience to simulate a more conversational atmosphere, and most of all moving around in the space I was in. I would step towards the audience, guide their attention across the room to a whiteboard by walking over, use the whiteboard as a resource to illustrate key numbers for my pitch, then take a couple steps towards my audience again and use my arms as well as my words to illustrate what I was saying. The space, in that scenario, was a resource that I used to illustrate points. By moving around in the large space, I was able to bridge that space gap with my audience by occasionally stepping towards them and physically lessening the distance. Furthermore, by using the whiteboard as a resource, it allowed me to draw my audience's attention towards something else that they could look at. Overall, this experience taught me the importance of using the resources around me. Since, prior to this presentation, I had only practiced in a small space, I had to adapt on the spot and work with the big space I had been provided with. Moving around, using various presentation techniques, and using the whiteboard was the strategy I employed to use the resources around me to make my pitch engaging.
chooseOneAnswer: A topic I could ramble on and on about is the incredible nature of the Turritopsis dohrnii (also known as the immortal jellyfish). What I find so interesting about this is not only this animal's mind bending capabilities, but also the potential human impacts and applications it could have. The Turritopsis dohrnii is a hydrozoan species that is able to revert back to an earlier stage of its life at a time when it feels threatened or gets injured, then grow up again. In theory, if it weren't for external factors such as predation and food supply, this hydrozoan would be able to revert to a younger age any time it needs, making it "immortal". That's the mind-bending part, because immortality seems like something only out of stories, but that other part I find really interesting is the human impacts it could have. I think that sometimes humans, as a species, get so wrapped up in their innovations, in the things they are able to build and expand, that they forget to observe the environment around them. I believe that is a mistake, because nature itself has evolved to become strong and durable, and if humans could look past their own innovations at the innovations of nature, we would be able to build incredible things aided by our knowledge of nature's structures. This is called biomimicry: when human problems are solved by mimicking the designs and systems found in nature. The Turritopsis dohrnii is my favourite example of this, because its ability to grow younger, to un-differentiate its differentiated cells, could have so many applications in medicine and other fields. Treatments for cancer and other diseases could be developed by observing the fascinating processes of the Turritopsis dohrnii.

proudProject:
Back in January this year, 3 friends and I won 3rd place at DeltaHacks with ReelJobs. The project was essentially a job board where you scroll job postings in the form of video shorts, and "liking" them automatically fills out the application using playwright. We scraped job postings from Greenhouse, voiced them with Family Guy or Spongebob characters, and added a background of Minecraft parkour or similar footage. 

I was responsible for the video upload from the FFmpeg pipeline to our object storage in DigitalOcean, streaming videos to the app that were semantically relevant to the user's profile and ensuring that the videos were served with that smooth, limitless scrolling feel. 

Since this was only my second ever hackathon, there were two very foundational lessons:

The first was to reinvent the wheel. My initial naive implementation of video streaming and scrolling couldn't even load a single video continuously without stuttering. I only started to make significant process once I did some research into how platforms like Instagram and Youtube pull it off so well. Even with majour improvements in proper video chunking and pre-loading the first few chunks of upcoming videos, reaching an acceptable state took the whole night. 

The second was to cushion spontaneity. Without a doubt, being able to think on the fly is one of the most important hackathon skills, but padding that quick decision-making with even just a bit of planning can drastically reduce risk while preserving speed. One hour before judging, our app was still serving videos from an old pipeline iteration. I traced the end-to-end video process to our object storage, determining that DigitalOcean must be serving old videos from some sort of CDN cache. My solution was to delete that storage instance and wire our pipeline to a new one, which I standby as a good solution, but I should've planned everything out better since I missed some parts of the rewiring process, bricking our entire pipeline. I was still trying different fixes in the line to the judging room. 

As a hail mary, I ran the pipeline one more time as we walked up to the judging table. As we presented, the videos generated, and miraculously, 30 seconds later when it was time to demo, everything worked perfectly.

proudProject:
One project I am especially proud of is HireON (https://hireon-jobs.vercel.app/), an AI-powered job tracking platform I built to solve a problem I was personally experiencing: job searching felt scattered, repetitive, and hard to manage. As a student applying for co-op and internship roles, I was constantly checking different job boards, saving links, rewriting information manually, and trying to decide which postings were actually worth prioritizing. I wanted to build something that made that process more organized and intelligent.

As a full-stack developer and product builder, I designed and built the application using Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Supabase/PostgreSQL, and the Gemini API. HireON aggregates job postings from multiple sources into a centralized dashboard, structures the data, and organizes applications through a six-stage pipeline. I also implemented AI-based relevance scoring, where Gemini scores job listings from 0–100 based on fit, helping users quickly identify stronger opportunities. Another feature I am proud of is the AI auto-fill flow, which extracts structured job information from career page URLs, reducing the need to manually copy details like title, company, location, salary, and description.

One of the biggest challenges was dealing with inconsistent job data. Every source formats information differently, and some career pages are much harder to parse than others. I had to think carefully about data normalization, fallback handling, and how to create a consistent user experience even when the original data was messy. Another challenge was designing the AI scoring in a way that felt useful rather than random. I learned that adding AI to a product is not just about calling an API; the value comes from giving it clear context, structured inputs, and a practical role in the user workflow.

I also had to pay attention to security and data ownership. Since each user has their own saved jobs and application pipeline, I implemented Supabase authentication and PostgreSQL Row Level Security so user data stays isolated. That made me think more seriously about backend design, access control, and building software that is not only functional but also reliable.

The biggest lesson I learned from HireON is that good software starts with a real problem. The technical stack matters, but what made this project meaningful was building something that directly addressed frustration I understood. It also taught me how important it is to balance product thinking with engineering decisions: the app needed to be useful, fast, secure, and simple enough for someone to actually use during a stressful job search. HireON made me more confident as a developer because it pushed me to connect frontend, backend, database design, AI integration, and user experience into one complete product.
chooseOneAnswer:
I would define creativity today as the ability to turn ideas, emotions, and perspectives into something meaningful, even when tools like generative AI are part of the process.

I don’t think AI completely diminishes the value of human art, but I do think it changes the creative process. Since I use AI and LLMs almost every day, I can clearly see how powerful they are for brainstorming, building, writing, and improving ideas. At the same time, I think there is a risk that people may rely on AI too much and skip the deeper thinking process that usually leads to truly original ideas.

Before generative AI, creativity often required people to struggle through many steps: imagining, experimenting, failing, revising, and finally creating something personal. Now, AI can sometimes take us straight to a polished result without forcing us to go through that process ourselves. That can be helpful, but it can also weaken our own creative muscles if we are not careful.

To me, AI is most valuable when it is used as a tool, not a replacement. It can help more people express themselves, especially those who may not have technical or artistic skills, but the human still needs to bring the purpose, taste, emotion, and direction. Creativity should still start with people; AI should help us expand it, not replace it.

A lot of people see it as a sport only for athletic or physically strong people, but I think the most interesting part is the problem-solving. Every route feels like a puzzle. That is what makes it so fun to me. It is not just about strength; it is about thinking, experimenting, failing, adjusting, and trying again. In that way, it feels very similar to programming. You face a problem, test different approaches, debug your mistakes, and eventually find the solution that works.

Anyone who enjoys solving problems should try bouldering at least once.

proudProject:
The project I am most proud of definitely has to be the game I made for my capstone project. I worked in a team of three people, and initially had no idea how we would approach making the game. It was a Rage based Golf Platformer called Golfing Over It, with a twist that allowed two players to play on the same screen without splitting it. This was achieved using DIY old school 3D glasses, and the process was called Anaglyph rendering. If one person was to wear the blue glasses, he would not be able to see the blue player, and vice versa for the player with the red glasses. And, that's not all. We programmed the rendering in a way such that, the red player can only pass through the Blue obstacles, and the blue player can only pass through red obstacles. All in all, making a ragebait yet fun couch coop game. It was well recieved by faculty and even got to be displayed at the Level Up Showcase in Toronto. 

As to how I contributed to this project. I was responsible for creating the player login system, connecting cloud based services like Azure Playfab, and architecting the entire UI/UX for the game. Be it the Main Menu, SinglePlayer Menu, Multiplayer Menu, Achievements Gallery, and the Player login Menu. Along with creating the achievements gallery, I was also responsible for creating each individual achievement, which meant adding custom parameters into the code that activated these achievements while also making sure that nothing breaks in the overall gameplay logic, which was also the biggest challenge I faced in this project. 

An important lesson I learned in all of this is that having a team which cares about the final product as much as you do, is very important. In order to achieve satisfying results, all team members need to have the same level of dedication, even if one person stalls the progress, the entire project can get hurt. 

So the lesson I learned is that choosing good teammates is very crucial.

proudProject: During my work at PatientCompanion, I was tasked with extracting information such as names, phone numbers, and positions from a document made entirely of screenshots. My role was to turn this unstructured set of images into structured and usable data. I had no prior experience with data extraction from images, so I started by researching different approaches and discovered Optical Character Recognition (OCR). I built a small prototype to test it on a single screenshot, but I quickly found that OCR struggled when dealing with large blocks of dense text. To improve the results, I refined my approach by breaking each screenshot into smaller sections and running OCR on each part separately. This significantly improved accuracy and made the extracted data more reliable. Through this project, I learned the importance of iterative testing, adapting quickly when something does not work, and breaking complex problems into smaller and more manageable steps.

proudProject:
The one I keep coming back to is VibeCheck, which I built at the IBM Z x Sheridan hackathon. It's an AI security scanner that you point at a GitHub repo, and it flags vulnerabilities and then actually opens one-click auto-fix PRs for you. I was the one who designed and built the two-tier scanning pipeline, where a fast Featherless AI model does the first pass and IBM watsonx.ai handles the deeper analysis, with the results streaming back live over SSE so you're not just staring at a spinner

The challenge that almost broke me was the streaming. Getting partial results to render in real time while two different model providers were doing work at different speeds meant a lot of wrestling with race conditions and half-formed responses. The other big one was just setting up the IBM Cloud stack under time pressure: watsonx.ai in Toronto, Watson NLU, object storage, Cloudant, all of it talking to each other before the deadline. The lesson that stuck was that a good demo lives or dies on perceived responsiveness. The actual scan time mattered way less than whether the user felt like something was happening. It ended up winning Best Cybersecurity & Trust, which felt great, but the thing I'm actually proud of is that it does something genuinely useful and not just impressive for 90 seconds on a stage.
chooseOneAnswer:
I think creativity has always been about taste and intent more than raw production, and AI just makes that more obvious. A paintbrush doesn't make you a painter, and a model that generates an image doesn't make you an artist either. What makes something art is the thousand small decisions about what to keep, what to cut, and why, and the point of view behind those choices. AI is incredibly good at giving you options and terrible at knowing which one matters.

So I don't think it diminishes human art. I think it raises the floor and the ceiling at the same time. The floor goes up because someone who could never afford an illustrator or never learned to code can now get an idea out of their head and into the world. The ceiling goes up because people with real taste can now iterate at a speed that used to be impossible. The honest risk isn't that AI replaces artists, it's that we drown in a flood of mediocre, frictionless output and stop valuing the human judgment underneath. I work with these tools every day as a developer, and my takeaway is that they're a force multiplier for people who already have something to say. They're not a substitute for having something to say.

proudProject:
The project I'm most proud of is Strata, an AI platform that automates property management for institutional real estate. I built the core system: a multi-agent setup on LangGraph where specialized agents handle things like resident messages, lease documents, and maintenance workflows, with a NestJS backend and Temporal running the async jobs underneath.

The hardest problem was state. With 10+ agents running at once, they kept stepping on each other's context, and pulling shared state from Postgres was too slow. I ended up building a separate state layer with Redis and SSE to sync execution context across agents, which got reads down to about 65ms. The other challenge was less technical: pitching it. We interviewed with YC and a16z Speedrun, and explaining a complex system to partners in a few minutes forced me to figure out what actually mattered about it.

The big lesson was that the demo is not the product. Getting agents to look smart in a demo took a weekend. Getting them to reliably respond to every resident message in under a minute took months of unglamorous work on retries, classification, and failure handling. I also learned that talking to real property managers early would have saved us from building features nobody asked for.
chooseOneAnswer: Teaching an AI to play Clash Royale. I built a system that runs 8 game emulators at once and trains a bot through self-play, and I can go on forever about it. Like how the bot's first "strategy" was placing every card in the corner because of a badly shaped reward function. Or how detecting troops on screen is harder than the actual strategy, so I trained a custom YOLO model just to see the board. Or how evolutionary training feels like raising hundreds of dumb children and only keeping the ones that win. Once I start explaining why the bot suddenly learned to wait instead of spending elixir instantly, it's hard to get me to stop.

proudProject:
One of the projects I have built that I am most proud of is Vera Physics, a free online physics teaching platform I developed for my school’s Physics Club, where I am an executive instructor. I built it to solve a problem I experienced when I was first learning physics: I struggled to find a free course that taught all the required topics. I often had to find different sources and videos for each topic and research what to study on my own. Now, after earning a silver medal and placing third nationally in the Canadian Young Physics Tournament, scoring a 5 in AP Physics 1, and serving as an executive trainer in my school’s physics club, I see the same problem in many students. I wanted to create a platform that was organized and taught physics in depth rather than a collection of random videos.

Using Flutter Web and Dart for the frontend, Firebase Authentication for login, and Cloudflare for fast video delivery, I built a system that organizes content by curriculum, breaks courses into precise units and subtopics, and hosts video lessons that I personally recorded and taught. As an assistant teacher at Spirit of Math and a ski instructor, I have experience teaching and breaking down concepts. I have also met with the Physics Department head of my school multiple times to discuss the structure of the curriculum and the videos to include.

One of the biggest technical challenges was curriculum alignment, since broader topics like modern physics contain subtopics that are not included in every course. This forced me to redesign the architecture to support course-specific filtering instead of a single static structure. Another obstacle was login, as the Firebase SDK version was not compatible with my project, so I had to use a REST API to create a custom environment. Vera Physics ultimately combines my experiences as both a programmer and a trainer, and it has reached over 1,000 unique users and handled more than 12,000 requests in 30 days through Cloudflare. It is a solution to a problem I once faced myself, which makes it the project I am most proud of.

proudProject:
A project I am proud of building was ShowUp.ai. ShowUp.ai lets you and your friends bet real money on each other, whether you show up to your classes or not. I personally always struggled with going to my lectures and ended up cramming for exams. Skipping lectures is easy because there is not immedite consequence. ShowUp.ai lets you bet against your friends before the start of the semster. Each time you skip a lecture, a small portion of the bet amount you put in goes towards your friend. At the end of the term, you get your remaining money back while your friend keeps anything that they recieved(vice-versa). 

My role in the project was centered around 2 things. The location tracking system and helping with UI. I integrated the Mapbox API to geocode classroom locations and implemented the Haversine formula to calculate whether a student was physically within 50 meters of their class. On the UI side, I built out the live analytics dashboard and the core attendance flow in Next.js and TypeScript.

proudProject:
Supply Match. It's a tool I built to help staff at a long-term care facility check medical supply orders against what they actually received. The idea came from my co-op at Mon Sheong, where I was doing nursing administrative support. I kept noticing how much of the supply verification happened by hand. Someone reads a list, then digs through boxes to confirm each item matches. It was slow and easy to get wrong, and the people doing it already had a hundred other things to handle. My family has spent a lot of time in and around long-term care, so the problem wasn't abstract to me. I wanted to fix it.
My role was everything on the build side. I scoped the problem, decided what the tool should do, and wrote it in React. The core idea was simple: show staff a picture of what each supply item should look like next to the order, so they can verify by sight instead of cross-referencing codes nobody has memorized.
The hardest part wasn't the framework. It was image loading. Getting the right product photos to show up reliably, at the right size, without breaking the layout or slowing the page down, turned into a real problem I'm still working through. The other challenge was less technical. I'm not a nurse. I had to keep checking whether the thing I was building matched how staff actually work, instead of how I imagined they work from a desk. A clean demo and a tool someone can use during a busy shift are not the same thing.
What I learned is that the boring part of a project is usually where the value is. Anyone can make the happy path work. Making it survive a real environment, with real interruptions and people who don't care how clever the code is, is the actual job. I also learned to build closer to the user. The best feedback I got didn't come from testing alone, it came from watching how people did the task.

proudProject:
A project I am proud of is Prompt Perfect, a real-time multiplayer game about prompt engineering that my team and I built for GDGHacks 2026. It was especially meaningful to me because it became my first category win, earning Best UI/UX, and it was only my second hackathon.

The idea behind Prompt Perfect was to make AI literacy more interactive and fun. Instead of learning prompt engineering through tutorials or lectures, players join a live lobby, look at a target image, and compete to write the best prompt to recreate it. The game then uses AI to judge how close each generated image is to the target, updates scores, and displays a live leaderboard.

My role focused heavily on the frontend, UI/UX design, gameplay flow, and real-time multiplayer experience. I helped make the interface smooth, responsive, and visually engaging so that the game felt polished rather than just functional. I also worked on syncing player readiness, prompt submissions, round results, and leaderboard updates so everyone in the lobby experienced the game at the same time.

One of the biggest challenges was handling the real-time multiplayer aspect. We had to make sure lobbies worked properly, game states updated instantly, and players did not get out of sync. Debugging under time pressure was difficult, especially late into the hackathon, but it taught me how important clear communication, quick iteration, and staying calm are when solving problems as a team.

Winning Best UI/UX made me proud because it showed that the details I cared about — layout, responsiveness, clarity, and user experience — actually mattered. More importantly, the project taught me that a good technical idea becomes much stronger when it is easy and enjoyable for people to use. Prompt Perfect helped me grow as a developer and gave me confidence that I can build creative, polished projects under pressure.

chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity is about intentional expression — what to say, how to say it, and why it matters
By that definition, AI doesn't replace creativity — it expands who gets to participate in it

AI enables more people to create:
Before generative AI, producing quality visuals or writing needed years of practice or money
A student from a Tier-2 city with a creative idea had no tools — now they do
That's democratisation of creativity, not destruction of it

The real concern:
Not whether AI can make art — it clearly can
The real fear is whether original human work will stop being valued
Whether designers, writers, artists will be replaced by a prompt

Why human creativity still wins:
AI produces the expected — statistically likely output for a given input
It struggles with the unexpected — the weird idea, the uncomfortable truth, the risk that pays off
That gap is exactly where human creativity lives and always will

Conclusion:
AI raises the floor of creative output — but doesn't lower the ceiling
The best creators will still be the best — just with better tools
People who had no voice before now have one — that's a win for human art overall

chooseOneAnswer: A topic I could ramble FOREVER about would be the art of producing (music). There is a nuance in the world of music production that not everyone knows exists. My favourite producing concept that is also tied to philosophy is the beauty of void—or in music the beauty of silence. There is a psychological auditory illusion called melodic implication where when you leave "space" (silence) in between chords, the listener's brain will automatically fill in the silence with their own interpreted notes (specifically, based on their music culture). This means that each and every listener is creating their own music in their head based on their experiences, while the sound waves are objectively the same to all. Similar to an open ended book that leaves the reader to imagine what happened in the aftermath. After learning this theory, one begins to realize that in classical music, silence occupies as much space as the notes themselves. What makes classical music a "classic" is the lack of music in the pieces.

proudProject:
One project I'm especially proud of is Sonata, a real-time gesture-controlled musical instrument that transforms hand movements into music using computer vision.

I've always been fascinated by both music production and technology, and Sonata was my attempt to combine those interests into a single project. Using Python, OpenCV, MediaPipe, and Pygame, I built a system that tracks hand landmarks through a webcam and maps different gestures to musical sounds in real time. The result is a touchless instrument that allows users to create music using only their hands.

The biggest challenge was achieving low-latency performance. Computer vision models can be computationally expensive, and even small delays between a gesture and the resulting sound make the experience feel unnatural. I spent a significant amount of time optimizing the video-processing pipeline and refining gesture recognition to ensure the system remained responsive during live use.

Through this project, I learned how important user experience is in technical systems. A model can be accurate, but if it doesn't feel intuitive or responsive, people won't enjoy using it. Sonata taught me how to bridge the gap between technical implementation and human interaction while giving me the opportunity to explore the intersection of engineering, creativity, and music.

proudProject:
In the first semester of my first year at university, for our engineering design project, we were tasked with using CAD software to create and 3D print a gripper mechanism that would be attached to a robotic arm and used to pick up a series of different objects.

There were different roles that we were given and each person had to select one role. My role was manager, meaning that my tasks involved organizing team meetings, creating an agenda before and after team meetings to figure out what we have to get done duirng the meeting and what we have to complete before the next meeting, respectively. I was also the main speaker during our weekly meetings with our TA to check on how we were doing.

A big challenge that we faced was prototyping early on. Since we were trying to nit pick and be as perfect as possible on our first attempt, we ended up coming up with a design and creating it really late into the project, which made testing and overall time management really hard. In the end since we could not test anything and prototype properly we ended up not being able to pick up any of the objects.

I learned the importance of just getting something done rather than focusing on making it perfect the first time. Like it's really important to create, test, reitrate, test again, make changes, and so on.

Although our final design did not work as intended, I am still proud of this project because it taught me a lot about teamwork, communication, and the engineering design process. As the manager, I learned how important it is to keep everyone organized and make sure that the team is making consistent progress. I also learned that being a good team member is not just about completing your own tasks, but also about helping the team identify problems early and finding solutions together.

This experience changed the way I approach future projects because I now understand the value of starting early, creating quick prototypes, and improving through multiple iterations. At GDG Hacks 3 @ UofG, our team decided to make a game despite having 0 experience. For the next 16 hours, we learned everything about game dev from the ground up, some of us getting 0 sleep. We then proceeded to miss the Devpost deadline by ONE MINUTE, and practically collapsed from happiness when a 10 minute extension was announced. Running on coffee, cold pizza and hope we ended up winning best game. 10/10 weekend, would do again. 

CodeyVillage is a gamified productivity app thats kind of like StarDew Valley for coders. You and your friends can build an island village through coins earned from doing real work : completed leetcode problems, github activity and job applications. We noticed many "game" productivity apps are solitary and unmotivating. Through a communal space, your friends can keep each other accountable and overcome grind sessions together. By building a communal island, the game become a living mural of your friend group's achievements that you can look back upon with pride.

I was the frontend developer and designer, and I learned so much about game dev using tools such as Phaser and Typescript. Multiplayer was very challenging to get working, and we spent hours wrestling with Web socket and server-side. The feeling of getting it working at 4 am was hard to describe. I gained a new appreciation for the commitment it takes to create a game, and how easily we can judge a new game as "bad" while it is the culmination of many years of work for a whole team of people. On top of getting experience with new tech stacks, our team relearn core software dev practices from the ground up to efficiently manage 4 developers at once with constant commits and merges. 

Our team is continuing to develop the game, hoping to launch a full beta by July. So far we've had over 100 players and we are so excited to get more.

proudProject:
The project I'm most proud of is ScheduleLynx, an instant scheduling application designed to help students manage their time through distributing tasks based on deadlines, availability, and recurring commitments.

As the lead frontend developer, I was responsible for designing and building the user interface. My work spanned the entire frontend development process, from creating initial UI/UX wireframes and prototypes to implementing a fully responsive dashboard and authentication system using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript within a component-based architecture.

The challenges came in two forms. The first was technical. My teammates and I were all relatively new to the languages and frameworks we were using, so syntax errors and debugging issues were a regular part of the development process. When we got stuck, we relied on a combination of guidance from our professor and teaching assistants, online resources such as documentation and developer forums, and a lot of collaborative problem-solving within the team. While it was frustrating at times, working through those challenges ourselves helped solidify our understanding of the technologies far more than simply reading about them.

The second challenge was learning how to design for real users rather than for myself. Early versions of the application made sense to me as a developer, but user testing quickly revealed that some interactions felt unintuitive or clustered. That feedback pushed me to revisit our approach, simplify the interface, and think carefully about how a busy or stressed student would navigate the app. Adopting that user-centered mindset fundamentally changed how I approach software development and taught me the importance of balancing technical functionality with usability.

What I’m most proud of is that ScheduleLynx wasn’t a solo project, it was the result of a collaboration with my colleagues. One of the biggest accomplishments was being able to make clear design decisions within a team environment, while also staying open to feedback and setting ego aside. As the project evolved, I worked to maintain a consistent user experience across the product. In the end, those collaboration and communication skills ended up being more rewarding and impactful than any technical challenges faced.

Claude responded: During Hack Canada, my team built Mathphic -> an AI that generates 3Blue1Brown-style math animations.During Hack Canada, my team built Mathphic -> an AI that generates 3Blue1Brown-style math animations. I built the rendering engine and a custom scripting language called Desq, so instead of having the LLM generate brittle code, it just speaks Desq and the frontend converts it into live animations through Desmos and MathJax.

Honestly the hardest part was just getting the LLM to output the right thing consistently. We went down so many crazy agentic rabbit holes that all fell apart. Turns out what worked was just giving Desq richer functions, tightening the instructions, and throwing in good examples. Constrain the problem before you over-engineer it.

Every single judge grilled us with "who is this for?" and we had nothing -> we made it because we loved math, not because it was a good pitch. We pretty much assumed it was all for nothing until one judge went overtime playing with it for half an hour. They felt so wrong not rewarding us that they created a 4th place award on the spot: Most Technically Advanced Hack.

Innovation isn't always appreciated -> but there's always someone out there willing to. Hence, I will never stop building, innovating upon the ideas in my head.

proudProject:
As a Neovim user, keyboard navigation is second nature to me, I rarely touch the mouse. When I started using Obsidian for note-taking, I immediately missed nvim-tree: a clean, keyboard-navigable file tree. Nothing like it existed for Obsidian, so I built one myself.

The result was obsidian-tree, a TypeScript plugin that adds a file navigator view to your vault with full keyboard navigation, smooth animations, and no mouse required. My role was everything, design, implementation, and publishing to the Obsidian community plugin registry.
The biggest challenge was understanding the Obsidian plugin API. It's loosely documented and somewhat opaque, so a lot of my early progress came from reading other plugins' source code and reverse-engineering how the internal component life cycle worked. Getting animations and state (expanded/collapsed folders) to feel native to Obsidian's UI, rather than bolted on, took more iteration than I expected.

The most meaningful thing I took away wasn't technical. When I built it for myself, "done" meant it worked for my workflow. But once real people started using it, I understood what done actually means: edge cases I'd never hit, vault structures I'd never imagined, and feedback that reshaped features I thought were finished. That shift, from building for yourself to building for others, changed how I approach every project since.
chooseOneAnswer:
Propositional logic. Give me a whiteboard and I will not stop.

Most people encounter it once in a math class, decide it's abstract nonsense, and never think about it again. I went the opposite direction.

What draws me in is the structure. A valid argument isn't just one that sounds right, it's one where the conclusion cannot be false if the premises are true. That's it. And once you internalize that definition, you start noticing how rarely people actually argue that way. After taking a basic proofs course, you learn that the argument "if it rains, the ground is wet; the ground is wet, therefore it rained" is invalid. However, this argument is used everywhere: in courtrooms, in headlines, in casual conversation. Formal logic gives you a scalpel for cutting through it.

The thing I love most is that the theory isn't separate from real life, it is real life, just dressed up in symbols. Vacuous truths, contrapositive reasoning, proof by contradiction, these aren't academic exercises. They're patterns of thought that show up whenever you're trying to figure out if someone's argument actually holds.

I got pulled into this rabbit hole through a logic puzzle I stumbled on, bought How to Prove It by Daniel Velleman, and worked through the whole book. That's a big part of what led me to study at Waterloo. I came for the puzzle, stayed for everything underneath it.

proudProject:
It's the middle of January. I'm scrolling on my phone as usual, slouched up in the arm chair of my university dorm. It was the normal 'Instagram reel break', something that had become part of my daily routine amidst the growing monotony of classes.

Then, it appeared. The mythical reel pull. The light at the end of the tunnel. The one that stopped the unstoppable doom scroll.

Five weeks later, I meet up with my 3 high school friends who I had all reached out to, having desperately pleaded with them for them to finally join me. As we strolled across the grassy McMaster University campus, reminiscing about our high school days and telling about our new stories, I thought to myself, that I had just built the perfect team.

This was not my first rodeo. Well, it almost wasn't. I wasn't someone who enjoyed coding very much, nor was I even someone in the tech field. However, just a few months before, I did my first ever "pseudo-hackathon", winning a research publication in the process. From there, I realized that, hackathons aren't just hangouts for people with questionable hygiene and scary passion for tech. Even more than that, it was the perfect excuse to devote entire days of time to a single project, with no distractions. And that caught my eye.

And so from there, I went on to win 1st at UWaterloo's HackRx, a pharmacy/healthcare-related hackathon with a $3000 prize, as well as place as a Runner-Up in the slightly more technical WesternU Developer Society SPARK, which also featured a healthcare theme.

Which leads us back here. The large doors to the Peter George Center for Learning whoosh in as my team and I strut in with an almost auraful confidence. It's the first day of McMaster MedSprint, a biomedical engineering hackathon with $2000 in prizes. We're greeted by the faces of countless bright-minded peers as well as a roundtable of who I presumed to be judges, before we take our seats and I perform my first ritual: devour all the snacks. I like to say that "you can't think rationally on an empty stomach (John Reith)", which also makes it all the more reasonable if our opponents are the ones with an empty stomach.

Just kidding. I would never do that at SummerHacks, I'm an ethical guy.

With Step 1 out of the way, we proceed to Step 2: sweet-talk the judges. Kidding, again. I nervously approach the judges and introduce myself, and throw in a few questions about the topic throughout our conversation (very subtly). The topic was to develop an innovation for limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, a grouping of rare genetic diseases that result in muscle degeneration and progressive weakness in the limbs. As a medical science student myself, I try to show off some of my knowledge and deepen the value of the discussion.

After gathering incredibly useful insight, I deliberate with the rest of my team and we begin the ideation phase... Operation BRAINSTORM.
chooseOneAnswer:
(Q1) I love hitting bird-cocks.

That's what I call the "ball" in badminton, a conception of the two names it goes by, 'birdie' and 'shuttlecock'.

As far as I can remember, we have always owned a badminton racket. From only being able to swing underhanded in kindergarten, to trying to get better in high school, gripping the handle of a racket and whipping it with explosive force has almost been second nature to me.

And there is something really addicting about hitting bird-cocks. You swing your arm, the head of your racket rips through the air, through the blinding ceiling lights, and BAM! The moment of contact sends shockwaves through the air, chills through your opponent's spine, and also goosebumps along your own skin as you crave that sound more and more.

I wasn't always able to land the perfect smash though. Like all things, I started off as a complete newbie to the sport. In Grade 8 (peak COVID period), when I couldn't bike to the recreation centre to play basketball or soccer, I decided it would finally be the time to pick up the old badminton racket in our garage that had primarily been used to swat flies. It was surprisingly effective at that, actually.

And so it began, the badminton training arc. I dragged along my younger brother every week as we went to the empty parking lot of our elementary school and whipped the bird-cock over and over again. We went on from noon to dusk, and frequently lost track of time between the endless back-and-forths. It was almost like playing catch, which if you know, is an INSANE dopamine loop. 

Eventually (and as COVID died down), we were able to form groups amongst the WeChat group chats and rent out empty school gyms that served no purpose after hours anyways. I bought my first "good" racket, which I was shocked to find could be that expensive. I got better at hitting a bird-cock as well, at least so that it wouldn't magically teleport behind me anymore as I looked around for where I "swear I hit it". 

Before I knew it, badminton had become less of a 'standout' activity and had become a regular part of my life. Every Saturday morning, badminton at the Rec. Every Sunday afternoon, badminton. Sometimes on Friday evenings, badminton at empty school gym. As Grade 11 came along, every MWF after school, badminton team training. 

I've always been a firm believer that sports can tell a lot about a person's personality, leadership traits, and problem-solving abilities. Especially for the "world's fastest sport", with a bird-cock that can travel faster than a Formula 1 car on impact, it can really refine your adaptability, reflexiveness, even collaboration if you're playing doubles.

From the first smash I landed in Grade 9 to the last time I played badminton just a few days ago, this sport has defined who I am today. Maybe we can play at SummerHacks? (running out of word count...you lied...)
summerHacksGoal:
(continued) Knee brace. Nice. Tongue-controlled? Cool. And AI-gait prediction + fall prevention? Whoa now, we're dangerously close to GPT-wrapper territory. But regardless, EPIC. As the most experienced member of the group, I pretty much came up with the idea myself. But ideation was hardly the hard part. Time for Step 3: cook.

What can I say, being grouped with 2 Waterloo Engineering students and a UofT Rotman student with several tech internships definitely has its benefits. The next few hours went by rapturously, with all of us clacking away at our keyboards while chatting about life, inhaling solder fumes while auto-scrolling reels on the big screen, and synchronously chomping on shawarma while stealing our friend's old retainers. And as the sun rose into the second day, I had felt a feeling unlike any other: the power of friendship.

But this was no time to relax. We scurried back from The Hub to the presentation site, carrying our 3D designed knee brace, our wire-infused retainers, and of course our heavy dark eyebags. The final hour had come, and we carried out our promise: we cooked. The finalist announcements came out, and we stepped up to the stage for our final presentation. Somewhere in the room, drowned out by the sound of applause, my teammates and I saw our past selves, all of our efforts and our struggles, smile back at us with satisfaction.

Step 4: What???

At the end, we were awarded the 3rd Place Overall Finish and 1st in Track 1, an accomplishment worth celebrating and being proud of. That is, if 1st and 2nd overall weren't COMPLETE GPT WRAPPERS? Everyone else in the room was certain that the teams awarded first and second had spent zero effort with their projects, both of them being completely web/app-based and made on... base44??? All of a sudden, I felt as if we had been robbed, unable to accept the fact that real effort could ever lose to mediocre prompt engineering.

But as I drove back in the same car as my teammates, singing along as we moved across the twilight skyline, I realized that I was focusing on the wrong thing. I probably wouldn't put this on my Medical School applications anyways. $250? That's more than 0. And while 1st and 2nd could walk out of this with a product in an oversaturated market, I gained not only new skills, industry connections with medical professionals, but also fond memories that I will never forget.
