proudProject:
One project I'm especially proud of is Quota, a GenAI-powered VS Code extension that my team built during DeltaHacks, where we ended up winning first place overall. The idea came from a problem I noticed while building AI applications: developers often add AI features quickly without realizing how expensive their API usage can become at scale. I wanted to create a tool that could help developers understand those costs before deploying. My role focused heavily on the backend architecture and AI integration. I worked on building a custom code analysis system using ASTs and Tree-Sitter to parse large codebases and identify costly AI usage patterns. I also helped develop a FastAPI backend that combined retrieval-augmented generation with vector search to provide architecture recommendations and cost optimization suggestions. The biggest challenge wasn't writing the code itself but it was dealing with ambiguity. There wasn't a clear blueprint for how to analyze arbitrary codebases, estimate AI costs, and then explain those findings in a way developers could actually use which is what we really wanted. We spent a lot of time testing different approaches, scrapping ideas that didn't work, and rapidly iterating during the hackathon.
What I learned from the experience is that great engineering is often less about having the perfect solution from the start and more about being willing to experiment, fail quickly, and continuously improve. I also learned how powerful collaboration can be when everyone brings different strengths to a project. Winning first place was exciting, but what made me most proud was seeing people use the product during our demo and immediately understand the value. It reinforced why I enjoy building software in the first place which is creating tools that solve real problems for real people!
chooseOneAnswer:
My answer to Q2:

I don't think generative AI has changed what creativity is. Instead, it has changed who gets to participate in it. To me, creativity is the ability to take ideas, experiences, and knowledge and combine them into something meaningful. Whether that's a painting, a piece of software, a business idea, or a scientific breakthrough, the value comes from the human behind it, the person deciding what problem is worth solving and what story is worth telling overall.
As someone who spends a lot of time building AI-powered applications, I've seen how AI lowers barriers that once prevented people from creating. A student with no programming experience can build a prototype. Someone with a great idea but limited artistic training can bring their vision to life. AI gives more people the ability to express themselves and experiment with ideas that might have otherwise stayed in their heads. That said, I don't believe AI replaces human creativity. The most important parts of creation which is curiosity, taste, judgment, empathy, and vision are still fundamentally human. AI can generate thousands of ideas, but it cannot decide which idea matters. It can write code, but it doesn't decide what should be built or why. In many ways, I see AI as similar to the calculator. Calculators didn't diminish mathematics but rather they allowed people to focus on more complex problems. Likewise, AI allows creators to spend less time on repetitive tasks and more time thinking, designing, and innovating. Rather than diminishing the value of human art, I believe AI expands access to creativity. The tools are becoming more powerful, but the imagination, intention, and purpose behind great work still come from people. That's what makes creativity valuable, regardless of the technology being used.

proudProject:
One project I’m really proud of is AURA, a hackathon project my team built after completely pivoting halfway through the event.

The hackathon ran from Friday night to Sunday afternoon, and our original plan did not go the way we expected. We spent most of Friday brainstorming and building around an idea, but by midnight, we realized it was not strong enough. Instead of forcing it, we decided to scrap everything and start over. That was definitely stressful because we had already lost a lot of time, and in a hackathon, every hour matters.

By around noon the next day, we landed on AURA: a product designed to help people better understand and manage their personal presence, communication, and confidence. My role was mainly on the product and business side. I helped shape the problem, define the user experience, decide which features actually mattered, and make sure our final pitch clearly explained why AURA was useful and different. I also helped keep the team focused when we had very little time left.

One of the biggest challenges was building under pressure while also staying realistic. After pivoting, we had under 24 hours to create something polished enough to demo. To make it harder, one of our teammates had to leave, so we were suddenly working with a smaller team. We had to cut features, prioritize the core experience, and focus on telling a clear story instead of trying to build everything.

That experience taught me a lot about execution. A good idea is important, but the ability to adapt quickly matters even more. We could have stuck with our original idea because we had already invested time into it, but being honest that it was not working gave us a much better outcome.

In the end, AURA won two tracks, which made the experience even more rewarding. I was proud because it showed that our team could stay calm, make hard decisions, and still build something strong under pressure. More than anything, it reminded me that constraints can actually lead to better creativity when a team is willing to move fast and trust each other.
chooseOneAnswer:
I would define creativity today as the ability to take ideas, tools, experiences, and perspectives, then combine them into something meaningful. Creativity is no longer just about who can draw the best, code the fastest, or produce something completely alone. It is about having a unique point of view and using whatever tools are available to bring that point of view to life.

Because of that, I do not think generative AI diminishes the value of human art or creativity. I think it changes where the value comes from. AI can generate text, images, music, code, and designs very quickly, but it still needs direction. The human is the one deciding what is worth making, what emotion it should carry, what problem it should solve, and what final version feels right.

To me, AI is similar to previous creative tools. Cameras did not destroy painting. Photoshop did not destroy photography. Canva did not destroy design. These tools changed the process and made creation more accessible, but they did not remove the need for taste, judgment, and originality. In the same way, AI gives more people the ability to express ideas they may not have had the technical skills to create before.

I have seen this in my own projects. At hackathons, AI can help generate code, brainstorm features, write copy, or speed up research. But it does not replace the hard parts: deciding what problem matters, understanding the user, making trade-offs, and building something people actually care about. Those parts still require human creativity.

I also think AI may make human creativity more valuable in some ways. As more AI-generated content appears online, people will care even more about authenticity, story, and intention. A technically perfect image or paragraph is not always meaningful. What makes something memorable is often the human reason behind it.

proudProject:
This is a project I am still currently working on with another developer.

Algorithms are absolutely everywhere, and it's easy to fall under their grasp. Hours can go by doomscrolling on Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, TikTok, etc., and it's really affecting me and a lot of my friends too. A lot of us have installed Beeper and similar apps to get rid of the reels on Instagram while still keeping messages, but it denies us access to the content we want to engage with and this doesn't solve the issue with YouTube. 

To combat this, I've come up with Qration, which is my take on RSS. RSS was the biggest way people on the internet could subscribe to various sources and get exactly what they want all the time until Google killed off their reader in the mid-2010s. Not many people my age know what RSS even was, and a lot of the readers that still exist today are obsolete, only for super technical users or clunky. Qration is targeted towards a general audience. You put in a link to a YouTube channel, a BlueSky account, some RSS feeds you want to subscribe to, and it just works. It's your feed, your rules, 100% free and 100% open source. It even works offline!

Some challenges I faced while making this was actually figuring out how we would go about this. The original idea was pretty much just an RSS reader but with a discovery page added to it. Then, we started thinking about how we would use it and what the best user experience would be. Taking inspiration from apps like Beeper and Grayjay, we came up with this idea. We pivoted a lot of times between when we started and today, and that is a lesson I've learned. Good ideas don't just come in a vacuum.

proudProject:
At a recent hackathon, my team and I set out to tackle a massive environmental and consumer issue: the barrier to repairing everyday hardware. Most people discard broken electronics because schematics are dense, diagnostic tools are inaccessible, and troubleshooting is highly non-linear. We built Reparo, an autonomous agentic system designed to turn messy, unstructured hardware documentation into an interactive, step-by-step diagnostic guide for consumers.
My primary responsibility was architecting the multi-step LLM reasoning engine and the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. The biggest hurdle wasn't just pulling text from a PDF manual; it was handling context and structure. Hardware troubleshooting is inherently conditional. For example, if a voltage reading is low, you go to step B; if it is completely dead, you go to step C. Standard vector search completely flattens these relationships, leading to dangerous or entirely unhelpful instructions.
To solve this, I bypassed simple vector lookups and implemented an agentic architecture with explicit state management. We parsed schematics into structured knowledge graphs, allowing the LLM to traverse documentation logically rather than guessing based on text similarity. When a user input a symptom, the agent didn't just throw an answer at them; it formulated a hypothesis, generated a targeted troubleshooting step, waited for feedback, and dynamically adjusted its path based on the result.
We pushed through 36 hours of continuous debugging, complex prompt engineering, and edge-case handling to deliver a working deployment that won first place in our track. This project reinforced a massive lesson for me about the reality of building with LLMs: raw generation is cheap, but deterministic execution is hard. True utility doesn't come from a single massive prompt; it comes from constraining the model within a robust architectural pipeline, managing state explicitly, and designing for the friction of real-world user interaction.
chooseOneAnswer:
Generative AI doesn’t diminish creativity; it shifts the bottleneck from mechanical execution to curation, conceptual synthesis, and systemic taste.
Historically, human creativity was tightly bound to technical gatekeeping. If you couldn't paint with precise physical technique, your visual concepts stayed locked in your head. Generative tools change that by making execution essentially free. Anyone can generate a high-fidelity image or a block of boilerplate code in seconds. Because of this, the baseline value of raw output has plummeted. True creativity today is defined by two things: the prompt, which is the starting insight, and the curation, which is the taste required to iterate and judge what is genuinely good.
AI tools don't have intrinsic intent. They operate on statistical correlations, meaning they excel at finding the average of human output. Left entirely to their own devices, they generate predictable, safe content. The modern creative act is using these tools as high-speed feedback loops. It allows a creator to test twenty distinct conceptual directions in an hour rather than spending weeks building just one.
Ultimately, AI democratizes expression by removing the friction of execution, allowing more people to bring complex ideas to life. However, it also raises the bar for what makes an idea truly distinct. When anyone can make something look polished, the only things that stand out are deep human insight, radical originality, and intentionality.

proudProject:
As a part of a school design course, my team built BARRY, a smart roof insulation system that alternates between an insulation layer and a heat-capturing layer to collect daytime heat for indoor heating at night. I was responsible for designing and implementing the software logic to control the motor that switched between these modes, aiming to maintain an internal temperature between 18-27 °C.

Initially, I developed a hysteresis-based design that relied on high and low thresholds for the external temperature, corrected by photoresistor data to account for daytime radiative heat transfer. I chose to defer setting the exact numerical thresholds until the integration phase. However, this led to a major challenge: when trying to establish those thresholds during integration, I discovered the logic was fundamentally flawed because it could not effectively account for seasonal differences or unseasonal weather variations. 

Then, instead of forcing a flawed approach to work, I restarted the logical design process and debated the challenges with my teammates to gain alternative perspectives. We pivoted our strategy, and I systematically redesigned the CircuitPython implementation so BARRY prioritized internal temperature to determine energy demand. The external temperature and photoresistor data were repurposed to assess energy supply, with the photoresistor determining day and night cycles.  

This rapid redesign taught me several critical lessons. I learned that documentation is a lifeline; preserving previous code via GitHub version control and leaving plain-text comments allowed me to easily salvage and adapt past logic into the final model. proudProject:
BrainLattice started as a mistake.
It was October 2025, the night before Hack the Valley, and I had a Math 135 midterm the coming Monday. I wanted to build something that would actually help me study instead of just generating a wall of text. The idea: take a PDF and turn it into a knowledge graph, so I could navigate concepts the way my brain actually works rather than reading linearly.
I overscoped it immediately. Knowledge graph, AI insights, audio podcast, study sheet, all in a 36-hour hackathon. The architecture was a mess and I burned through LLM tokens with no real plan. When a judge asked "why not just use NotebookLM?", I froze. I didn't have an answer because the project was too scattered to have one.
I shelved it. Four months later I rebuilt it from scratch.
This time I cut everything except the core: PDF in, interconnected Obsidian vault out. Each concept becomes a markdown note with working backlinks, and you can explore the whole thing as an interactive force graph before exporting. I also built a CLI so people could skip the web UI entirely.
The hardest part was dealing with scale. Generating notes for a 200-page textbook means a lot of LLM calls, and they kept timing out. I ended up offloading the work to async Lambda workers with a job queue, so the frontend could just poll for progress instead of waiting on a single slow request. Even then it was too expensive, so I rethought the extraction step: instead of dumping the whole document into the LLM to find concepts, I pulled headings first to build a concept list, then chunked and processed in batches. That brought token cost down a lot and made it noticeably faster.
The launch flopped. I posted the demo and got almost no views. But the people who did find it actually used it, and the CLI hit 300+ installs with basically no reach.
I'm proud of it mostly because of the gap between the two versions. The first one failed because I couldn't say no to features. The rebuild worked because I could.

https://brainlattice.rickytang.dev

proudProject: For WRO Future Engineers 2023, my teammate and I built a fully autonomous robot that could navigate a randomly generated obstacle course for three consecutive laps with zero human input. We ranked top 5 nationally in Canada which I’m proud of. I handled the software side, specifically the computer vision pipeline and the communication protocol between the Raspberry Pi (vision) and Arduino (motor control). I wrote the HSV masking logic that detected red and green pillars and translated their screen positions into steering corrections, and built the custom serial protocol so the Arduino could distinguish speed commands from turning commands. The constraints were brutal in the best way. One camera. One button. No room for lag. PID for wall-following and HSV masking for obstacle avoidance had to work together seamlessly, and tuning them was a constant balancing act. If a color threshold was off by even 5 points, the robot would clip a wall and fail the run. I learned that debugging is the work. I came in thinking the clever architecture was the hard part when I was proven wrong. The hard part was building the mental model to reason about why a robot behaves incorrectly at 2am when it worked fine an hour ago. That debugging process taught me more than anything else I've done.

proudProject:
Turning my family’s 1980s split‑level into a living smart home laboratory.

It started in grade 7 when my mom brought home a smart lightbulb from Costco. As the room lit up from the touch of my phone, I became obsessed. I was a kid with no money, so instead of buying $70 smart switches, I reverse engineered them. I taught myself to flash Tuya firmware, soldered $5 ESP8266 boards to thrift‑store wall plates, and wrote firmware and integrations that let every hallway light turn on before you realize.

I gutted our sprinkler timer, integrated weather APIs, and cut our water use by a third. I machined a “chair sensor” from a Coke can so my desk lamp turns on when I sit. I even motorized my bedroom door, repurposing an old RC car motor, so it responds to voice commands. Each project pushed me to learn something new: PCB design, RESTful backends, 3D modeling, low-power RF, even a bit of meteorology.

Most importantly, I didn’t build alone. I published my custom dashboard on GitHub, now with 800+ stars and running in close to 5000 homes. I joined open source, maintaining the official Switchmate integration for Home Assistant, supporting 500K–1M devices globally. The GitHub tickets became my crash course in async Bluetooth, Git, and Python. This all culminated at 15, when I was invited as the youngest guest ever onto the Home Assistant Podcast, meeting a global community that had mentored me from forum posts to PRs.

proudProject:
Dubbify (Real-Time AI Voice Translation)
My Role: Lead Full-Stack Developer

The Challenge: Achieving true "real-time" translation. Standard pipelines created a jarring 5-to-10-second delay when passing audio between transcription, translation, and voice synthesis APIs under strict hackathon constraints.

The Solution: I re-architected the Python backend to handle chunked streaming via WebSockets. Instead of processing full sentences, we streamed audio packets in micro-batches concurrently, dropping latency down to milliseconds and earning the "Best Project Built with ElevenLabs" award.

The Lesson: I learned how to aggressively optimize API payloads for speed and realized that preserving human identity (tone and emotion) is just as critical to UX as technical accuracy.

proudProject: One project I'm proud of is SeniCare, a daily health check-in app for seniors living alone that my team built at CtrlHackDel 2026 and that won Best Use of the Google Gemini API. The app lets a senior record a short video and have a natural voice conversation powered by Gemini, then combines facial analysis, contactless heart rate estimation, and their responses into a simple Green/Yellow/Red health result with clinician-friendly summaries. On the engineering side, I focused on the check-in workflow and owned the server-side validation that prevents incomplete or abandoned check-ins from being saved as complete, which mattered a lot in a health context where a half-finished check-in could give a clinician false confidence. Our biggest challenge was scope: we had an ambitious idea with a lot of moving parts and kept trying to perfect everything at once, until we decided to get one full path working end to end before layering on the harder features, which was the best call we made all weekend. My main takeaway was that a strong idea only matters if you can actually show it working, and I think we won the Gemini award because we used the API thoughtfully in a real flow rather than just describing its potential. I'm proud of SeniCare because it pushed me to build software that has to be reliable when it genuinely matters to someone.
chooseOneAnswer: I think creativity holds the same value today as it always has, and generative AI is simply a tool that lets people accomplish things they couldn't before. I don't believe AI diminishes human art any more than a paintbrush diminishes a painting. It's just an instrument used to reach an end goal, and the value still comes from the vision behind it. In that sense, AI genuinely does let more people express themselves freely, since someone without the technical skill to paint, compose, or code can now bring an idea to life. That said, I think over-dependence on AI strips away the human charm that makes art meaningful. proudProject:
You know a file exists somewhere, but was it the Slack thread from two weeks ago, that nested Notion page, or the random PNG dropped in a channel at midnight? At Hack Western 12, my team built komo as a unified search hub that lets you query across Slack and Notion in one shot and actually understand what you find, not just locate it. For heavier assets like PDFs and presentations, we download the files, run OCR, and feed the extracted text plus surrounding message context into a Gemini-powered summarization layer, so you get back the file and a concise explanation of when and why it mattered in your workspace. The frontend runs on React, Next.js, TypeScript, and Tailwind, while the backend uses Node.js and Express with a unified search endpoint that brought query times down by roughly 96%. We placed 3rd overall, and the moment "Onboarding slides" came back with both the file and a clean summary of how it was discussed in context was the moment komo felt like a real product.

chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity has always been a moving target. Every time a new tool arrives, whether it was the printing press, the camera, or the synthesizer, someone declared that the old form of expression was dead and that something essential had been lost. We are having the same conversation again with generative AI, and I think we are asking the wrong question when we frame it as AI versus human art. 

In the present day, I would define creativity less as the act of producing something from nothing and more as the act of making meaningful choices. A painter chooses what to emphasize, what to leave out, what feeling to chase. A writer chooses which truth to tell and which angle makes it land. Generative AI does not make those choices, it produces statistically plausible outputs based on patterns in human-made work. The person prompting, curating, editing, and deciding what is worth keeping is still the one doing the creative work. The tool just collapsed the distance between intention and artifact. 

That collapse is genuinely significant though, and I do not want to dismiss what changes. When the barrier to producing a polished image or a compelling paragraph drops to near zero, the signal-to-noise ratio in creative spaces gets harder to navigate. It becomes easier to flood a market with mediocre AI-generated content and harder for human creators to compete on volume or surface-level polish alone. That is a real economic and cultural pressure that especially affects illustrators, writers, and musicians who were already working in precarious conditions. Pretending that does not matter would be dishonest. 

The more honest question is not whether AI diminishes human art but who benefits and who gets hurt by its adoption, and whether the industry structures around creative work are adapting fairly. A tool that empowers a first-generation student to visualize their ideas and also undercuts a professional illustrator's livelihood is doing both things simultaneously, and both things are true. Where I land is this: creativity in the age of generative AI is defined by taste, judgment, and intentionality more than ever before. Anyone can generate, but not everyone can curate with meaning. The human contribution shifts upstream toward vision and downstream toward refinement, and the middle gets automated. That is uncomfortable and also full of possibility. The best creative work being made right now with AI is being made by people who understand both sides of that tension, and I think that will only become more true over time.

rambleTopic: The gap between how technology is designed and who it is actually designed for. I can go for hours on this. Most software implicitly assumes a very specific user: someone with full motor control, reliable internet, a large screen, and fluency in the dominant language of the interface. Every assumption that goes unquestioned quietly locks someone out. I got obsessed with this while building LookSmith at HackHarvard, a 3D modeling tool controlled entirely by facial movement, where I realized that the default input paradigm of mouse and keyboard is just a design choice that stuck around, not an inevitability. The same goes for search interfaces, onboarding flows, error messages, and notification systems. Small design decisions compound into an exclusionary product. I find it endlessly fascinating that empathy and engineering are not opposites, and that the most technically interesting problems often come from asking who this was not built for.

proudProject:
I programmed an autonomous mobile robot with a 2-DOF robotic arm capable of navigating, identifying, picking up, and scoring game pieces using computer vision. I integrated encoders, a 3-axis IMU, motor controllers, and an AprilTag vision system, combining their data to localize the robot within ~5 cm accuracy at full speed. The robot also used a custom swerve-drive system, where all four wheels steer independently for omnidirectional movement, and I built closed-loop control for both the drivetrain and arm to get it reliably scoring with sub-degree precision. I also handled electrical integration, CAN-bus networking, power management, and system debugging to make everything work together under competition conditions.

Qualified our team for the 2023 FRC World Championships in Texas for the first time in its 15-year history :)

Also ran a robotics programming bootcamp to teach 25 highschool students of all skill ranges how to code, and the fundamentals behind robotics engineering

proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of is SkinAI, an AI-powered skin lesion classification system designed to assist in the early detection of melanoma and other skin conditions. As a third-year Computer Science Engineering student interested in AI/ML and healthcare technology, I wanted to build a project that combined technical innovation with real-world impact.
I independently designed and developed the system using TensorFlow, Keras, Flask, and MobileNetV2. The goal was to create a web-based application that could analyze skin lesion images and provide predictions in real time. To improve transparency and trust in the model's decisions, I also incorporated explainable AI techniques that allow users to visualize which regions of an image influenced the prediction.
One of the biggest challenges I faced was working with medical image data. The dataset contained variations in image quality, lighting conditions, and lesion appearance, which affected model performance. I spent significant time preprocessing data, experimenting with augmentation techniques, and fine-tuning the MobileNetV2 architecture to improve accuracy and generalization. Another challenge was integrating the trained model with a Flask-based web application while maintaining efficient inference times.
Throughout the development process, I learned that building a successful AI solution involves much more than training a model. Data quality, user experience, deployment, and explainability are equally important. I also strengthened my debugging, research, and problem-solving skills by continuously testing and refining different approaches.
What makes this project meaningful to me is that it demonstrates how technology can be used to address real-world healthcare challenges. It reinforced my interest in applying artificial intelligence to create solutions that are both innovative and socially impactful.
This experience also motivated me to continue exploring AI and open-source development. Alongside my AI/ML internship at ReTechno Technologies LLP and my contributions to GirlScript Summer of Code (GSSoC), SkinAI has been one of the most valuable learning experiences in my journey as a developer. It taught me perseverance, adaptability, and the importance of building technology that people can trust and benefit from.
chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity today is not defined by the tools we use but by the ideas, intentions, and problem-solving abilities behind them. Generative AI has transformed the creative process, but I do not believe it diminishes the value of human creativity. Instead, it expands access to creative expression and enables people to bring ideas to life more efficiently.
Throughout history, new technologies have changed how humans create. Cameras did not replace painters, calculators did not replace mathematicians, and computers did not replace programmers. Similarly, generative AI is not replacing creativity—it is becoming another tool that creators can use to enhance their work.
What makes human creativity unique is context, emotion, personal experience, and critical thinking. AI can generate text, images, music, and code based on patterns it has learned, but it cannot truly understand human experiences or assign personal meaning to its creations. The vision, purpose, and decision-making behind a project still come from people.
As a Computer Science student, I regularly use AI tools to learn concepts, debug code, and explore new ideas. However, the most important part of the process remains identifying a problem, designing a solution, evaluating trade-offs, and refining the final result. AI helps accelerate execution, but human creativity guides direction.
I also believe generative AI has democratized creativity. People who may not have formal training in design, writing, or programming can now experiment with ideas and create projects they previously could not. This lowers barriers to entry and encourages innovation from a wider range of individuals and communities.
At the same time, responsible use of AI is essential. Creativity should not become a process of blindly accepting AI-generated outputs. Instead, creators should critically evaluate, modify, and improve those outputs. The most impactful innovations will likely come from collaboration between human creativity and artificial intelligence rather than from either working alone.
In my view, creativity in the age of AI is the ability to combine human imagination with powerful technological tools to solve problems, communicate ideas, and create meaningful experiences. The value of human art and innovation remains strong because technology can assist creation, but only people can provide purpose, empathy, and vision.

proudProject: Me and a team of 4 developers are building a pilot of a new way to learn using AI, partnering with UWaterloo. We want to create something that is not for cheating but that encourages learning. We feed relevant course materials into the AI using techniques such as BM25 and use prompt engineering to get the LLM to ask the student questions and otherwise prompt them in turn to think critically about the course materials. One challenge we faced was figuring out how to deal with hallucinations. While LLMs have really improved, we wanted to maintain accuracy. So along with using course materials in tool calls to create a solid basis for our LLM answers, we also added a prof correction functionality so professors can update and correct the chat answers. Another challenge was moving fast while keeping accuracy. At one point, we noticed that our website was very slow. We eventually found out that the Claude-generated code we'd pushed had an N+1 query, a classic database mistake that can really impact performance. It had looked logical, and it did work, so we'd overlooked it. From then on, we kept a running checklist of things to check our LLM code for, and we could even ask Claude to double check the items on the list. I learned about AI techniques, hosting apps and monitoring and making decisions with imperfect information. It's been really interesting and now we have students using our platform, and I'm curious to see what's next and how it goes!

proudProject:
REAPER AI is the one. I built it at GenAI Genesis 2026 with a team of four in under 24 hours, and it's the project that made me feel like I'd actually built something that mattered.
The idea came from a real frustration: REAPER is one of the most powerful DAWs in the world, but its learning curve stops a lot of producers before they ever make anything. We built REAPER AI to solve that. It's a natural language assistant that lives as a side panel inside REAPER. You describe what you want, and it builds you a full multi-track session: drums, bass, chords, melody, right instruments loaded, MIDI written, everything immediately editable. Every note is yours to move, delete, reshape. The AI scaffolds the session. The song is still entirely yours.
My role was lead on the AI pipeline and music theory engine. I integrated Gemini 2.5 Flash for composition logic, built 30+ direct REAPER function calls via an HTTP bridge, and designed the music theory layer handling voice leading, humanization, and key/BPM adherence. I also built the audio-to-MIDI transcription feature using Spotify's basic-pitch neural network, which lets you hum or play anything and have it transcribed directly into an editable MIDI track.
The hardest challenge was getting REAPER function calls to behave deterministically. LLMs are probabilistic; REAPER's scripting engine is not. Getting the model to produce code that executed correctly on the first call, every time, without crashing the session required tight few-shot prompt engineering and a carefully curated set of example scripts. We also had to build a real-time music theory validation layer from scratch, because unconstrained generation produced harmonically incoherent output.
What I learned: constraints are features. The best creative tools don't do everything, they do one thing so reliably that the user never has to think about it. Building for a professional user forces a level of precision you don't get building for a general audience. The producer still decides what they want the track to feel like, what key, what emotional arc, what's working. The creativity never left their hands.

proudProject:
I'm building Committr, a full-stack GitHub analytics dashboard that analyzes your code beyond GitHub's graph. It shows what you actually shipped by tracking lines added and deleted, language breakdown, PR velocity, and team collaboration patterns. It's built with Spring Boot, Angular 19, PostgreSQL, Redis, Github OAuth 2.0, and Jenkins CI/CD.

I started this project wanting to explore a real-world full-stack architecture while building something I'd actually use. The initial idea was a personal dashboard analyzing my commit patterns. But when I mentioned it to my other software engineer friends, they all wanted something similar. That's when I decided to turn this into a proper multi-user application.

Very early on I realized that GitHub's API gives raw commit data, but turning that into insights is expensive. If I queried raw commits on every dashboard load, I'd hit rate limits immediately and the performance would tank. So I built a scheduled ingestion pipeline that fetches GitHub data, normalizes it into a relational schema, and pre-aggregates everything into a snapshots table. Each week, the system calculates lines added/deleted per contributor, PR merge times, language distribution, and commit streaks. This way, the frontend queries a table that's already been computed, not raw commits.

I chose Spring Boot because the ecosystem is solid for this kind of work. I get Spring Data JPA for database operations, Spring Security for OAuth 2.0, and clean REST endpoints without boilerplate. PostgreSQL made sense because I needed a relational schema with proper indexing. The snapshots table is the performance bottleneck, so I indexed it on (repo_id, contributor_id, week_start). That one index changed query time from seconds to milliseconds.

Redis sits between the frontend and PostgreSQL for expensive aggregations. Badge renders and stats get cached with TTLs that align to when the scheduler runs. If someone embeds my badge in their README, that's a public request that could hit thousands of times. Redis means I'm not running a database query for every view.

The frontend is Angular 19 with standalone components, which means no module boilerplate. I route params directly to component inputs, use Signals instead of RxJS observables for state management, and let computed values handle filtering. The chart components are fully reusable and stateless. They just receive data through inputs and render. FilterService holds the global date range and contributor selection as signals, and everything else reacts to changes.

Jenkins automates deployment. Every push triggers a pipeline that builds the backend, runs tests, builds the frontend, then spins up Docker containers. CI/CD means I don't have to think about it. The whole point of building production-grade infrastructure is that it should disappear into the background.

A lot of this came from wanting to understand how these pieces fit together at scale. Building something that works taught me more than reading about these technologies would have.
chooseOneAnswer:
Generative AI may change the way we create, but it has not changed the fundamental definition of creativity. AI enables human creativity rather than diminishing it, because genuine creativity stems from taste, judgment, and lived experience that no model can replicate, because history has shown that tools like this expand creative fields rather than collapse them, and because AI is already generating entirely new creative disciplines that did not exist before. 

Creativity requires a lived experience, something AI simply does not have. Nobel Prize-winning CEO of Google DeepMind Demis Hassabis said at Davos earlier this year that today's AI systems are "nowhere near" human-level intelligence. This matters because the most compelling creative work comes from specific, personal experience. An artist deciding which colors to use to convey a specific emotion, or a writer choosing which detail carries the emotional weight of a scene, is drawing on something a model genuinely cannot access. A model trained on the internet has no childhood, no loss, no moment that changed how it sees the world. It can pattern-match toward something that resembles those things, but it cannot actually draw from them. The judgment required to fill a blank page with something true still belongs entirely to the person holding it.

Second, this exact debate about humans losing their creativity has happened numerous times before. From roughly 2005 to 2012, digital art made in Photoshop was regularly dismissed because people thought it wasn’t the artist who made it, it was the computer. Traditional artists felt their craft was under attack every time a digital piece won a competition. What actually happened was instead of shrinking the creative world, Photoshop expanded into publishing, film, medicine, advertising, and fine art simultaneously, and created entirely new roles like motion graphics, UX design, and digital illustration that had no real equivalent before. The tool changed what was possible, so the definition of craft expanded with it rather than collapsing. The fear around AI art today is nearly identical, and history suggests it will resolve the same way.

That leads me to my third point. Beyond preserving existing creative fields, AI is actively generating entirely new ones. Four of LinkedIn's top five fastest-growing job titles right now are AI-related, and AI/ML job postings surged 163% between 2024 and 2025, reaching 49,200 positions in the US alone. These are not just technical roles. Prompt engineering, fine-tuning models, building AI-native products, and designing human-AI workflows all require curiosity, judgment, and the ability to imagine something that does not exist yet; the same qualities that have always defined creative work. The skills are also evolving rather than disappearing, with roles requiring prompt engineering tripling between 2024 and 2026 even as the standalone job title shifted into higher-level positions like AI Engineer and Applied ML Engineer. The medium changed, but the underlying creative demand did not.

So in short, I think if history is any indication, AI is not the end of human creativity, it is probably just the next chapter.

proudProject:
TLDR: I became a human ML algorithm

I developed a surface electromyography (sEMG) control system for an award-winning bionic arm! The arm itself was entirely designed, modeled, 3d printed and assembled by me and my wonderful teammate (though he definitely deserves most of the credit for the hardware). My main contribution was translating signals received from the sEMGs to consistent and human-like movements for the servo motors controlling the arm.

In theory, surface electromyography is great for the purposes of a prosthetic device! No invasive, expensive surgeries; instead, just decorate yourself with some stickers. The goal was to increase general accessibility and affordability for limb differences. However, because sEMGs try to pick up muscle activations through layers of skin and fat, there come with a lot of noise and inconsistencies. We also had to account for the tradeoffs between latency and accuracy. To top it all off, the entire signal detection algorithm had to run on a raspberry pico pi, so that meant no machine learning or high compute developments.

I got to work. To do so, I essentially acted like a human machine learning algorithm. I hooked myself up to the sEMGs, flexing and unflexing repeatedly to record the data. Over and over again. 

It took hours of training data and visualizing them to find and track patterns. I essentially turned myself into a human machine learning algorithm: manually labeling data, identifying trends, filtering out noise, and fine-tuning the system. I translated those observations to a low effort (for the device. not me, unfortunately) algorithm, which was ultimately optimized to be both accurate and efficient.

I lost many MANY hours of sleep throughout the development of the project, but a spectator's story made it all worth it. She shared that her father, a veteran, had lost his arm and seeing our project gave her a sense of hope. That moment specifically motivates me to keep on building.

Fun Fact: The entire project was done w/o genAI or vibe coding!

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1expYN0jzU4nJX95PqanOqwrqL14t-vMC?usp=sharing
chooseOneAnswer:
2. TLDR: Creativity == Originality

I think creativity and originality have become synonymous. True originality (and creativity) is rare, but never scarce. There are an endless number of genuinely original ideas waiting to be discovered.

The problem is that humans and AI specifically tend to work by referencing the status quo. That's just how we're wired. We reference existing experiences, art, technologies, and ways of thinking. AI does this even more explicitly. It's literally built on patterns of what already exists.

But, I do believe AI has the ability to enable more creativity.

It can help people express ideas that would otherwise stay trapped in their heads, grounding abstract thoughts into something concrete. But it can also encourage people to accept the first reasonable answer rather than searching for a better one.

The most impactful innovations aren't the ones that optimize existing processes. They're the ones that change the fundamentals. AI is incredibly good at helping us explore what already exists, but that should only be used as a tool and not the basis for new creativity.

proudProject:
One project I'm particularly proud of is building a transformer language model. Inspired by wanting to truly understand how large language models work rather than just using them, I implemented every component from the ground up using PyTorch, building a custom multi-head attention mechanism, feed-forward network, layer normalization, and positional encoding rather than relying on high-level abstractions.

My role was everything: architect, implementer, and debugger. I built a Byte Pair Encoding tokenizer, custom multi-head attention with query/key/value matrices, and a complete GPT-style architecture with 12 transformer blocks, a 16,000 token vocabulary, and 512-dimensional embeddings, implementing both a custom version for learning and an optimized PyTorch version for training.

The biggest challenge was understanding the mathematics behind attention. Concepts like why we scale dot products by the square root of the head dimension, how residual connections prevent vanishing gradients across 12 layers, and why multiple attention heads learn fundamentally different linguistic patterns were things that only clicked once I implemented them line by line. When my custom attention produced unexpected outputs, debugging forced me to deeply understand tensor shapes and matrix multiplication in ways that no tutorial had achieved.

The deepest lesson I learned was that complexity emerges from simple operations repeated thoughtfully. The transformer's power comes entirely from matrix multiplications, softmax, and addition stacked carefully together. There is no magic, just math applied with intention. That realization changed how I approach complex systems: break them down, implement each piece until you truly understand it, then trust that the complexity will emerge naturally.
chooseOneAnswer:
One issue I constantly ran into was the lack of a proper poker bankroll tracker. Every app I tried was either too simple, missing key features like multi-platform balance tracking, or cluttered with things I didn't need. So I built my own iOS app called Veritas, designed specifically around how serious players actually think about their bankroll.

The process was entirely iterative. I'd identify a gap, build a solution, test it at the table, realize something felt off, and refine it. Features like a computed anchor-based balance formula and session verification with permanent field locking came directly from real frustrations I encountered while playing. The app is now on the App Store, built in SwiftUI with Core Data handling all the persistent storage.

What made this process interesting was how I built it. I used generative AI as a creative and technical collaborator throughout, describing what I wanted, evaluating what it produced, redirecting when it missed the mark, and pushing for better solutions when the first answer wasn't good enough. This gets to your second question about creativity and AI.

I think creativity today is less about producing output from nothing and more about vision, taste, and judgment. Anyone can generate code or art with AI, but knowing what to build, why it matters, and when something isn't good enough yet, that's still deeply human. AI didn't diminish my creativity in building Veritas. It amplified it, letting me move faster and focus on the decisions that actually required my judgment rather than getting stuck on syntax.

Human art and human-built software still carry something AI alone can't replicate: a specific person's perspective on a specific problem they actually lived. Veritas exists because I felt the frustration. AI just helped me build the solution faster.

proudProject:
Pedisense was the first time I had ever worked with hardware. Our idea to build pressure-sensing insoles with an ESP32 and ten force sensors and our team was completely clueless about where to start.

My role was everything on the hardware and firmware side. I figured out the circuit design, learned to solder on the fly during the hackathon, and wrote the embedded C++ firmware to read sensor data through an analog multiplexer and stream it over BLE in real time.

The hardest part was the BLE connection. The sensor data was not streaming reliably and we could not figure out why. This would have been stressful enough in a normal setting, but being at Princeton's hackathon, we had to go to New York. At the time, were at a Knicks game and had our laptops open in the stands SSH-ing into our server, running diagnostics while the crowd went crazy around us. We fixed it from there.

The lesson I took from it was that hardware is just software with less patience. The feedback loop is slower, the errors are less readable, and you cannot just restart and hope for the best. You have to understand what is actually happening at every layer. That forced me to think more carefully and systematically than I usually do in pure software projects.

Pedisense worked in the end. It was not pretty, but it streamed real data, triggered haptic feedback correctly, and did what we said it would do. For a first hardware project built in 36 hours, that felt amazing.

proudProject:
I built OptiMove, a tool that takes a photo of a physical chessboard and tells you the best move.
The idea came from my university chess club. During matches, spectators would crowd around the board, debate the best move, and sometimes pull out their phones to manually type the position into an engine. It was clunky. I looked for a tool that could just read the board for you, and nothing like it existed. So I built it.
That was a problem, because as a first year I'd never built anything end to end. I knew some Python and knew the basics of HTML, but that was it. I figured I'd learn as I went.
The hardest part was getting a computer to actually see the board. I spent weeks on OpenCV learning edge detection, perspective transforms, and grid extraction, and the real fight was handling variety: different board colors, piece sets, and lighting. I then trained a CNN in TensorFlow and ran dozens of failed experiments before hitting 98.5% accuracy. I still remember watching it correctly call every piece on the board for the first time. After that I integrated Stockfish to analyze the position and return the best move.
The biggest thing I learned was how to be okay with being stuck. There were a lot of moments where I had no idea what to do next, and the only way through was to keep reading, keep testing, and keep trying until something clicked. That feeling of pushing through the uncertainty and ending up with something that actually works is what made me fall in love with building.

A random topic I could ramble on and on about is the scientific, economic, and military history between 1920 and 1970.

Einstein had already published general relativity, and our entire understanding of the universe had shifted. Then discoveries started coming back to back: quantum mechanics was developed, the neutron was discovered, and fission became understood. The Great Depression hit, and discoveries kept pushing human knowledge forward anyway. Then war started accelerating everything, because suddenly there was urgency and money, and the brightest minds were all pointed at the same problems. Those discoveries then changed the course of the war itself, ending with a nuclear weapon built on a physics paper written less than a decade earlier, by a scientific goliath named Oppenheimer.

I love this topic because of the combination of circumstances. The economic pressure, the geopolitical tension, the scientific momentum due to Einstein's discovery, all of it stacking on top of each other in a way that will probably never happen again. Humanity progressed more in those 50 years than it had in the previous hundreds of thousands.

The 1927 Solvay Conference photo is so exciting to me as you can see the greatest minds of the century in the same photograph: Einstein, Bohr, Curie, Heisenberg, Schrödinger, and Dirac. Every single person in that image changed the world. That picture alone summarizes why I find this period so fascinating.

proudProject:
One project I'm particularly proud of is **"Empowering Engineers with AI Tools for Effective Communication."** The idea came from observing that many technically skilled students struggle to express their ideas confidently during interviews, presentations, and group discussions.

My role was to research, design, and build a framework that introduced students to AI-powered tools such as Grammarly, Yoodli, Tome, and other communication assistants. I explored how these tools could help improve writing, public speaking, presentation design, and overall professional communication. I was responsible for organizing the research, evaluating different AI platforms, and creating practical workflows that students could easily adopt.

One of the biggest challenges was balancing technology with authenticity. I didn't want the project to simply encourage people to "let AI do the work." Instead, I focused on showing how AI can act as a coach that helps people refine their own ideas and communication style. Another challenge was comparing multiple tools fairly and identifying where each one added unique value.

Through this project, I learned that good communication is just as important as technical knowledge. I also realized that the best use of AI is not replacing human creativity but amplifying it. Beyond the technical aspects, the project strengthened my research, critical thinking, and presentation skills, and it inspired me to continue building solutions that help people grow with technology rather than be intimidated by it.
chooseOneAnswer:
Option 2
To me, creativity today is no longer just about creating something from scratch—it's about asking interesting questions, making unexpected connections, and giving ideas meaning. Generative AI has changed *how* we create, but not *why* we create.

I don't think AI diminishes the value of human art. A paintbrush didn't make artists less creative, and cameras didn't end painting. Instead, they expanded what was possible. I see AI as another tool—one that can help people who may not have traditional artistic skills express ideas they couldn't before.

That said, AI can generate images, music, or text, but it doesn't have lived experiences, emotions, or personal memories. It cannot know what it feels like to miss someone, celebrate a small victory, or grow through failure. Those experiences are what make human creativity meaningful. AI can imitate patterns, but humans create stories behind those patterns.

What excites me most is that AI lowers the barrier to creation. A student with an idea can build a prototype, a writer can overcome creative blocks, and someone who has never drawn before can visualize their imagination. It makes creativity more accessible, not less valuable.

In the future, I think the most powerful creators won't be the ones competing against AI, but the ones learning how to collaborate with it. The originality will come not from the tool itself, but from the unique perspective of the person using it.

proudProject:
PoliTalk is a Chrome extension I built at SheHacks that checks short-form videos (TikTok, Instagram, YouTube) for political bias in real time. It records the video that's playing, transcribes the speech, gives a left- or right-wing bias score, and pulls up related news so you can get context on the topic. Built with React/TypeScript, Express, ElevenLabs, and Gemini.

While this was my second-ever hackathon, it felt like my first - during my actual first one, I couldn't figure out git and didn't end up committing anything. This was my teammates' actual first hackathons too, so I ended up leading the team, which felt strange considering how lost all of us were.

We hit our first problem early. We'd naively assumed we could scrape videos straight off TikTok and Instagram, but after spending a few hours fighting blobs and base64, we found ourselves stuck. So we pivoted: instead of scraping, the extension would simply record whatever was on the screen. It felt almost too simple, but it ended up saving us from spending hours building separate scrapers for Instagram and YouTube, since recording worked across all of them directly.

The other challenge was us. When everyone on a team is new to the sport, the team probably isn't going to win, and we were new to all of it, the coding and the hackathon itself. So when we got tired and stressed, it came out sideways, and we started taking it out on each other in small ways. It took a bit of huffing and puffing before we clocked what was happening: the friction was killing our productivity and, honestly, none of it was worth it. So we let it go and got back to work - and that shift was what carried us. 

We ended up winning Best Beginner Hack and placing as finalists, which I really do think came down to us fixing our attitude before it sank the project. Learning to catch that and reset as a team was probably more valuable than anything I picked up about the code.

proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of is LA ROUTE, a multimodal transportation app that my team and I built at LA Hacks. We wanted to tackle a problem that many people face in large cities. There is rarely a single “best” route. Some people care most about speed, others about cost, safety, or minimizing walking distance. We wanted to give users more control over those trade-offs rather than a generic recommendation.

My role focused on developing the routing system and integrating transportation data from multiple sources. We combined LA Metro GTFS data with OpenStreetMap data to build a transportation network that supported public transit, walking, biking, and rideshare options. We also incorporated crime data to generate safety-aware route recommendations.

The biggest challenge was bringing together multiple datasets and transportation modes into a single routing system. Different data sources had different formats and assumptions, and our early route recommendations were often inconsistent. With only a weekend to build the project, we had to quickly debug issues, refine our approach, and focus on delivering a reliable solution.

What I enjoyed most about this project was seeing how an idea that started as a conversation between teammates turned into something people could actually use. There were plenty of moments when things didn't go as we expected, but figuring out those problems together was one of the most rewarding parts of the experience. Seeing our project place in the Top 50 at LA Hacks was rewarding because it validated both the technical solution and the collaboration that made it possible.

proudProject:
“Welcome to this week’s Entrepreneurship Office Hours! Today, I’ll be covering how to conduct cold outreach.”
This was me every Saturday last summer summer, hosting workshops for high school girls across Toronto through CapitalHER. I co-founded this program two years ago to provide financial literacy and entrepreneurship resources to under-resourced communities, eventually securing a $100,000 grant to support over 140 participants over the past two years. But beyond the numbers, this experience challenged me to rethink my approach to leadership.
I was so excited for the launch after months of hard work, but we got into the first few workshops, and it was just... dead silence. I asked everyone to introduce themselves, and nobody wanted to volunteer. I asked a question, and no one raised their hands. It felt terrible because I didn't know how to connect with them.
My immediate reaction was to fix it by being stricter - picking on people to answer questions, pushing harder on deadlines, and enforcing rules. It completely backfired. It just made them more intimidated.
I realized I was totally misreading the situation because imagine being thrown into a completely new environment, dealing with complex concepts for the first time, you would also be overwhelmed and scared of giving the wrong answer.
So, I shifted. I started putting the girls into smaller groups and doing icebreaker activities so they could make friends in a more intimate setting. More importantly, I focused on building personal trust. I started staying late just to hang out and chat, checked in privately with anyone who seemed discouraged, and openly shared my own mistakes. By "learning in public" alongside them, I modeled that failure was just part of the process. Once I put in time to make those personal connections, everything changed. They started sharing these incredible ideas and reaching out to me for advice. It taught me that if people don't trust you as a human being first, nothing else matters.

proudProject:
SoundFire – IoT Wildfire Suppression System
When my team of six first proposed using sound waves to put out fires, we were met with a lot of skepticism, including from ourselves. It sounds counterintuitive, even a little absurd. But that tension between "this shouldn't work" and "let's make it work anyway" is exactly what made SoundFire one of the most rewarding projects I've been part of.
My role sat at the intersection of system design and hardware: I led the overall integration architecture and built the servo control system responsible for physically localizing and aiming at fire sources in real time. That meant I needed to understand every subsystem deeply enough to make them all talk to each other, the temperature, wind, and humidity sensors feeding into the fire detection logic, the PID control loop driving the dual-axis servos, and the LoRa wireless network coordinating across multiple devices. I was the connective tissue of the build.
The hardest part wasn't any single technical problem. it was doing all of this with six people simultaneously building in parallel. With a team that size, keeping everyone aligned on a single coherent vision while subsystems are evolving in real time is genuinely difficult. We had different skill levels across the team, hardware and software work that needed to stay in sync, and fast-moving parallel progress that made communication a constant challenge. On top of that, we had to produce thorough documentation throughout, not just for submission, but so that anyone on the team could pick up where someone else left off.
We solved it by treating integration as a first-class concern from day one. Rather than bolting everything together at the end, I pushed for us to define clear interfaces early:what data each subsystem would produce, in what format, at what rate so that integration became a matter of connection rather than negotiation. It slowed us down slightly upfront and saved us enormously later.
And then, at the end of it all: the sound waves actually put out a flame. That moment made every late night worth it. It was proof that a weird idea, executed rigorously by a team that trusted each other, can become something real.
The lesson I walked away with is threefold: design before you build, because integration debt compounds fast; hardware-software boundaries are where projects live or die, so own them explicitly; and no technical skill matters more than your ability to communicate clearly under pressure. SoundFire taught me all three at once.

i think creativity today has changed a lot because of tools like generative ai, but i don’t think it diminishes human creativity at all. if anything, i think it enables more people to express ideas they previously couldn’t bring to life on their own.

at the end of the day, i see ai less as a replacement for creativity and more as an amplifier for it.

proudProject: One project I'm especially proud of is a research paper I completed under the guidance of my economics professor, examining whether apartment amenities mediate the relationship between income and violent crime across U.S. cities. My role was to create an entire professional and academic research paper. I cleaned and merged over 30,000 observations from multiple public datasets (apartment listings, crime statistics, and Census income data) into a single working dataset, then turned that data into graphs, maps, and visualizations to make the patterns apparent. To fill a gap in the data, I also built a Python web scraper from scratch that pulled landlord safety ratings from a site with no public API, giving me an entirely new dataset to test as a proxy for landlord behavior. From there, I applied OLS regression, regression trees, bagging, and random forest models to test how amenities, income, and population density actually related to crime. The biggest challenge was the scraping itself: the site's data wasn't in the raw HTML, so I had to reverse-engineer its underlying API by inspecting network requests and dynamically fetching a build ID before I could even pull a single city's data. That process taught me persistence and resourcefulness. When my baseline model showed no relationship between amenities and crime, I didn't treat that as the end of the paper. I kept testing other specifications, interaction terms, regression trees, bagging, and random forest models, until a clear pattern emerged. Amenities didn't predict crime, but income and population density did. Pushing past that first dead end is what actually got me to a finding I could stand behind, rather than a paper that looked like it had nothing to say.

proudProject:
At the GenAI Genesis 2026 hackathon, my team and I built a project called InferOpt, and it is hands down the work I am most proud of. We ended up placing in the Top 10 out of over 270 teams. We noticed that as developers scale generative AI applications, API costs quickly become a massive roadblock. To fix this, we built a semantic caching service that matches incoming user queries to similar ones we have already answered, which successfully cut downstream API costs by 55% to 75%.

My main role was architecting the backend and wiring the infrastructure together. I set up an asynchronous Python server using FastAPI alongside an Express.js API gateway to manage request routing, handle the Redis cache resolution, and piece the responses together across our microservices. Because we wanted users to actually see the money they were saving, I built a real-time metrics dashboard using React and Tailwind, eventually deploying the entire stack using Docker and Nginx. To make sure the cache behaved exactly how we wanted and everything was solid under the hood, I backed the system up with thorough Pytest unit and integration tests.

The biggest challenge was definitely managing the asynchronous interactions between Redis and our routing services without slowing things down. We had to make sure the semantic matching logic was incredibly fast, if it added too much latency, it would completely defeat the purpose of caching in the first place. Getting that balance right was a massive learning curve that taught me so much about real-world system architecture and efficient data flow. It proved to me that building impactful AI tools isn't always about relying on the biggest models; sometimes, it is just about engineering smarter, more reliable infrastructure to support them.

When I architect pipelines that take unstructured data and dynamically generate polished corporate drafts, or when I integrate models to analyze real-time audio and video signals, I never see the AI replacing the human element. Instead, it handles the tedious formatting and heavy lifting, freeing people up to focus on the high-level, strategic decisions that actually require human ingenuity.

I firmly believe AI enables far more people to express themselves freely because it democratizes the baseline of execution. Someone with a brilliant vision but lacking the specific technical skills to write boilerplate code or design a pixel-perfect layout can now bring their ideas to life almost instantly. The true value of human creativity today lies in taste, direction, and knowing exactly which questions to ask. Generative AI is ultimately just a highly capable tool, but the human is still the one deciding what the canvas should look like and why it matters.

proudProject:
I went to HackCanada with a group of friends from our school but decided to work on a project solo while still having everyone help shape it throughout and until the end. It is a language learning browser extension that focuses on immersion and embeds itself into your day-to-day life without needing any excuses for you to learn a language, such as dopamine for instance. It works by carefully replacing words on any webpage the user is visiting, introducing them to the structure of the language while keeping track of their progress with passive and active assessment without friction. It is called GlossPlusOne as it heavily depends on Krashen's i+1 hypothesis for knowledge acquisition.

Many of the problems I faced while building this project came down to understanding the scope for the hackathon and then beyond. The idea sounded convincing while it was running around at the back of my mind. But as a person who's not an expert in linguistics, I had to make up use-case scenarios that were personal and resonated with my problems, and also receiving input from friends, other hackers, and mentors. With agentic coding tools, those ideas could be tested out very quickly and effectively.

At the end, the project was a success. I won second place at HackCanada, and got a chance to pursue the project further with the support from SPUR innovation centre and the mentorship they provide. Currently, I've brought up two friends to work toward putting the project in real user hands as co-founders.

The most important lesson I've learned is that hackathons are great places to test out any crazy idea and seek validation from friends, teammates, and judges, even if I have to work on it alone.

My role was everything. Architect, developer, data engineer, designer, researcher. I had never worked with PostGIS spatial queries or agent-based modelling at this scale before. The hardest moment was realizing midway through that my simulation was running in 45 minutes instead of 30 seconds — I spent three days profiling the bottleneck before finding that a single unindexed spatial join was the culprit. Fixing it taught me more about database performance than any course had.


The lesson I carry from CalgaryPulse is that the most paralyzing part of any ambitious project is not the technical difficulty — it is convincing yourself that you are allowed to build something this big. I am a student in Fredericton, a small city, with no team and no senior engineer to ask. I had to decide to build it anyway and figure it out as I went. That decision changed how I approach every project I touch now. I do not wait until I feel ready. I start and I figure it out.
chooseOneAnswer:
> I think creativity in the age of generative AI is actually more demanding than it was before, not less. Anyone can now generate a song, a painting, a piece of code. The tools are free, fast, and genuinely impressive. But that means the bottleneck has shifted entirely to the idea itself. Execution used to be the hard part. Now the hard part is having something original enough to be worth executing.

> My own experience bears this out. I am currently building four original projects simultaneously — KALI, a ternary LLM inference runtime in C where weights are just {-1, 0, +1} and matrix multiplication becomes addition and subtraction; Black Cat Radio, a universal emotional resonance engine that finds the exact fictional moment across anime, film, books, and music that matches what you are going through right now; Freddy Trails, an AI storytelling engine that turns a walk through Fredericton into an interactive narrative with QR-unlocked location stories; and NAATAK, a live AI reality show where agents modeled on real celebrity personalities react to actual breaking news 24/7 and viewers vote to evict them weekly. None of these came from a prompt. They came from noticing something that did not exist and deciding to build it.

> AI did not give me these ideas. It helped me build them faster. And I think that is the honest answer to the question — generative AI lowers the cost of execution so dramatically that it exposes exactly who actually has original ideas and who was relying on execution difficulty to disguise the fact that they did not. It does not diminish human creativity. It just makes the creative gap between people more visible than it has ever been.

rambleTopic: Ternary logic and why it should have won. Most people do not know that before binary became the standard, there was a Soviet ternary computer called Setun built in 1958 that ran on three states — negative one, zero, and positive one. It was more efficient and more elegant than its binary contemporaries. It lost not because it was worse but because the industry standardized around binary and momentum did the rest. I find this slightly tragic. I am currently building KALI, a ternary LLM inference runtime in C, partly because of this history — weights stored as {-1, 0, +1}, matrix multiplication replaced entirely by addition and subtraction, running on a Raspberry Pi with no GPU. The idea that the entire trajectory of modern computing might have looked completely different if one architectural decision had gone the other way keeps me up at night in the best way
bearOrMuffin: Bear with sharks for arms, no hesitation. At least that fight has a defined endpoint and I respect the chaos of it. The muffin-baby situation has no resolution — it is a permanent cognitive crisis that follows you into every bakery, every nursery, every brunch for the rest of your life. Imagine the legal liability alone. Imagine explaining to someone why you just tried to butter their child. The bear is a one-time catastrophic event. The muffin-baby confusion is a slow, socially catastrophic unraveling that compounds daily. Also I feel like fighting a bear with sharks for arms is objectively the more interesting story to tell later. Nobody wants to hear about the time you had an epistemological crisis at a bake sale. Everyone wants to hear about the shark-armed bear. I would lose but I would lose memorably and that feels very on brand for me.

A project of mine that I am proud of is a personal Roblox game that I developed to play with my friends and family called Drinkrena. I was the sole developer, designer, and modeler for the 3-D game developed in Roblox, doing anything and everything. 

The game was really simple and it was one of my first projects, looking back it wasn't the best thing I could've made, far from it. However, during that one project, I was faced with many set backs and learned so much.

When I started making the game, I had no idea how any of it worked; I just saw an Instagram reel and got interested in game development with Roblox. Here, I faced my first challenge, being overwhelmed by all the terms and buzzwords and not understanding any of it. The way I overcame this one was by avoiding taking it all in at once and following simple guides or tutorials that guided me in getting set up, eventually, I was able to get all the necessary tools and programs on my computer to begin actual development

So following instinct, I began following tutorials I found online, but I was stuck in a weird place where they were either too simple to show satisfying results, too complex for me to follow, or not actual tutorials but instead videos made for entertainment. 

Quickly getting distracted I faced another challenge, that being having so many ideas and so much inspiration that taking small steps made me feel like I was wasting time. I realized that I had so many ideas that I wanted to make real but no actual experience or ability to bring it to life. Realizing this, I had to limit my scope, forcing myself to break down features into a roadmap of what they actually are and slowly building up them in each step.

Eventually I was able to create a functional game with some cool features and a very very simple gameplay loop. The game was dumb and barely worked, being "spaghettied" together by all the random things I had the ability to add and remnants of things I wasn't able to fully add. Despite this, I still published the game and got my younger family to play the game. 

Once we all loaded up into the game through Roblox we played it and discovered all the features and unintentional bugs that I never intended to add but were still really cool. This taught me another valuable lesson. A single person will never make a perfect project, users are bound to break something and being alone will always let some things slip by you or have a weird outcome and viewpoint that you would have never seen on your own.

Overall, despite the project being quite simple, I'm proud that it was the first program that I had ever run on multiple devices at the same time (even though Roblox made it super easy to do).
