proudProject:
A project I'm really proud of is Vit. Basically, it was a DaVinci Resolve plugin that acted like Git for video editing, allowing multiple people to make different changes to a video like color grading, clipping, and subtitles all simultaneously. My friends and I originally came up with the idea because one of us really enjoyed video editing and so we decided to build it out at GenAI Genesis. Unfortunately however, even though we toiled away, we didn't end up winning (possibly because our product had 0 AI in it 😭). Despite this, the reason I'm proud of the project is because we continued working on the project after the hackathon, and upon dropping a release video on x, we actually got 2 million+ views and hundreds of people telling us how much they enjoyed using the project and had wanted something like it.

While working on the project, I assumed multiple roles, staying flexible as we approached different challenges. For instance, at the start, I brainstormed how we would actually track all the different changes, suggesting that we could use json metadata to track it. Moreover, I was responsible for a large part of the technical implementation, which came with its own fair share of challenges. One main challenge that we faced was that the DaVinci Resolve API was not well maintained and out of date, meaning that a lot of the API interface they provided didn't work. Specifically, we struggled with tracking color because the API didn't provide a good method of doing so. In fact, this was such a large challenge that we weren't even able to get it working by the end of the hackathon, and only by continuing to try workarounds did we get color saving working afterwards. This project taught me the importance of building and working on things that you enjoy, rather than things you think are impressive. We took on this project because of a personal passion and because we all thought it was cool, and this allowed us to continue working on it afterwards and persevere despite minimal visibility into alternatives to API problems. If we had simply been building the project as resume padder, I don't think we could have even made a successful finished product. But, overall, I'm glad that I got to work on a really cool project that thousands actually downloaded and integrated into their workflows!
chooseOneAnswer:
2. To be honest, I don't think that the definition of creativity has changed since the past till present day, even with the advent of generative AI. Rather, I think that creativity is a concept of its own, with AI simply being a vessel with which you can display creativity. 

To give a specific definition, I would say creativity is "the desire to explore". This is a very vague definition, I will admit, but what this means to me is that creativity shouldn't be bounded by your ability to come up with something new or to design something that people have not seen before. Rather, so long as you wish to push yourself and do something, regardless of what that is, I think that counts as being creative. So, for example, I think that even a task as simple as reading a book can be creative. When I read webtoons, I'll often have my mind wander, and I'll think of possible future plots or backstories for side characters. I don't think this is some innate talent that only some people have, but rather that its just something that happens and it counts as creativity. In other words, so long as someone has a desire, whether that's the desire to read a book or paint a picture, I think that they can be creative.

Now as for how AI fits into this, I don't think it does. Or rather, I think it fits into a separate category. To be precise, I know that a lot of people like to debate if AI art for example is real art, but I don't think this really makes sense. To me, it's like taking an apple and trying to compare it to oranges. Rather, I think AI provides you with ways to be creative, but that doesn't make it any more or less valid than traditionally perceived creative activities like drawing or music. I think AI stands alongside human art, showcasing another form of human creativity without detracting from the value. If I want to make a cool video for example, I think that using AI to help me edit quicker is a perfectly valid approach. But, if I showed it to other people, I wouldn't tout or brag that I made something better than a human could edit or that it would replace video editing. Rather, I would simply think that I made something cool and want to share with them the new thing I had created. In other words, I would have a desire to share with others a cool thing I had explored. And so, I think AI provides people with so much more freedom to express themselves freely, because it simply gives them other avenues of expression, rather than killing off current creative means.

proudProject:
A project I'm very proud of would be CUThere, a centralized events platform for Carleton students to always find the best events happening on campus. I went into this thinking the hardest part would be building the product, but I quickly realized that creating a product is a lot more than just programming or vibe coding with Claude Code. The design decisions, constant iterations, feedback, and figuring out how to actually get people to care about what I built ended up being just as important.

One of the first challenges I faced was realizing that instead of building what I personally like, I had to actually understand how students discover events and what would make them care enough to use another platform. I taught myself Figma and went through many different versions of the design, constantly asking for feedback from my cofounder, friends, and even strangers studying in the library. Often, the things I thought were obvious weren't obvious to anyone else, and some of the features I spent the most time on weren't even the things people cared about. Learning to separate my personal preferences from what users are actually attracted to was probably one of the biggest lessons I took away.

Another part I didn't expect was how much work goes into getting people to actually use something. My cofounder and I jokingly made one of our friends the "ABG CMO" of CUThere, but somehow the joke turned into a very real lesson in marketing and community building. We reached out in Carleton Discord servers, talked to clubs, and tried to figure out how to get our product in front of the people it was meant for. I found that the hardest part was getting people interested after the product existed. Building CUThere was exciting, but getting students to trust it, try it, and keep using it was a whole different challenge.

I also had the opportunity to demo CUThere to funders from Carleton and Shopify's Builder Sundays. Getting to show something I built to other students, builders, and professionals was both exciting and lowkey nerve wracking because suddenly every decision I made was being tested. The feedback I got from those conversations helped me rethink parts of the project and reminded me that building in public is a lot more valuable than trying to perfect something alone.

CUThere taught me that creating something useful is a lot less about having the perfect idea and a lot more about being willing to listen, iterate, and keep improving. The best parts of the project weren't just the lines of code I wrote, but the conversations I had and the process of turning a random idea into something real.

proudProject:
One project I'm particularly proud of is a gut microbiome disease classifier I built with a team of four at a DataQuest Hackathon. The goal was to classify patients into one of three groups (Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, or healthy) using microbiome sample data from the GMrepo database.

My role was leading the data preprocessing and model development. We were working with over 2,000 features across 2,105 samples, which meant the data was high-dimensional, sparse, and imbalanced, making it a genuinely hard classification problem. I applied L1-regularized logistic regression for feature selection to cut through the noise before training an XGBoost model, and handled batch effects and class imbalance as part of the pipeline.
The biggest challenge was the time constraint. We had to make fast decisions about which techniques to prioritize and when to stop tuning. I learned a lot about scoping down a problem quickly and trusting the process even when early results looked messy. We ended up hitting 82% accuracy and 93% AUC, which I was really proud of given the complexity of the data and the timeline.

What stuck with me most was how much the CS and biology intersection opened up. You could see real diagnostic potential in the approach, and that made the technical work feel meaningful beyond just the competition.
chooseOneAnswer:
1. What is a random topic you could ramble on and on about?
Socioeconomic inequality. I could genuinely talk about this forever. What gets me is how much of where you end up in life is determined before you ever make a single choice. The neighbourhood you grow up in shapes the schools you attend, which shapes the opportunities you can access, which shapes your income, which shapes the neighbourhood your kids grow up in. The cycle is so well documented and yet somehow still treated like a series of individual failures rather than a structural pattern.

I find the data side of it particularly fascinating. Things like the relationship between zip code and life expectancy, or how upward mobility rates in the US have been declining for decades while the narrative of meritocracy has somehow gotten louder. There is research showing that a child born into the bottom income quintile in Canada has roughly a 1 in 3 chance of making it to the top two quintiles, and people will debate endlessly about whether that number reflects effort or circumstance, when the more interesting question is why the number looks so different depending on your postal code.

And that number does not hit everyone the same way. When you layer in race, gender, immigration status, or disability, the picture gets a lot more complicated and a lot more honest. A first generation immigrant woman of colour is not just navigating one system that was not built for her, she is navigating several at once, and the compounding effect of that is something aggregate statistics tend to flatten out completely.

I also think about how technology intersects with all of this, which is probably why it follows me into my work. AI and automation have real potential to widen those gaps if the people building the tools are not thinking carefully about who benefits and who gets left out.
summerHacksGoal:
The ideal time looks like landing on an idea I actually care about and spending the first few hours doing what most people skip: actually scoping it. Mapping out what we are building, what the MVP looks like, how we are splitting the work, and what we are cutting if time runs out. I want to go into the build with a real plan, not just vibes and a GitHub repo.

From there I want to watch that plan turn into something real. Picking the stack deliberately, moving fast but not recklessly, and checking in as a team as things shift. There is a version of a hackathon where everyone is heads down on their own piece and nobody talks until the demo. That is not what I am looking for. I want the kind of collaboration where we are constantly pulling each other forward.

What makes that even better is being surrounded by people who have that same fire for their own ideas. Seeing someone else light up when they talk about what they are building out of a room is one of the most motivating things I know. That energy is contagious and it makes you push harder on your own work just from being around it.

More than anything I want SummerHacks to feel like the kind of environment where you genuinely believe you can pull off something ambitious in a weekend. Where the outdoor setting strips away the noise, the timeline feels tight but not impossible, and the people around you make the whole thing feel like more than a competition.

I would leave feeling like I built something I am proud of, executed on a real plan, and came away with at least one idea still worth chasing.

proudProject:
A few months ago I cold-emailed Kim Knight, one of the most well-known Cisco Packet Tracer developers in the community, just hoping she'd point me in the right direction. That email turned into cisco-pt-mcp, an open-source MCP server that lets AI clients like Claude, Cursor, and VS Code actually control a running Packet Tracer instance.

I built everything: the extension that runs inside Packet Tracer, the Python MCP server, the PyPI package, the docs. The hardest part was wrestling with Packet Tracer's scripting environment. It's a legacy system that was never designed for this. A lot of what I needed wasn't in any docs. I just had to figure it out.

What I learned is that shipping something real is a completely different problem than getting it to work. Making it installable in five minutes for someone who isn't me took almost as long as building the thing itself. I also learned that building for systems people already use, even old ones that everyone's written off, is often more impactful than chasing whatever's trending. Millions of students still use Packet Tracer. Nobody was building for them. That gap was worth filling.
chooseOneAnswer:
I think creativity used to be partially defined by friction. Learning to draw, code, write — the barrier was real. A lot of ideas never made it into the world because the person who had them couldn't get past the technical part. AI is dissolving that barrier, and I think that's mostly good.

It doesn't make human art less valuable. What it does is shift where the value lives. If anyone can generate something visually stunning with a prompt, then what becomes interesting is the why. The intent, the perspective, the question that started it. That part is still human.

I see this in my own work. cisco-pt-mcp wouldn't exist in its current form without AI helping me work through edge cases and documentation at 2am. But the original idea, what if an AI client could just control Packet Tracer directly, came from me noticing a gap nobody else was filling. The creativity was in the question, not the execution.

AI gives more people the ability to realize ideas they've always had. That's not a threat to human creativity. That's just more humans getting to be creative.

proudProject:
One project I'm proud of came from a fusion energy hackathon (or datathon, the line was honestly pretty blurry) where our five-person team had to design an optimized geometry for a quasi-axisymmetric (QA) stellarator for producing the medical isotope Mo-99. It was different from the competition I'd done before, because rather than building something from the ground up, the entire challenge was about optimizing something that had been done before, and squeezing the best possible result out of it.

What was also different was the scope of knowledge we had to absorb in a very short time. We were not only given a brief lecture on the physics, but we also had to wrap our heads around the business case and data considerations, since the final deliverable was basically an investor pitch. We basically had to think like startup founders and make a compelling argument for why our stellarator design was worth funding over everyone else's.

My role spanned both the technical and presentation sides. On the technical side, I was involved in running geometry iterations, sweeping across different parameter dimensions to try to maximize the optimization gradient. I had to leave the business and physics case to the rest of my teammates, who handled it very well (no challenges here).

The technical challenges, however, were a lot. The organizers didn't release the promised GitHub code and supporting documentation until the night before the competition, which meant we had to draft a rough sketch of anything without prior guidance and had to edit retroactively once it was released. Computationally, we were running our iterations on a Mac without a proper GPU, which made each sweep painfully slow and limited how many configurations we could realistically test. And on top of all that, keeping a five-person team aligned and productive took more active coordination than expected.

In the end, our stellarator was, for lack of words, mid. It was neither the most optimized nor the cheapest, but fell in the middle of the graph they used to grade it. We placed second, which initially felt like a bit of a surprise given how "average" our solution seemed on paper. However, looking at the other submissions, it made sense as some teams had pushed so hard on optimization that their cost estimates exponentially rose into thousands of trillions of dollars, while others cut costs so aggressively that their designs could barely produce any isotopes at all. Being “mid:, as it turns out, was actually a defensible position.

I took away two main lessons. The first: I should have switched to my teammate's computer (with a separate GPU) much earlier. The time we lost to slow iteration cycles genuinely cost us a lot. The second: even a middle-of-the-road solution can win if you position it well. Knowing your audience, framing your finances clearly, and making a confident case for your decisions matter just as much as the solution itself, and sometimes even more.
chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity, at its core, has always been a human thing, and I don't think that's changed. If anything, the rise of generative AI has made that clearer. People usually frame AI as a tool that assists human creativity, but I like to flip it around, because human creativity is the tool that AI depends on entirely. Everything a generative model has ever produced exists because humans created a precedent for it first. There's something ouroboros-like about it, because AI helps us create, but only because we already did the creating that taught it how. It's a loop, and humans are the ones who started it.
That framing matters when people ask whether AI "enables" creativity. I don't think it inherently does. Humans have never needed a special tool to be creative, as we're wired to find inspiration in pretty much anything. A building, a walk outside, an overheard conversation, the way light hits something at a weird angle. What AI can do is help rearrange and refine, but that's more of an enhancement to existing creative thinking than some new door it's opening for people who previously had none.
The more interesting question is whether AI diminishes the value of human art, and my honest answer is "not inherently, but the way some people use it absolutely does." There's a difference between using AI as part of a creative process and using it to bypass one entirely. When someone generates an image with a text prompt and calls it their art, they're larping on the accumulated work of thousands of artists who spent years developing a style, often without compensation, credit, or even consent. That's not creativity, and it does damage to the perceived value of craft because it floods the space with output that looks like skill without requiring any.
That said, I don't think AI-generated work is "creative" in any meaningful sense. It doesn't have intent, it doesn't have a point of view, and it doesn't have anything to say. It pattern-matches and recombines. The result can be visually impressive, but impressive and creative aren't the same thing. A human making something (even something technically rough) is making a decision about what matters to them, and that's where the actual value lives.
So where does that leave us? I think the people who will get the most out of AI are the ones who already have a strong creative foundation and use it to move faster or explore ideas they wouldn't have had the resources to execute otherwise. For everyone else, the risk is mistaking the tool's output for their own voice.

The project I am most proud of is my Restaurant Delivery System, a full-stack platform I built and have continued improving over several months. It started as a monolithic Java application with a basic frontend, but as I learned more about distributed systems through coursework, I decided to refactor it from the ground up into a cloud-deployed microservices architecture.
My role was end to end. I redesigned the backend into independent services communicating through Java REST APIs, containerized each service with Docker, and orchestrated everything with Kubernetes on Google Kubernetes Engine. I implemented asynchronous messaging between services using KubeMQ, designed a normalized SQL schema across multiple databases, and added JWT-based authentication with HTTP cookies for secure, stateless communication. I also replaced hardcoded configurations with runtime environment variables so the application could run reliably in a cloud environment.
The biggest challenge was the refactor itself. Going from a single monolithic codebase to a distributed system meant rethinking how data flows, how services talk to each other, and how to keep things consistent when multiple databases are involved. There was no single tutorial that covered exactly what I was trying to do, so a lot of it came down to reading documentation, testing things in isolation, and slowly piecing the architecture together.
What I learned is that good architecture is not just about making something work, it is about making something that can keep working as it grows. The application is still live and running on Google Cloud Platform today, which taught me that shipping something is different from shipping something you can actually maintain. That distinction has shaped how I approach every project since.

proudProject: I was in a group of 5 people, where I was directed to be the project manager of the group. This was part of a university course at UofT, where we were given a client problem from a real client, and we had over 4 months to curate an effective solution and prototype for the problem. We were a group of computer engineering majors, therefore we were expecting a computer related project. However, we received a project that was the complete opposite of our expertise; it was a civil engineering related project. When we read the title of the project being “Shoring system tieback de-stressing”, we knew we were screwed. Not a single person we knew noted what shoring systems were. At first I thought it was a boat related project. Apparently, it was redesigning a wall. How on earth are we supposed to redesign a new wall? This was the first challenge we faced, as there was just so much to learn in this new field, and we didn’t know where to start. Therefore as a project manager, I curated distinct deliverables and small tasks that can be done by the team in order to reach our goal. I made sure all the entire team were satisfied with what they were doing, and adapted to their schedules if changes needed to be made. I checked in on everyone every week to make sure their progress was good. The important thing with this kind of project is to take it slow, one small thing at a time. There is no way to learn all of civil engineering in a week. But we hit a massive barrier within our project progress; actually developing new solutions to the problem. It’s hard to create new solutions on something that has been a staple in civil engineering for hundreds of years. In fact, our client told us that we will be one of the first people in the world to ever research within this field. The team were all anxious, but I curated the same strategy, take it slow. I led discussion of brainstorming, opened talk about certain fields, and encouraged ideas from all sectors. I ensured that we were not overwhelmed at any time, and I made sure all opinions were heard. We ended with over 100 ideas, which diverged into 3 proper, creative and distinct ideas. The final obstacle was actually creating a prototype. Since we were computer engineers, 3D CAD and designing was all new to us. But again, we took it slow. We all took precious time to learn all the tools and create a decent design that was presented to our client. Our client was pleasantly happy about our design. I learned that anything is possible, no matter what background you are from, that learning and diving into something new is possible, as long as you take it slow.

proudProject:
One project I'm especially proud of is Shop-A-Sketch, which my team built at UofTHacks13 and ended up placing 3rd overall out of 161 projects.
The idea came from a surprisingly common problem: sometimes you know exactly what you want to buy, but you have no idea how to describe it. Maybe you've seen a dress, a pair of shoes, or a piece of furniture somewhere and can picture it perfectly in your head, but typing keywords into a search bar gets you nowhere. We thought, "What if you could just draw it?"
We built a visual e-commerce platform that lets users sketch an item and uses Gemini AI to convert that sketch into real-time product search results. I worked on both the frontend and backend, helping build a responsive drawing canvas in React and Tailwind while also integrating the Google Shopping API through a Python and Node.js backend. Within 36 hours, we had a full-stack application that could take a user's doodle and turn it into actual products they could browse.
The biggest challenge was that people's sketches are... not always great. A quick stick-figure drawing of a shoe doesn't exactly provide a lot of information! We spent a lot of time experimenting with prompts, testing edge cases, and figuring out how to get useful search results from imperfect drawings. There were definitely moments when things broke right before judging, but that's basically a hackathon tradition.
What I'm most proud of isn't the placement, though that was definitely exciting. It's that we built something people immediately understood. During demos, people would start drawing random objects just to see what would happen, and watching them get excited when the search actually worked was incredibly rewarding.
The project reminded me why I love hackathons so much. There's something really special about taking an idea from a random conversation, building it with a team of motivated people, and having a working product by the end of the weekend. Shop-A-Sketch taught me a lot about rapid prototyping, working through ambiguity, and creating technology that feels intuitive for users. More importantly, it reinforced that some of the best ideas come from solving small everyday frustrations.
chooseOneAnswer:
To me, creativity is the ability to connect ideas in ways that nobody else has thought of/explored. It isn't just about making art. It's about problem solving, storytelling, designing experiences, building products, and finding new ways to express ideas.
Thus, I don't think generative AI diminishes creativity, but instead, I think it expands who gets to express it. Growing up, there were plenty of things I wanted to create but didn't always have the skills for. AI helps lower some of those barriers and let's people to turn ideas into reality which I think is exciting.
Simultaneously, I don't think AI replaces creativity because creativity isn't just about the final output. Two people can ask an AI to generate an image, but the interesting part is why they chose that idea, what inspired them, and what they do with the result. The technology can generate content, but it can't replicate someone's life experiences, perspective, or curiosity.
As someone who spends a lot of time at hackathons, I've seen AI become a powerful building tool. It can help brainstorm ideas, debug code, and speed up development. But the most memorable projects I've seen didn't succeed because someone used AI. They succeeded because someone noticed a problem worth solving and came up with a creative solution.
I think we're moving toward a future where creativity looks different from what it did before. Instead of asking whether AI-generated work is "real" creativity, I think the mindset shift lies in seeing what you choose to create with these new tools.
The way I see it, AI is simply a tool. The creativity still comes from the human behind it - and now, more people than ever have the ability to make cool ideas come to life!

The project I'm proudest of is My Light and Spirit, a 2D game I built in Unity with a small team. My role was mostly on the gameplay architecture. I engineered the core systems using OOP principles and design patterns to create 20+ extensible classes, and I built an integrated level builder so the designers on the team could author content without ever touching the scripts. That part mattered a lot, because the whole point was to collaborate toward one cohesive, immersive experience instead of a pile of disconnected features.
The biggest technical challenge was writing code that could actually scale. Early on I'd hard-code things just to get them working, then watch them fall apart the second we added a new mechanic, so I had to learn to design systems that future me and my teammates could extend without rewriting everything. We were also building under real time pressure heading into the competition, which forced a lot of fast decisions.
The lessons that stuck were mostly about teamwork and focus. Knowing when to limit scope instead of chasing every idea, and figuring out what's actually worth our hours versus what just sounds cool. Cutting features hurt in the moment but made the final game way stronger. We ended up placing 3rd in a 2,000+ person international competition and grew the game to 500+ active users, which made all the scope discipline feel very worth it.

I got into it making class-based combat games in Roblox as a kid, and I'm still obsessed with the same things: tuning ability kits, designing combos that feel satisfying to chain together, and endlessly tweaking numbers until a hit actually lands the way it should.
The part I'll really talk your ear off about is balancing, and specifically how "balanced" and "fun" are often not the same thing. The technically optimal, perfectly symmetrical design is frequently the most boring one. Sometimes a slightly overtuned, slightly chaotic mechanic is exactly what makes players fall in love with a game, and knowing when to prioritize fun over the spreadsheet is the whole art of it. I find that tension endlessly fascinating.

proudProject:
NeuroStep is a project I'm proud of. It's a wearable ankle device built at JAMHacks 10 to help people with Parkinson's disease manage freezing of gait (FOG), a symptom that causes sudden, involuntary inability to move and raises fall risk.
My role centered on the hardware and on-device machine learning: pairing an ESP32 microcontroller with an IMU to track gait in real time, then training and deploying a TensorFlow Lite model that classifies the tremor and gait signatures preceding a freeze, triggering a haptic motor to deliver a vibrotactile cue that interrupts it.
The biggest challenge was bridging the gap between our training data, a clean dataset from a controlled clinical setup, and our own noisy hardware. I built a calibration routine that collects resting samples at boot to normalize sensor readings against the original dataset. Compressing our PyTorch model to fit the ESP32's limited memory, and soldering hair-thin haptic motor wires that kept breaking .
I learned how much real-world deployment differs from clean datasets, and how to navigate accuracy versus hardware constraints. Most importantly, I learned that constraints force the most creative engineering.

proudProject:
Last Wednesday, I walked into the OpenClaw Toronto Hackathon with one friend and some newfound teammates.

None of us knew anything about crypto. None of us had developed OpenClaw agents before. We hadn’t heard of the GOAT Network or the x402 transmission protocol.

But our project, ShieldClaw, built upon all of those — and much more!

Every crypto marketplace dreads the day a security vulnerability is exposed. ShieldClaw alerts you before that day arrives.

An red-teaming framework that orchestrates six specialized subagents, ShieldClaw systematically runs mass agentic attacks on any transactional crypto platform.

Best of all? You don’t pay a penny until ShieldClaw discovers a vulnerability — and you pay to find out. A staggered pricing system depending on severity makes this viable. With a Telegram bot interface, anyone can easily access the Claw agent.

And we won!

Check out the dashboard: https://openclaw-hackathon.vercel.app

My key lesson: new technologies are indeed intimidating, but think of them as just a tool rather than a vast faceless sea of code. Think of what they accomplish and what they help you to do — that'll narrow your task and help your visualize!

P.S. My best memory was showing the “how many rs are in google” LLM trick to recruiters who had never seen it before 😅

proudProject:
The project I'm most proud of is Marketly, a marketplace intelligence platform I built that aggregates listings from Facebook Marketplace and Kijiji, surfaces AI-powered pricing insights, and helps users find deals before they disappear.

The idea came from a deeply personal place. I spent 6 months obsessively hunting for my first car (a 2003 Acura RSX) on Facebook Marketplace and Kijiji. I was spending hours a day refreshing feeds, cross-referencing prices, and losing listings to faster buyers. When I finally found one, I felt a huge sense of relief. Then it got crashed into. The thought of starting that exhausting search all over again was genuinely devastating. That frustration became the fuel for Marketly.

I was the sole developer and designer. I built the frontend with Next.js, the backend with FastAPI, used Supabase for the database, and integrated Google's Gemini to generate pricing estimates and listing insights. I also deployed the full stack to Google Cloud Platform. The biggest technical challenge I faced was a cold-start problem in the insights engine. Since there are so many different items in the world and no live dataset to go off of, the AI had nothing meaningful to reason about and its estimates were either vague or outright wrong. I solved this by seeding the database with eBay snapshot data as a baseline and building a Gemini-powered price estimation fallback that could infer reasonable ranges even with sparse local data. Shipping that fix and watching the insights become genuinely useful was one of the most satisfying moments I've had as a developer. 

Another major hurdle was scraping reliability. Facebook Marketplace is super hostile to automated scraping. They actively block bots and require authentication to see most listings. My initial backend scraper kept breaking. I redesigned the architecture entirely: instead of a server-side scraper, I built a Chrome extension that runs in the user's own authenticated browser session and pipes listing data to a new backend ingest endpoint. It was a completely different approach than I'd originally planned, but it was the right call and taught me that sometimes the best solution means throwing out your first design. 

What I'm most proud of is that Marketly has real users and solves a real problem. People are using it right now to avoid the exact frustrating experience I went through. That's the part that sticks with me. Building something that genuinely helps people, not just as a portfolio piece, but as something that would have helped me made every debugging session and architectural pivot worth it. The biggest lesson I took away is that the best products come from problems you've actually lived. The motivation never dried up because it wasn't abstract. Every feature I built, I could immediately ask: "Would this have helped me find that car faster?" And that's what kept me passionate and building for months.
chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity has always been hard to define, but I think what most people mean when they use the word is something like: the ability to express something true about your experience of the world in a way that resonates with someone else. By that definition, generative AI is definitely creative at least on its own, but I do think it's one of the most powerful creative tools ever built. Honestly, most AI-generated content feels soulless to me. You can tell when something was just prompted into existence with no human intention behind it whether its images, songs, videos, etc... These things are all super technically impressive, but they all somehow say or speak nothing. The model is pattern-matching at an enormous scale, but pattern-matching isn't vision nor taste. It doesn't know why it's making what it's making. 

But then something like Tung Tung Tung Sahur goes viral, and it's genuinely funny and strange and kind of brilliant. The difference though? There's a human sensibility behind it. Someone had a comedic instinct, a cultural context, a bit they were going for. The AI was the instrument; a human was the artist and more humans expanded upon it further. That distinction matters enormously. The tool doesn't diminish the artist nowadays, but just raises the floor for what an individual can produce. Someone with no formal design training can now generate visuals that would've required a professional a decade ago. Someone with a song idea but no music theory background can now hear their concept realized. Where I do think there's a real risk is in what happens when we stop asking why. 

When AI is used to generate content at volume with no intention behind it, just filling feeds with slop, and just straight up producing noise; then yes, I think something is lost. The soul in art was never in the medium; it was always in the intention. As someone who builds and uses AI tools constantly, I've seen both sides up close. When I use AI to help me build something, the thing I'm building still comes from a problem I cared about, an experience I lived, a vision I had. The AI accelerates my ability to realize that vision. It doesn't replace the vision itself. Generative AI is the most powerful creative amplifier humanity has ever had access to. Whether it enables expression or drowns it out depends entirely on whether there's a human vision at the wheel.

Your friends, your classmates, people who didn't even play games. There was no sweat lobby to dread, just chaotic fun with a building mechanic nobody had figured out yet. What makes it bittersweet is that the conditions that made it special can't exist again. Gaming culture has shifted massively. Everything is hyper-competitive, everything is content, everything is optimized. The casual magic of just playing something before the world figured it out is gone. Fortnite tried to bring it back with OG season, and it was a great attempt, but sadly nothing will compare to 2018 :(

proudProject: One project I am especially proud of was developing a digital twin for 3D printers in Unity. It combined two of my biggest interests: game development and 3D printing. The project was created for research purposes, specifically to help track how nozzle wear affects print quality.  My role was entirely independent. I researched digital twin concepts, studied how they could apply to additive manufacturing, and built the Unity simulation myself. Since I did not have a step-by-step guide or regular mentorship, I relied on external resources, documentation, research papers, and trial and error. This forced me to become more resourceful and comfortable learning unfamiliar concepts on my own. One of the biggest challenges I faced was trying to mimic filament behavior realistically. The methods I attempted either became too memory-intensive or were not scalable, so I was unable to fully solve the issue at the time. Later, I learned that there were simpler approaches I had not been aware of, which taught me an important lesson about the value of mentorship and experienced feedback. I am proud of this project because it allowed me to combine creativity with technical problem-solving while contributing to research. It taught me how to work independently, how to push through uncertainty, and also that asking for guidance is not a weakness. I would define creativity today as the ability to use any available medium to communicate an idea, emotion, or perspective in a meaningful way. With tools like generative AI, creativity is no longer limited only to technical skill with a brush, camera, instrument, or software. Instead, it can also come from vision: knowing what you want to express, how to guide the tool, and how to shape the final result into something that reflects your intent. I do not think AI automatically diminishes the value of human art. The AI may generate the image, but the person behind it is still trying to communicate something. I understand why many artists are frustrated or concerned, especially those who have spent years developing their craft. Their skill and dedication should absolutely be respected. However, art has always expanded through new tools and mediums. Modern art, for example, can sometimes appear simple on the surface but still be recognized as meaningful because of the idea behind it. In the same way, generative AI can be another medium for expression rather than a replacement for all art. To me, the beauty of art is that it is limitless. Human value can be found in many forms of creation, and generative art should not be excluded simply because the process looks different.

proudProject:
I’ve always relied on Git for my projects and genuinely loved using it, but I eventually realized I was treating it like magic. To truly understand what was happening under the hood, I built Wyag: a fully functional Git client engineered from scratch in Python. Since it was a solo project, I was responsible for the entire architecture, from implementing a Merkle DAG data structure to building a content-addressed object store using SHA-1 hashing and zlib compression.

The biggest challenge I faced was an infuriating bug where my cloned repo’s commit hashes weren't matching the official Git CLI. After days of debugging binary parsers, I realized the issue was object identity discrepancies within the tree objects. Git expects a very specific, undocumented lexicographical sort for its tree entries before hashing. I had to write a custom sorting algorithm to ensure bit-for-bit compatibility with the official CLI. Overcoming that hurdle and finally seeing my Python clone perfectly execute 15 core Git commands was incredibly rewarding. The whole process gave me a profound appreciation for low-level system design and taught me more about data structures than any class could.

proudProject:
The project I'm proudest of is ferrodb, a relational database I'm building from scratch in Rust. I'm the only developer.

It already runs single-table SQL end to end. A disk manager handles fixed-size pages, tuples sit in slotted heap pages serialized against a typed catalog, and a buffer pool fronts the disk so nothing above it touches raw I/O. B+ trees index the data, with secondary indexes resolved through a two-hop lookup. A recursive descent parser builds an AST, and a Volcano-style engine of pull-based operators (scan, filter, projection, index scan, insert/update/delete) runs queries while maintaining indexes and checking constraints, all driven from a CLI.

The challenge I remember most was the Halloween problem: updating a row while scanning the index it touches shoves that row forward in the B+ tree, so the scan reaches it again and updates it forever. I fixed it by materializing every matching row before applying a change.

Next I'm adding joins (in progress), a cost-based optimizer, MVCC transactions, and write-ahead logging for crash recovery, then Postgres wire compatibility so standard clients can connect. Once the single-node engine is complete, I plan to make it distributed: replicating the WAL across nodes with Raft consensus so it survives machine failures, then sharding for scale.

The biggest lesson is how much of a database is correctness at the edges. The happy path is short; keeping the indexes, catalog, and disk consistent through every write is the real work.
chooseOneAnswer:
Competition math, easily. I could go for hours.

The part I like is sitting with a problem you have no idea how to start and finding a way in. There's usually more than one route to the answer, and the satisfying part is working out which tools actually crack it. Over time you build up a big collection of those tools and start recognizing which problem wants which one.

Number theory is a good example. Modular arithmetic, Fermat's and Euler's theorems, and CRT each "unlock" a specific kind of problem that looks hopeless head-on. The crossovers between areas are the part I find most interesting, like the roots of unity filter, where complex numbers can answer a counting question. That's what made me realize how connected the areas actually are.

I've qualified for the AIME and earned Distinction on the Euclid (top 6%), CSMC, COMC, and many other contests along the way.

I could keep going, but I'll stop there.

proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of is SignSense, a sign language interpretation system that my team built during my first year of Software Engineering at the University of Waterloo.

The goal was to make communication more accessible by translating sign language gestures into text and speech in real time. I worked on the software side of the project, helping integrate computer vision and machine learning components into a usable application. We used technologies such as Python, TensorFlow, Flask, and MediaPipe while running parts of the system on a Raspberry Pi.

One of the biggest challenges was balancing accuracy with performance. It was relatively easy to create a model that worked well in controlled conditions, but much harder to make it responsive and reliable in real-time use. We spent a lot of time debugging, testing, and refining the pipeline to reduce latency and improve the user experience.

The project taught me that building software is often less about writing algorithms and more about solving practical problems. It also reinforced how rewarding it is to work on technology that can have a meaningful impact on people's lives. Seeing the system successfully interpret signs for the first time was one of the most satisfying moments I've had as a developer.
chooseOneAnswer:
I think creativity today is less about the tools available and more about the ideas and intent behind how those tools are used.

Generative AI has lowered the barrier to creating things, whether that's writing, art, software, or music. Because of that, more people can experiment with ideas that they may not have had the technical skills to express before. I see that as a positive development.

At the same time, I don't think AI diminishes the value of human creativity. The most meaningful work still comes from people deciding what problems are worth solving, what stories are worth telling, and what trade-offs should be made. AI can generate content, but it doesn't replace curiosity, taste, or lived experience.

As someone who has used AI extensively in software development, I view it as a powerful collaborator rather than a replacement for human creativity. The best results usually come from combining human judgment with the speed and capabilities of AI tools.

proudProject:
Coding never felt as simple as the tutorials made it look. That gap between what tutorials promised and what coding actually felt like is what led me to help create Algorithm Alchemists, a nonprofit that teaches Python, web development, and competitive programming for free. I built the initial curriculum for our junior classes, breaking down complex programming concepts into lessons beginners could follow. I also became one of the junior teachers, leading weekly Python classes for students of all ages and experience levels. Outside the classes, I led our outreach and advertising, creating promotional reels and Instagram content that reached over 100,000 users, along with designing our website.
The biggest challenge came from teaching itself. Students would ask questions I hadn't fully thought through, and I had to find new ways to explain a concept on the spot when my first explanation didn't land. This pushed me to understand the material at a deeper level than I needed for myself, since explaining something simply requires understanding it completely. The outreach side brought a different challenge. I had no background in marketing or design, so creating Instagram content and building a website meant learning skills I didn't have, while still keeping up with teaching and curriculum work.
That outreach work helped Algorithm Alchemists expand into Quebec and British Columbia. Together with my team, we have now taught programming for free to over 300 students.
The lesson I learned is that teaching something is a different skill from knowing it. Breaking down a difficult problem for a student forced me to relearn concepts I thought I already understood, and that process made me a stronger programmer. I also learned that growth often comes from the parts of a project you are least prepared for. I started this nonprofit to teach coding, not to run outreach, but leading that side turned out to be just as important to its growth as the curriculum itself.
chooseOneAnswer:
Most people assume traffic lights run on a simple repeating timer, but the way an entire street can give you green light after green light is actually a small math problem solved by the city. It is called a green wave, and once I learned how it worked, I could not stop noticing it.
Each intersection has an offset built into its timing, calculated from the distance to the next light and a target driving speed. If you drive at that exact speed, you hit every light right as it turns green, almost like the city is clearing a path in front of you. The catch is that this only works in one direction at a time. The offset that creates a green wave going north would need to be reversed to create one going south, so most streets only give the wave to one direction while the other side just deals with normal lights.
The system is also more fragile than people realize. A single car turning left, a pedestrian pressing the crosswalk button, an accident a few blocks away, or rush hour traffic can throw off the timing enough to break the wave completely. Some cities have moved past fixed timers entirely and use real time sensors and adaptive signals that adjust the offsets as traffic actually moves, instead of relying on a schedule built for ideal conditions.
What gets me about this topic is how invisible it is. Every day, drivers move through a layer of city-wide engineering and math built specifically to manage their commute, and almost nobody notices it unless they go looking for it. It is one of those systems that only reveals itself once you know it exists, and after that you cannot drive through a city without thinking about it.

proudProject:
One project I'm especially proud of is Sonata, a real-time gesture-controlled musical instrument that transforms hand movements into music using computer vision.

I've always been fascinated by both music production and technology, and Sonata was my attempt to combine those interests into a single project. Using Python, OpenCV, MediaPipe, and Pygame, I built a system that tracks hand landmarks through a webcam and maps different gestures to musical sounds in real time. The result is a touchless instrument that allows users to create music using only their hands.

The biggest challenge was achieving low-latency performance. Computer vision models can be computationally expensive, and even small delays between a gesture and the resulting sound make the experience feel unnatural. I spent a significant amount of time optimizing the video-processing pipeline and refining gesture recognition to ensure the system remained responsive during live use.

Through this project, I learned how important user experience is in technical systems. A model can be accurate, but if it doesn't feel intuitive or responsive, people won't enjoy using it. Sonata taught me how to bridge the gap between technical implementation and human interaction while giving me the opportunity to explore the intersection of engineering, creativity, and music.

proudProject: As a team, I worked on a project on using reinforcement learning to enable a surgical robotic arm to autonomously reach and pull soft tissues during surgery in a simulation environment. As a person who has little background in reinforcement learning, the project started out rough, and I had little idea of what to do. To overcome this challenge, I began by taking baby steps by making a much simpler project using reinforcement learning to learn the technique and understand the concepts. After I got the gist of using the technique, I was able to have a better idea of how to approach the robotic arm implementation and eventually was able to build a robot that can autonomously approach the target point, grasp, and pull back. This taught me that when faced with a major technical road block, it is often easier to begin by implementing simpler versions of that task and then build up step by step to reach the final goal. This is an important attribute for hackathon projects, which often involve exploring and implementing project ideas that were previously unfamiliar to the hacker. Another challenge was the scope of the project, which not only involves robotic control logic, but also setting up a simulation environment that is able to handle soft tissue collision modeling and thereby allow the reinforcement learning training to happen. To address this challenge, as a team, we decided to take a divide and conquer approach, where one team member works on robotics control logic and the other works on collision modeling. Meanwhile, both team members keep regular communications with each other to ensure that the project remains in-sync. By doing this, we were able to ensure that both parts of the project is completed on-time and with a high quality, which are essential qualities of a successful hackathon project.

proudProject: The project I'm most proud of is Eura, an AI math tutoring app I founded and built. The idea came from watching students struggle not because they couldn't do math, but because they had no one to think through it with them. Eura lets a student draw their work freehand, like they would on paper, and get feedback on their actual steps, not just a final answer. It uses GPT-4o to read the handwriting and SymPy to verify the math independently, then responds with Socratic hints that push the student to figure it out themselves rather than just telling them what's right. The goal was always to feel less like a calculator and more like a patient tutor sitting next to you. I started as the only person building it, which meant making every decision: product, architecture, design, infrastructure. As I recruited more people to help out, I took on more responsibility over high level decisions while still keeping a high code output. This was a learning curve, but my experience as a platform engineer during my last internship gave me enough of a foundation to manage the transition. The hardest parts weren't technical. Working async across a distributed team sounds manageable until you realize how much normally gets resolved by just being in the same room. We had to get disciplined about time commitment, decision making, and handoffs in a way I hadn't needed before. Similarly, keeping costs under control while the app is still finding its footing requires a lot of intentional restraint. GPT-4o vision calls add up fast, and the temptation to over-build infrastructure before you actually need it is real. Learning to hold off on that was harder than I expected. The biggest engineering lift was migrating from a fully on-device architecture to a real backend, auth, database, file storage, all of it, without breaking what already worked. It touched every part of the stack and took longer than expected, but it's what made Eura something that could actually serve real users at scale rather than just demoing well. Right now Eura runs on a desktop. Getting it onto iOS, wrapped in Capacitor and running natively on iPad, is what we're doing next. That's where the freehand drawing experience genuinely makes the most sense, and it's the version we've been building toward from the start.
chooseOneAnswer:
To me, creativity is the ability to express a vision and convey it to others. That definition hasn't really changed with generative AI. What's changed is the set of tools we have to do it with.
I think of AI as exactly that, a tool, and a tricky one. Anyone who has actually tried to make something with it knows how hard it is to get the model to produce what you truly have in mind. I run into this constantly when I build frontends. I'll have a clear picture of the layout and the feel I'm going for, but getting the AI to render that vision usually takes round after round of nudging, correcting, and rebuilding. It rarely hands you what you want on the first try. You have to drag it toward the thing you see in your head, and that part is genuinely hard. The vision still has to come from a person, and so does the judgment about whether the result is any good. For that reason, I don't think AI diminishes the value of human art. Consider a chef. A chef who buys their ingredients instead of growing and handpicking every one is not a lesser chef. Growing your own produce might give you more precise control over the final dish, but the creativity was never in the farming. It was in the vision for the meal and the skill to bring it to life. AI sits in the same place. It gives you ingredients you didn't have before, but you're still the one deciding what to make and whether it works or not. What excites me most is that AI lets people express themselves in ways they couldn't before. Plenty of people have a clear vision but lack specific skills to realize it. They can't draw, or score music, or write code. That gap used to silence ideas that could’ve existed. AI lowers the barrier. It doesn't replace the person behind the work. It gives more people a way to get the thing in their head out into the world, which is the whole point of creating anything. So the value of art was never really about how difficult the medium was. It was about the vision being shared. AI changes the medium, but it doesn't touch the vision. It just lets more people share theirs.

proudProject: My friends and I developed Thermasense, which is a hospital delirium detection platform designed to support early clinical intervention. We used Arduino-based wearable sensors to monitor real-time patient vitals, specifically temperature variability, and streamed this data to a backend for clinical analysis. Many of my past projects were very API-centric (e.g. study apps, etc.) but this project was very different as it uses actual math and applies concepts learned in course material. We learned that a high second derivative of a patient's temperature is indicative of early onset delirium. My main role was to train and evaluate a machine learning model to accurately classify patients into different risk tiers. This was my first time working with machine learning and datasets, so I learned a lot about cleaning data and the steps it takes to train a model. I used the MIMIC-III dataset to train a RandomForest model and achieved an 80% classification accuracy. I ran into many challenges with using the MIMIC-III dataset, as a lot of the data was patient temperature data was missing so the classes were very imbalanced (17:1 ratio). Because of this, I learned about the SMOTE technique to compensate for the class imbalance with synthetic data. This project required a lot of patience and testing and re-testing to achieve a higher model accuracy, and I learned that working with machine learning takes a lot of time :(

proudProject:
As a second-generation immigrant, food has always been my parents' love language. "Have you eaten yet?" is how they show they care. One day, while talking with a friend, I realized I barely knew how to make the Chinese dishes my family cooked growing up. I joked, "I don't even speak Chinese well, when I go to university, I think I'm going to forget my mom's food."
That conversation became Food for Thought, a youth-led collective I founded at 2am at a cafe. What started as an idea grew into a program supported by a $15,000 City of Toronto grant, bringing together over 200 youth through cooking workshops, community events, and conversations about food, culture, and identity.
My role was a little bit of everything: writing grants, recruiting volunteers, coordinating chefs and community partners, designing promotional materials, and facilitating workshops, all while navigating Grade 12. One of the biggest challenges was learning that a good idea is not enough on its own. I had to learn how to build partnerships, communicate a vision, FIND THE RIGHT PEOPLE, and create experiences that people genuinely wanted to be part of.
The biggest lesson I took away is that community is designed. From recipes passed down through generations to makeshift kitchen stations in an empty community centre, every meaningful experience is built by people who care. People do not feel connected simply because they share a room. The environment, conversations, and small details matter. That lesson continues to shape how I approach everything I build today, from hackathons, engagement and creative projects.

Creativity isn't the act of making marks or arranging notes. It's the act of deciding: choosing what's worth making, knowing when something is finished, judging why one version moves you and another doesn't. Execution was always the visible part, so we mistook it for the whole thing.

Generative AI collapses the cost of execution. That's both a loss and a gift. It erases the scarcity that gave craft its premium, which genuinely hurts people who built a life on that skill. rambleTopic: Honestly? The QWERTY keyboard. It was designed in the 1870s partly to slow typists down so mechanical typewriter arms wouldn't jam, and we've been stuck with a deliberately inefficient layout ever since. Faster alternatives exist, like Dvorak and Colemak, but none caught on because switching costs too much and everyone's already locked in. It's a perfect little case study in how technology gets frozen by habit rather than merit. The "best" option rarely wins. The first good-enough option that everyone agrees on does. I find that quietly fascinating.

proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of is helping organize SOON Hackathon. Over 12 weeks, our team built the event from the ground up, creating a unique experience where 40 hackers would live, build, and learn together in a mansion for two nights.

My role was part of the operations team, and I was responsible for securing a venue that could accommodate more than 40 participants. This turned out to be one of the biggest challenges of the project. Many property owners were unwilling to host a large group, and despite reaching out through Airbnb, VRBO, real estate contacts, and other channels, I faced repeated rejections and often felt like I was making no progress at all.

Rather than giving up, I expanded my search strategy. I leveraged my network, sought recommendations from people I met along the way, explored social media platforms, and even used AI tools to identify alternative options for me to use. Through persistence and creative problem-solving, I was ultimately able to secure a suitable mansion for the event.

This experience taught me the value of adaptability, resourcefulness, and perseverance. It also showed me that some of the most rewarding growth comes from stepping into unfamiliar roles. Although I had never worked in operations before, the challenge pushed me outside my comfort zone and helped me discover skills I didn't know I had.

proudProject: At an NVIDIA hackathon I built something called Meridian — a fully local AI stack running an LLM, a Monte Carlo simulation, and a vector database, all on a single DGX Spark with zero cloud dependency. The pitch was basically: what if you could run a serious decision-support system somewhere with no internet, no data leaving the box, no API bills? Think field research, or anywhere privacy actually matters.My role was… most of it, honestly. Small team, big ambitions, classic hackathon energy. I owned the architecture — wiring the LLM up to the simulation layer and getting the vector DB to actually be useful instead of just sitting there looking impressive on a slide.The challenge that nearly broke me was making everything play nice locally. When you're not allowed to phone home to a cloud API, you suddenly care a lot about memory, about what fits on the hardware, about whether your "elegant" pipeline actually runs before the demo. There was a stretch around hour 18 where the whole thing just refused to load the model, and I had this very humbling moment of staring at a terminal at 3am questioning my life choices. Turned out to be a config issue that took two minutes to fix and four hours to find. Classic.The lesson that stuck: a working demo beats a beautiful idea every single time. I'd been so attached to making the architecture clever that I almost ran out of time to make it run. Now I build the dumbest possible end-to-end version first, get it working, and then make it smart. Ship the skateboard before the spaceship.The other lesson was about constraints being a gift. "No cloud allowed" sounds like a limitation, but it forced every decision to be intentional. We couldn't lazily offload anything, so we actually understood our whole system. I came out of it genuinely better at the fundamentals — memory, local inference, how these pieces fit — than I would have from a project with infinite resources.Also I learned I can in fact survive on vending machine snacks and stubbornness for 36 hours, which is either a strength or a red flag depending on who's asking.I'm proud of Meridian not because it was flawless (it wasn't), but because it was the first time I built something I genuinely couldn't have built a year earlier. That gap — between who you were and who you are now — is the whole reason I keep doing this.
chooseOneAnswer: How would you define "creativity" today, with tools like generative AI? Does AI diminish human art, or enable more people to express themselves?I think the panic over AI killing creativity is mostly a panic about the wrong definition of creativity.If you define creativity as "the technical act of putting pixels or notes or words in the right place," then sure, AI is coming for that, and that's scary. But I don't think that was ever the actual creative part. The creative part is taste — knowing what's worth making, why it matters, and what to throw away. AI can generate a thousand images in a minute. It cannot tell you which one is any good or why you should care. That's still entirely on us.So my honest take: AI doesn't diminish human art, but it does relocate the value. The premium moves away from execution and toward intent. A person with a clear vision and bad hands used to be stuck. Now they can express the thing in their head. That feels like a net expansion of who gets to participate, not a contraction.But I'm not a starry-eyed optimist about it either. There's a real flood-the-zone problem. When making stuff is free, the bottleneck becomes attention and trust, and we're already drowning. The risk isn't that AI makes bad art — it's that it makes infinite mediocre art and buries the good stuff. And there's a genuinely thorny issue around models trained on artists' work without consent, which I don't think "but it enables more people!" fully waves away. Both things can be true: it's empowering and there's an unpaid bill.Where I land is that creativity has always been about constraints and choices, and AI is just a new, very powerful, slightly unnerving constraint to work within. The painter didn't stop being creative when photography arrived — the job changed. Photography didn't end painting; it freed painters from having to be reality-capture machines and pushed them toward impressionism and abstraction. I suspect we're in a similar moment.As someone who builds these systems and also spends weekends on creative-ish stuff, I refuse to see them as enemies. The tool is the tool. A guitar doesn't make you a musician, and a model doesn't make you an artist. It just lowers the floor so more people can get in the room. What you do once you're in there is still the entire point.

proudProject:
A project I am incredibly proud of is Tandem, a real-time, multimodal AI tutoring platform that my team and I built from scratch in an intense 36-hour hackathon environment. The core vision behind Tandem was to bridge the gap between static online learning and genuine human interaction. We wanted to build a system that acts like a real 1-on-1 human tutor—allowing a user to speak naturally, sketch out concepts on a digital whiteboard, and receive immediate, intelligent, and context-aware feedback.

As the Team Lead and Core AI Engineer, my role was to architect the entire backend infrastructure and take primary ownership of shipping the high-concurrency interactive layers. I was responsible for connecting our Next.js frontend with a fast FastAPI backend, while integrating advanced AI models like Gemini 2.0 and ElevenLabs to power the real-time voice and vision capabilities.

The biggest challenge we faced was handling severe API latency and state synchronization. In an interactive whiteboard environment, the user's speech, mouse drawings, and the AI's vocal response all need to happen in near-perfect harmony. With only a few hours left before the judging deadline, our entire voice layer began to collapse under heavy network latency spikes. The delay between a user finishing a sentence and the AI speaking back felt incredibly unnatural, effectively breaking the immersion of a "live conversation."

Instead of panic-pivoting or stripping out the feature, I pulled a desperate all-nighter to completely tear down and re-engineer our backend communication pipeline. I moved away from standard HTTP requests and manually patched a highly optimized file-based IPC and streaming architecture directly into our stateful server. By streaming audio chunks concurrently while processing the multimodal context, we managed to bypass the latency bottlenecks. We shipped the fix with literally ten minutes to spare before submissions closed.

This project taught me two massive lessons. First, it completely demystified complex, distributed AI systems for me; it forced me to move past using basic wrappers and actually understand data pipelines, audio streaming chunk sizes, and low-latency network protocols under immense pressure. Second, it taught me the value of resilient engineering under tight constraints. When everything breaks at 4:00 AM, the teams that win aren't the ones with the most flawless initial plan—they are the ones who can ruthlessly debug, isolate the bottleneck, and rebuild a robust solution on the fly. Seeing Tandem run completely flawlessly during live judging and taking home first place remains one of the most rewarding moments of my developer journey.
chooseOneAnswer:
How we define "creativity" is undergoing a massive shift. Historically, it was tethered to technical execution—the years spent mastering oil painting brushstrokes, learning the intricacies of music theory, or memorizing syntax for complex programming languages. Today, tools like generative AI can mimic that execution in seconds. Because of this, creativity in the present day is no longer about the mechanical labor of producing an artifact; it is about taste, curation, conceptual depth, and the ability to connect disparate ideas. AI has turned execution into a commodity, moving the true locus of human creativity upstream to intent, prompt engineering, and vision.

AI does not diminish the value of human art; instead, it serves as an equalizer that enables more people to express themselves freely.

The argument that AI ruins art echoes the historical panic surrounding the invention of the camera. In the 19th century, portrait painters feared photography would kill their medium. Instead, it liberated them. Artists no longer needed to spend hours capturing a photorealistic likeness of a nobleman; they were freed to invent Impressionism, Cubism, and Surrealism. Similarly, generative AI handles the exhausting baseline labor of creation, lowering the barrier to entry for millions who have brilliant ideas but lack the physical dexterity or years of specialized training to manifest them.

For example, a brilliant storyteller who cannot draw can now use AI tools to generate graphic novels. A visionary game designer who doesn't know how to write low-level code can leverage LLMs to build functional prototypes over a single weekend. AI acts as an intellectual bicycle, expanding human capability rather than replacing it. It democratizes the playing field, making the barrier to entry for creative expression practically zero.

However, this democratization inevitably causes a massive surge in generic, AI-generated content. When anyone can generate a polished image or a functional script with a single line of text, raw execution loses its novelty. Consequently, authentic human art actually gains more value, not less. We will increasingly crave the human element—the imperfections, the lived experiences, the vulnerability, and the specific intent behind a piece of work. The value shifts from the final object to the human story and effort attached to it.

Ultimately, AI tools are mirrors of human intent. They cannot feel, suffer, or experience the world; they can only synthesize what we have already created. By taking care of the tedious, repetitive elements of the creative loop, generative AI pushes humans to think more deeply about what they want to say, rather than just how to construct it. It transforms us from solitary builders into directors, architects, and curators of our own imaginations.

rambleTopic:
I can ramble endlessly about the hidden genius of building software without an ORM (Object-Relational Mapping). Most developers default to heavy frameworks like Hibernate or Prisma because they want to avoid writing raw SQL. But when you switch to a type-safe DSL like jOOQ, you unlock total control over your database.

Instead of letting an ORM generate horrific, unoptimized queries behind your back, you get to express clean, complex relational logic—like multiset queries for eager-loading nested data aggregates—directly in your backend code. It completely eliminates the notorious N+1 query problem and makes performance debugging straightforward. There is something deeply satisfying about treating your database as a first-class citizen rather than hiding it behind an abstraction layer. I could talk for hours about query optimization, indexing, and why direct database interaction always wins.

chooseOneAnswer:
Today, I would define creativity as the process by which we transform ideas, experience, and feelings into reality, for example, through the use of generative AI. Generative AI can create visual art, text, music, and computer code at a much quicker pace than a human could alone, but humans still provide creative direction. Therefore, creativity is defined by the human who decides which problems to solve, expresses which emotions, tells which stories, and chooses what final outcomes they feel represent the way they want to present themselves.

I do not believe that the introduction of AI takes away from the value of art created by humans. Rather, I believe that generative AI is an additional method for artists/craftsmen to express themselves creatively, especially artists/craftsman who possess a strong concept, but lack the technical ability to produce their particular medium. For instance, if an artist/craftsman is not skilled at drawing, they can use generative AI to create a visual representation of their concept and then further refine it, thus providing them with a visual means to share their imagination. As such, although generative AI art will always have its place, artwork produced by human hands will hold greater significance due to the human element – including personal experiences, intent, cultural context, and emotion.

Generally speaking, I do not see AI as a replacement for creativity, rather, I view generative AI as an alternative way that artists/craftsmen express their creativity. Artists/craftsmen who think critically about how they apply the technology of generative AI while still using their own artistic taste, judgment, and unique style will continue to be some of the most creative artists/craftsmen going into the future.

proudProject:
A project I'm proud of comes from my first hackathon, where I showed up alone as a 16-year-old (I'm still 16!). I quickly realized I was actually the most experienced coder there, surrounded by university students from various engineering disciplines who had never coded before but wanted to learn, just like I did. Being around people so much older than me was intimidating at first. But once I started talking with them, I found the confidence to share my own ideas, and that's how we landed on building a sustainability game.

My role on the team ended up being part coder, part mediator. This was actually my first time navigating GitHub, even though I had coded before, so I was learning the workflow alongside everyone else. We had so much fun together: eating, playing games, and discovering new tools as we coded late into the night. Whenever arguments came up between teammates, I found myself stepping in to smooth things over, and that made pride radiate through myself.  The feeling of being better at something next to people so much older than me.

The biggest challenge hit once we finally had a working demo. I had no idea you needed a separate tool to actually deploy a website, so I taught myself a brand-new platform called Vercel on the spot, figuring it out as I went. Our end result wasn't exactly what I had pictured, but I was proud we walked away with a finished project! The moment that stuck with me most was when the judges asked what major I was in, and I said "high school." Watching the awe spread across their faces taught me to keep on showing up to events that seem over the top for myself, but in actuality nothing is!

proudProject:
During Toronto Tech Week 2026, me and my teammate built Agora (greek for marketplace).

Agora lets your AI agent buy or sell computing power from other AI agents, all done autonymously.

It is built on:

> GOAT Network
> x402 payments for seamless transactions

I founded Ataraxia, a clothing brand built around calm and serenity for a younger audience. My role spanned everything: branding, design, and building the full-stack storefront myself using Next.js, Supabase, and Stripe, plus handling shipping logistics and customer experience.

The biggest challenge was juggling every role at once. What I learned is that building something real forces you to care about details you'd otherwise skip.
