proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of was helping coach a U16 football team. While it wasn't a traditional technical project, it challenged many of the same skills: leadership, communication, planning, and problem-solving.

As one of the team's coaches, my role involved developing defensive strategies, mentoring players, and helping create a positive team culture. Throughout the season, our coaching staff worked to improve not only our players' athletic abilities but also their confidence and teamwork.

One of our biggest challenges came late in the season when several key players became unavailable before an important game. Many of our defensive schemes relied on their strengths, and suddenly much of our preparation no longer applied. Instead of giving up, our coaching staff quickly adapted. We redesigned portions of our game plan, taught players unfamiliar positions, and simplified concepts so everyone could execute confidently under pressure.

The process was far from smooth. Every practice seemed to reveal a new problem. Some players were frustrated by changing responsibilities, while others lacked confidence stepping into larger roles. As coaches, we had to communicate clearly, remain patient, and constantly adjust our approach based on what was working and what wasn't.

What made this experience memorable was seeing how the team responded. Rather than focusing on what we had lost, players embraced the challenge. Athletes who had previously played supporting roles stepped up as leaders. Teammates encouraged one another, and the entire group became more resilient.

By game day, our team looked very different from the one we had started with. Although the circumstances were far from ideal, the players executed the new game plan with incredible effort and determination. Watching them overcome adversity together was one of the most rewarding experiences I've had as a coach.

This project taught me that successful teams are not defined by perfect plans but by their ability to adapt when plans fail. It also showed me the importance of leadership during uncertainty. Technical skills and preparation matter, but the ability to communicate, support others, and remain flexible often determines success.

Those lessons continue to influence how I approach engineering projects, research, and collaboration today.
chooseOneAnswer:
One issue I regularly encounter is how isolating learning can be.

When studying for the MCAT, I noticed that many students spend hundreds of hours preparing alone. While there are countless resources available online, finding people with similar goals, schedules, and learning styles is surprisingly difficult. As a result, many students struggle with motivation, accountability, and burnout.

To address this problem, my friends and I began developing a platform that combines MCAT preparation with community building. Instead of simply providing study materials, the platform helps students connect with others who are preparing for the same exam. Users can form study groups, track progress together, share resources, and support one another throughout the process.

What excites me most about this idea is that it focuses on the human side of learning. Technology has made information more accessible than ever, but access to information alone does not guarantee success. People often learn best when they feel supported by a community.

Although our project is still evolving, it has changed how I think about problem-solving. Rather than asking, "How can we provide more information?" I now ask, "How can we help people learn, connect, and grow together?" I believe some of the best solutions are not those that replace human interaction, but those that make meaningful connections easier to create.

I strongly believe that AI diminishes the value of art as a whole, and that it removes our ability to express ourselves freely. Creativity is the process of working within the limits of the available resources with the freedom to make your own decisions.

Art is an expresion of a human condition and any rules, practices, or methodologies used are up to the person making the art. The essence of art is to be heard in the way you want to be heard, and AI clouds a practices creativity that creates its best works through its inability to find a "better" medium. If art were a lush forest, AI would be the mega 12-lane highway that cleans out a path from one condo to another.

AI can make quick, fast and easy products, but without a person to work through the creation, limits, and decisions, there is no human condition being expressed (i.e. no soul behind the art). The process and struggle with creativity itself IS the art, and therefore, the value of art is diminished the moment AI is implemented.

A large part of human expression (in my opinion) is a desire to externalize a feeling that struggles to be expressed. You can't feel the weight and edges of your struggles if you remove the struggle itself, and that is exactly what AI seeks to do. From my point of view, creativity thrives from having no clear path, so I'd define creativity as the ability to create with the need for set pathways (choosing not to use generative AI).

proudProject:
I’ve been a massive Formula 1 fan for years, so when I got the chance to work as a Machine Learning Engineer for WATonomous (design team at UWaterloo) on their F1Tenth platform, it felt like the perfect combination of my personal passion and my technical interests. The project involved developing autonomous racing algorithms and vehicle trajectory forecasting models for real racing!

My primary role was to design and optimize the systems that predict how a vehicle will move on the track. I developed PyTorch models to forecast trajectories, aiming for as much precision as possible so the autonomous car could make split-second navigation choices. Additionally, I built deployment pipelines using Docker to ensure our modules could be tested reliably before they ever hit the actual hardware.  

The biggest challenge was dealing with extreme physical constraints, specifically the latency. In autonomous racing, a delay of even a few milliseconds in the navigation stack can mean the difference between a perfect corner and crashing into a wall. When we first deployed the models, the workflows couldn't process the incoming data fast enough for real-time racing. I had to dive deep into optimizing our C++ and ROS2 workflows, strictly managing memory and streamlining how data moved between our perception and navigation stacks. After a lot of refactoring, I managed to cut navigation stack latency considerably, keeping our vehicle responsive on the track.  

This project taught me a massive lesson about data and the real world, theoretical accuracy means nothing if your system is too slow to act on it. In school, you are often judged solely on the accuracy of your model on a static dataset. But working on an autonomous vehicle made me think about hardware limitations, real-time processing constraints, as well as edge computing. Plus, watching a vehicle successfully navigate a track using code you helped write is an awesome feeling!
chooseOneAnswer:
The toughest challenge I face every single day is probably my own screen time. Like a lot of people my age, I suffer from chronic doomscrolling. I’ll open my phone to quickly check a notification, and suddenly I am forty minutes deep into a rabbit hole of reels, wondering where my afternoon went. It’s the ultimate form of procrastination, and when you're balancing a busy academic schedule, it really chips away at your mental clarity as well as your time.

For a long time, I tried solving this the "obvious" way: downloading app blockers, setting strict timers, or putting my phone in another room. None of it really worked because it felt like a chore. The real solution, I found, wasn't about forcing myself to stare at a blank wall instead of a screen; it was about replacing the cheap dopamine of scrolling with actual (offline) engagement.

I deliberately swapped digital breaks for active, real-world breaks. Instead of scrolling through a feed during a thirty-minute study gap, I’ll force myself to leave the desk entirely. I use that time to go to the gym, go grab food with friends or honestly just go outside for a walk.

Essentially, the fix is to literally touch grass. By replacing passive consumption with active habits, like lifting weights or just having a laugh with my roommates, I get a genuine mental reset. When I return to my laptop, my brain actually feels refreshed instead of drained by another screen. It’s a simple solution, but it has completely changed how I manage my focus and energy (at least for the most part).

I am proud of my first completed hackathon project. My practical
coding skills are rusty and have been for a while (I am not a CS major
;-;, and the coding I do is mostly to accelerate computations, plot
graphs, regression analysis, etc.). So by second year uni I had pretty

Our goal was to create a virtual aircraft traffic controller. The
motivation was that audio signals sent from transmission towers to
airplanes often get muddled and prone to miscommunication (i.e. people
with different accents can be hard to understand). We initially wanted
to have an audio model decipher audio between the pilot and the
transmission towers but the API kept on bugging out. Every other team
we talked to had the same issue so we decided to pivot to transcribing
a text command to audio with their API. This was an unfortunate
setback but after doing an all nighter to figure it out, we decided a
simpler completed project is better than something incomplete (with a
bug we cannot fix).  The Higgs Boson text to speech models were
reliable so we used that to transcribe a voice command (i.e. telling
the plane to move somewhere), built a scrappy plot visualization with
matplotlib so the user could see the plane movement react to a voice
command.

I handled most of the front-end and back-end. We used streamlit API
which integrated nicely with the matplotlib visualization my team
member worked on. I learned (the admittedly basic) skills of full
stack development.

rambleTopic:
Integrated silicon photonics. I believe it's the future. Traditional compute
has reached fundamental physics limits. Moore's law is dead as we are
reaching the limits to how small and efficient we can make transistors
without quantum effects becoming significant (and making any chip
unpredictable). The next frontier is in fundamentally reimagining how
compute is performed, using photons instead of electrons. The most
tangible immediate advantage is by using optical interconnects instead
of traditional electrical connections. In data centers, we have many
independent processors that need to be connected to communicate with
each other. Using electrical connections lose the shape of the
electrical signal much quicker than light, and as a result can
transmit a limited amount (bandwidth) of information. The bandwidth
using an optical connection is several orders of magnitude greater,
and doesn't come with the heat production of electrical connections.

proudProject:
A project I’m particularly proud of is a GenUI, a self evolving Shopify storefront, that explored a more adaptive form of e-commerce: a website that continuously learns from user interactions and reshapes itself to better match individual intent over time.
I played a part in the full-stack development, owning the end to end product. I designed and implemented the storefront architecture, built the interaction tracking and user state system, and integrated an AI-driven personalization layer that updated product ranking and page content based on user telemetry events such as clicks, browsing patterns, and engagement depth. The core idea was to move beyond static recommendation engines toward a system where the interface itself evolves as the system learns the user.
The main challenge was defining what “adaptation” should actually mean in a controlled system. Early iterations made overly aggressive changes based on sparse signals, which led to unstable and inconsistent shopping experiences. I had to design a hybrid approach combining lightweight rule-based constraints with embedding-based similarity models so that the system could adapt while still maintaining coherence and predictability in the storefront layout.
Another challenge was separating short-term intent from longer-term preferences. Without this distinction, the model would overreact to momentary browsing behavior. I introduced session-level vs. persistent user memory to stabilize personalization over time.
The key insight from this project was that the novelty of adaptive systems is not in making them more “intelligent,” but in making them constrained enough to remain usable. A storefront that evolves with the user is only valuable if the adaptation remains understandable, consistent, and trustworthy. This shifted how I think about AI systems, from optimizing model behavior in isolation to designing end-to-end systems that balance learning, stability, and user experience.
chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity, to me, is fundamentally the ability to express an idea, belief, emotion, or perspective in a way that did not previously exist. The tools we use to achieve that expression have changed throughout history, from paintbrushes and cameras to digital design software, and generative AI is simply the newest addition to that continuum. However, its emergence forces us to rethink what creativity means in a way that previous tools did not.
From first principles, generative AI is built upon the work of human creatives. The images, writing, music, and ideas that make these systems possible originate from countless artists, authors, and creators. Because of this, I do not view AI-generated content as something entirely separate from human creativity. Rather, it is a derivative technology that stands on top of existing creative work.
At the same time, I believe art is not merely the final product. Art is the process of expressing oneself. In that regard, generative AI can be incredibly empowering. Someone with a vivid imagination but limited technical skills can now explore themes, communicate ideas, and create works that would have previously been inaccessible to them. Natural language becomes a medium of expression. AI lowers barriers and allows more people to participate in creative spaces.
Yet I also think something is lost in the process. Traditional artistic mediums offer nearly complete control over every creative decision. With generative AI, some of that agency is delegated to a model whose internal reasoning remains largely opaque. Even if the output reflects your intent, it may not fully belong to you in the same way a hand-drawn sketch or self-written story does. The technology introduces a layer of separation between the creator and the final work.
This is where the discussion becomes more nuanced. One could argue that an artist who spends hours crafting detailed prompts, refining outputs, and iterating through hundreds of generations is exercising creative control in a meaningful way. In that context, AI begins to resemble a brush rather than a replacement for the painter. Furthermore, human artists themselves learn by observing and drawing inspiration from others. In a sense, people are also "trained" on the creative works that came before them.
For that reason, I do not believe AI diminishes the value of human art. Instead, it changes what we choose to value. When technical execution becomes more accessible, originality of thought, intentionality, and personal perspective become even more important. AI expands who can create, but it does not eliminate the uniquely human desire to express something personal. Ultimately, creativity is not defined by the tool being used, it is defined by the intention behind it.

proudProject:
One project I’m especially proud of is the urban planning tool my team built at QHacks for the City of Kingston challenge.

We created a 3D 1:1 replica map of Kingston where users could design their own buildings, customize their size, materials, and surroundings, and place them directly onto the city map. The project used Kingston’s open datasets of over 4,500 buildings and 700 roads, and included real-time simulations for traffic, road blockages, pedestrians, and movement around the proposed development site. Instead of just showing a static model, we wanted people to actually understand how a new building could affect the city around it.

My role was helping shape the idea, build out the technical direction, and make sure the final product felt presentable and impactful. Since the project was pretty technically complex, one of our biggest challenges was making the demo clear and easy to understand. Urban planning can get overwhelming very quickly, so we focused on turning data and simulations into something visual, interactive, and honestly, kind of cool.

The project ended up winning the City of Kingston challenge, which gave us a gold ticket to return to Kingston and pitch the idea to the mayor for a $10,000 opportunity. As an international student, getting invited to City Hall to present to the mayor was a crazy experience. We spent $150 each to travel back, stayed overnight, and practiced our pitch so much that we probably kept the hotel awake.

What makes me proud is not just the technical complexity, but the whole journey behind it: coding on the bus, traveling through a snowstorm, building with my teammates Phineas, Vihaan, and Dhan, and turning a hackathon project into something a real city wanted to hear. It reminded me that hackathons are not just about winning prizes. Sometimes, they create opportunities you never expected.
chooseOneAnswer:
I would define creativity today as the ability to push beyond existing patterns and create something that feels unexpected, meaningful, or personal. With tools like generative AI, creativity is no longer just about how fast you can execute. It is also about taste, direction, and knowing what is actually worth making.

Large language models learn from existing data and patterns. Most of the time, they are very good at remixing what already exists, predicting what usually comes next, and producing something that feels polished. That is useful, but I do not think that is the same as true creativity. To me, creativity often happens when someone pushes against the norm, challenges the expected answer, or combines ideas in a way that does not already fit neatly into existing patterns.

That is where human creativity still matters. AI can help generate options, but humans decide what is interesting. AI can imitate styles, but humans bring intention, context, emotion, humor, and lived experience. The most creative ideas often come from someone asking, “Why does it have to be this way?” or “What if we tried something completely different?” AI alone does not naturally take those kinds of risks unless a person pushes it there.

So I do not think AI diminishes the value of human art. If anything, it makes the human part more important. Since anyone can now generate something technically impressive, the real value becomes the idea behind it, the taste in curating it, and the courage to make something that does not just follow the average pattern.

AI enables more people to express themselves, which is powerful. But the best creative work will still come from people who use AI as a tool, not as a replacement for original thought.

proudProject:
Speedcubing started being part of my life when I was 8 years old. My uncle bought me a cube for Christmas, and after one week of obsessing over how to solve it, I was hooked ever since. 

I started going to competitions when I was 9. The biggest thing for me was how at these competitions, I wasn't weird or quirky like how I was at school, but was rather accepted and fit in perfectly. I had found my people. That is to say, speedcubing gave me so much more than just a puzzle to solve: it developed my social skills and my ability to connect, and a community to be a part of. After a couple of years, I truly grew into a real member of the community, and was even ranked 2nd in Canada at one point in the Square-1 event.

So, when I learned some React and API basics during the end of high school, the first place I went to for building out my own first full-stack project was some sort of cubing tool, one that replicated the other online tools I had used but filled in some sort of gap that I was missing. And despite the space for online cubing tools being very crowded, somehow something felt missing and I had to find my angle. What I found was missing was fast and flexible official competition data aggregation at the individual competitor level.

The build was solo and took a couple of iterations, and kept improving naturally as I got more experienced as a dev. It started with a plain Next.js project with a horrible UI before I even had AI coding tools, let alone used Claude skills. It scaled it to a much simpler React + FastAPI app, then scaled down to just React + Convex serverless to keep the overhead as simple as possible once I learned that with these sort of things, less was more. I ended up making a clean timer (one that I use now whenever I cube), with data analytics with a dashboard to see upcoming competitions in your area.

What I really learned wasn't just the code. It was more product thinking, learning to choose a specific need over a safe or broad one. As a developer, you need to be comfortable with the discomfort in building while being in a crowded space. There's still value in being unique --- this comes with cutting scope into 1-2 core features that really make your product different than any others. For me, that insight only came from being a genuine member of the community first.

To answer question 2, creativity was never about the tools. It's about having something real to say. Take it from me, I work at an AI music startup. After seeing how many ways there are to approach using AI to create, I genuinely believe that it's not AI that diminishes human art, but rather the fear of it taking over that is. The line comes with whether or not we still have something real to say. And in the world of AI, superficiality, and social media, we aren't redefining creativity, just strengthening our senses on what is real. And AI's role in this is that it blurs the line on defining what 'real' is. 

At Staccato, the startup I work at, users will urge my team and I to not tell anyone that they are using the product, an AI music cowriter. Sure, they might be just covering their asses here, but the real point of this is that its evidence that although people use AI to create something truly theirs, it still makes them feel like what they're doing is fake. What's important here is that the tool never replaced intent, although the world seems to take it as so. 

To talk about why I feel so strongly about intent over tooling is because to me, art is soul-to-soul communication. Regardless of what tools you use, whether that be AI, some magic paint brush, or hiring some company to create your vision, you can't cheat someone out of access to their soul through art. This is why realness matters so much in art specifically, and why the tooling is independent of your intention to communicate something to another human. 

At the end of the day, AI is an amplifier of real human intent, and that intent will absolutely show above the ones who use AI as a substitute to fill in words that somebody doesn't have. We aren't redefining creativity, we're just being forced to clarify what we truly meant to say all along.

proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of is TrashData, an Android file cleaner application built in Kotlin that helps users identify and remove junk files such as old files, large files, and duplicates. I worked as a core developer on the project, contributing to the app's functionality, user experience, and AI-powered file analysis features.
The application scans device storage using WorkManager, allowing long-running scans to execute efficiently in the background while providing progress updates and completion notifications. To detect duplicate files, we implemented MD5 hashing, enabling accurate identification of identical files without automatically deleting user data. We also integrated an AI-powered keyword tagging system using the Groq API and the Llama model, which analyzes file content and metadata to generate meaningful keywords. To improve reliability, we developed an offline fallback mechanism that performs keyword extraction when the API is unavailable.

One of the biggest challenges was handling storage scans efficiently while maintaining a responsive user experience. Managing large numbers of files, implementing duplicate detection, and integrating AI services required careful optimization and error handling. We also had to balance functionality with user safety by ensuring that file deletion actions always required explicit confirmation.
Through this project, I strengthened my Android development skills, gained experience working with background services, APIs, and file systems, and learned how to build reliable applications that combine traditional software engineering with AI-driven features. The project reinforced the importance of designing solutions that are both technically robust and user-friendly.
chooseOneAnswer:
One issue I frequently encounter is information overload while learning new skills. As a computer science student, I often use multiple platforms YouTube, documentation, articles, coding platforms, and AI tools to learn a single topic. While having access to so much information is beneficial, it can also be overwhelming because the resources are scattered, repetitive, and sometimes contradictory.
To address this, I would build a personalized learning assistant that aggregates content from different sources and organizes it into a structured learning path. Instead of simply recommending resources, the system would track a user's progress, identify knowledge gaps through quizzes or coding exercises, and dynamically adjust future recommendations. Generative AI could be used to summarize long resources, create personalized explanations, and generate practice problems tailored to the learner's current level.
The goal would not be to replace existing educational resources but to help learners navigate them more efficiently. By reducing the time spent searching for the "right" resource, students could focus more on understanding concepts and applying their knowledge. I believe solutions that simplify access to information can have a significant impact because one of the biggest challenges today is not the lack of information, but managing it effectively.

bearOrMuffin: I’d pick the bear with sharks for arms. The second option sounds funny on paper, but in practice it’s a serious cognitive impairment with real-world consequences. Not being able to distinguish a muffin from a baby would make everyday life unsafe, stressful, and ethically unmanageable. You wouldn’t be able to function normally in public spaces, and even simple decisions around food or caregiving would become impossible without constant external verification. That kind of uncertainty isn’t just inconvenient it fundamentally breaks basic trust in perception. The bear with sharks for arms is obviously dangerous, but it’s a bounded, physical problem. It exists in a defined situation: you can prepare, avoid it, or at least understand what you're dealing with. It’s a one-time survival scenario rather than a continuous distortion of reality. So while one option is terrifying, it’s still logically navigable.

chooseOneAnswer: Answer to question 2: I do not believe AI diminishes the value of human art. Instead, it makes creative expression more accessible. People who may not have advanced artistic skills can use AI to visualize ideas, tell stories, or experiment with different styles. In this way, AI functions much like a camera, a paintbrush, or editing software: a tool that helps people bring their ideas to life. At the same time, human art remains uniquely valuable because it is shaped by personal experiences, emotions, and perspectives that AI cannot genuinely possess. A painting created from someone's memories, a song inspired by a personal struggle, or a story reflecting lived experiences carries a depth that comes from being human. Rather than replacing artists, AI challenges them to explore new possibilities and collaborate with technology in innovative ways.Ultimately, creativity today is not defined by the tools we use but by the ideas and intentions behind them. AI can help more people participate in creative expression, while human imagination continues to provide the originality, meaning, and emotional connection that make art truly impactful.

proudProject:
I'm proud of hosting Apocalypse, a 150-teenager, 44-hour, zombie apocalypse-themed hackathon! Hackers built tech for the zombie apocalypse, while exploring side quests, making new friends, and collecting bottle caps (our made-up currency).

My role was managing a team of 10 (and being the main person securing the venue & funding), though I touched every part of the event.

There were challenges galore! It took 6 months of sending cold emails before I secured a venue (Shopify!) and funding ($50K) for the event. I chose to keep going because I really wanted this specific event, a high-effort hackathon that prioritized the intrinsic joy of making, to exist. Once that was sorted, and planning kicked into full gear, I had to manage a bigger team, for a longer project, than anything prior. I got great help from my teammates, but it was still a lot of pressure keeping projects on time, and conflicts untangled, because we only had one chance to get this right. And almost every day, whether it was spiking flight prices or a new rule from our venue, there was a new challenge for me to deal with, often requiring me to make decisions with limited certainty (scary!)

It was all so, so worth it. A few lessons:

- A genuine cold email can open up the world to you! (It's not as embarrassing as it may seem...)
- Taking a chance on strangers can change your life for the better, even if it's chaotic in the moment.
- Trust the process! I'd have a few less grey hairs if I trusted that, even if things aren't perfect, I would resolve any problem with time.
- I can do (almost) anything if I commit to the bit! Apocalypse was the first time I really felt this, because hosting it felt impossible until it happened. So much awesomeness has happened in my life since learning this lesson :)

What matters most to me, though, is how making Apocalypse happen positively impacted at least a few people! One attendee walked in knowing little about code, and has since hosted numerous hackathons of her own. Multiple others went on adventurous gap years because of the people they met at Apocalypse, learning just how big and lovely the world is (beyond the Big Tech recruiting grind).

While there's certainly much I could've done better, I'm just grateful to have been able to create something unique and positively impact other people, and hope to work on something like this in the not-so-far future :)

proudProject:
One project I'm proud of is THRD PLACE, a platform I built with a team for DeltaHacks 12 to help students find and organize study groups at their school. Our goal came from think of a "third space", that essential space beyond home and work where community happens. We noticed that a lot of students studied alone despite taking the same classes, so we wanted to make it easier for them to find others to learn with. 

My role was the backend developer. I built the FastAPI backend, set up the Supabase PostgreSQL database, handled authentication, and implemented most of the core functionality, like creating study study sessions, joining them, and managing the group chats. I also helped connect everything to the frontend and polished UI pieces when needed. 

What made this project especially memorable was how chaotic it was. We couldn't settle on an idea until around 5 PM on day 1, which meant we had far less time than expected. Additionally, one of our teammates couldn't make it, so we worked in a smaller team. 

To make things even harder, I chose to use FastAPI and Supabase even though I had never used either. Before this, I had mostly worked with Flask and simpler databases. I wanted to push myself to learn something new, but doing so during a hackathon meant I was constantly balancing learning with building. There were plenty of moments where I felt stuck trying to understand how something worked while knowing there were still features left to implement.

The biggest challenge came near the end of the event. At 3 AM, we had major features that weren't fully working, everyone is tired, and time was running out. Since I was determined to see it through, I stayed up while my teammates got some rest, and spent all night integrating features, debugging issues, and finishing the submission. It was stressful, but it was also a moment where everything that happened that weekend suddenly came together. Most memorably, after my teammates left, I met up with one of my friends from another team whose team also went to sleep so it was just two dudes with incomplete projects thugging it out with egregious amounts of caffeine.

More than anything, this project changed the way I approach learning. Before this, I often felt the need to fully understand a technology before I could start building with it. This experience taught me the opposite and that sometimes the best way to learn is to just dive in, build something ambitious, and figure things out as you go. The final product was far from perfect, but it gave me the confidence to tackle unfamiliar technologies and ideas without waiting until I felt "ready".

Even though building THRD PLACE was exhausting, it showed me what could happen if you just go for it. There's something super rewarding about turning an idea into a working product in such a short time and it left me excited to continue building and learning.
chooseOneAnswer:
To me, "creativity" is the ability to connect ideas, experiences, and perspectives to generate ideas that feel meaningful and original. With the rise of tools like generative AI, I feel that "creativity" can be expressed by people who may not have the technical skills that was needed before. In the context of art, this means that there is more of a focus on imagination, judgment, storytelling, and intentional choices. These new tools can help create images, music, writing, and designs, but humans are the ones that decide what is worth creating at the end of the day, why it matters, and how it connects with others.

I do not think AI diminishes the value of human art. I still feel that works created by humans carry personal experiences, emotions, cultural perspectives, and intentions that make the work meaningful. A painting, novel, or song resonates with the audience not only because of its final form but because they can reflect the artist's thoughts and worldview. AI can imitate styles and generate content quickly, but it will never possess lived experiences or personal motivations the same way humans do.

At the same time, I think AI enables more people to express themselves freely. People may have creative ideas but lack formal training in drawing, music composition, graphic design, or writing. Although there are people who find success even without training, there are many who don't. Generative AI can lower the technical barriers and allow people to bring their ideas to life. For example, someone with strong story writing skills may use AI to create illustrations or a small business owner can design marketing material without extensive design experience. In this sense, AI made artistic expression more accessible to those who have creative minds.

However, the use of AI in art raises concerns for me specifically in regards to originality and fair recognition for artists whose work was used to train the AI systems and ultimately contribute to the art they generate. As these tools get more popular, I think that standards need to be developed that respect human creators while pushing innovation forward.

Overall, I view generative AI as a creative tool rather than a replacement for human creativity. AI changes how art is made, but the value of human art is preserved. The most compelling creative works will always have humans at the root and generative AI can only expand what humans can create, but the meaning, purpose, and emotional depth of art comes from human insight.

proudProject: One of my favourite projects that I have built was a group project of a Rube Goldberg machine for Western's Annual Science Olympics. In the previous year, we ended up dead last: we faced multiple different issues - including no successful runs, constant waste of materials for test runs, and inconsistency for each section. It was also very rushed: as a group, we were facing conflicting schedules where everyone's setups could only be made and constructed by the developer themself. This year, we wanted the first-place goal. It was our goal to work together to not only win a podium place, but also for an actual system to work in one run. Being a contributor last year, I decided to step in and be a leader on how our Rube Goldberg machine can stand out. In countless hours after school, snacks being eaten, burns from hot glue guns, we carefully planned out each step, patched each flaw, and made sure that, sequence after sequence, our project would work once and for all. Blueprinting and tweaking specific parts, I made sure that everything was also equally contributed to by members, so one would not feel overwhelmed, or one would not slack off. The day before, our dream finally came true when each sequence worked perfectly in its place, and the day before, we made sure that multiple runs worked well before we sent it to Western. At last, we finally obtained our goal of not only a podium place, but first overall for Rube Goldberg Machines. While not entirely tech-related to my other successful software projects, it was a collaboration that was unique and carefully handcrafted by me as a leader and contributor, triumphing from a defeating the year prior.

proudProject:
Overview: Kernel Harbor is a threat detection server/agent project that I and some engineers at AMD, TrendAI (formerly Trend Micro), and KPMG worked on over the past ~2 months.

Role: I came up with the idea, formed our team, and lead development of the system with the guidance of a security researcher / engineer.

Challenges:
 - learning in-depth kernel programming (via eBPF syscall monitoring)
 - decreasing telemetry transport and ingestion latency for our Go analysis server
 - figuring out a time we could meet up every week to discuss what we're going to do next lol

Lessons:
 - Ask questions and ask them early. I had the fortune of being able to ask a security researcher / engineer at TrendAI how production grade EDRs work under the hood. I found that asking him questions especially early on in system design was enlightening and helped a ton in our team being able to create a working threat detection pipeline.
 - Documentation is king. Especially when working collaboratively, communicating your understanding of a section of a project to your teammate is crucial for everyone to perform as a cohesive unit. Documenting especially the kernel adjacent code in C was very useful when debugging and ensuring APIs and contracts we assume are true.

proudProject:
A project I'm proud of is Prompt Perfect which is a real-time multiplayer game my team developed during GDGHacks 2026 at the University of Guelph. In this game players comete to reverse-engineer AI-generated images by coming up with descriptive prompts in an effort to recreate the image under time constraints. This project ultimately won the Best UI/UX Award making it a very rewarding experience both technically and creatively.

My role focused on both frontend and backend development which is essentially a full stack developer. I helped built the React and Vite frontend while also contributing to the FastAPI backend, and worked on creating a fun designs for user interfaces and gameplay flows in Figma. I also helped implement the real-time multiplaayer functionality using WebSockets which allowed players to join live lobbies, submit prompts and see the leaderboaard updates instantly.

One of the biggest challenges we faced was integrating Google Gemini API for both the image generation and semantic judging. Unlike traditional image comparison (pixel to pixel comparison), we wanted to judge by similarity in the patterns of the image (vectors) for better accuracy and this required a lot of experimentation and testing.

Through this project, I learned the importance of designing systems that balance technical complexity with user experience. I gained hands-on experience wokring with WebSockets, API integrations and full-stack development architecture while collaborating closely with teammates under hackathon time constraints. But most importantly, I learned how user-centered design can vastly improve the quality and usuability of a product. Seeing players judges enjoy the game and receiving recognition for our UI/UX design made the project especially memorable aand rewarding.

A topic I could ramble on about for hours is science-based lifting. What started as a simple goal of getting stronger turned into an interest in the research behind muscle growth, recovery, and performance.

I enjoy reading studies and listening to evidence-based fitness researchers to understand why certain training methods work. I find it interesting how small variables such as exercise selection, training volume, recovery, and progression strategies can significantly affect results over time. I often find myself comparing different studies, looking at the quality of the evidence, and thinking about how the findings apply in practice.

What I enjoy most is the optimization aspect. There are always questions to explore, like how much training volume is optimal, how close to failure you should train, and how recovery affects progress. One thing I’ve learned is that there isn’t a single universal answer, people respond differently to the same training approaches. Because of that, I’ve started thinking more in terms of experimentation: testing different methods, tracking progress, and adjusting based on how my body responds rather than relying on a one-size-fits-all plan.

In a way, it is similar to programming. Both involve problem-solving, testing hypotheses, analyzing outcomes, and continuously improving a system over time.

proudProject:
One project I am proud of is TorontoGuessr, a web game inspired by GeoGuessr where players are shown a location in Toronto and have to guess where they are on a map. I built it because I wanted to make something that felt local, playful, and personal. Instead of creating another generic productivity tool, I wanted the project to make people engage with the city around them and notice places they might normally walk past.

My role was to design and implement the core experience: showing Toronto locations, letting users make guesses, calculating how close they were, and turning that into a simple scoring system. The challenge was making the game feel smooth and intuitive while still being technically solid. I had to think about map interactions, distance calculations, UI state, and how to make each round feel quick enough to be fun but structured enough to be replayable.

The biggest lesson I learned was that a good project is not just about having a clever idea. It is about reducing friction. If the interface is confusing, the scoring feels arbitrary, or the feedback is unclear, the user stops caring. I had to keep asking whether each part of the game made the experience more enjoyable or just added complexity.

I am proud of TorontoGuessr because it connects software with physical space. It is technical, but it is also about curiosity, memory, and exploring the environment around you. That fits what interests me about SummerHacks: building something creative under open skies, outside the usual walls of a workspace, and using technology to help people interact more intentionally with the world around them.

rambleTopic:
I could ramble about why the original Pokémon games are surprisingly good examples of systems design. On the surface, they are simple RPGs, but underneath, they are full of carefully balanced constraints: limited party size, type matchups, move slots, encounter tables, resource management, and imperfect information.

What makes them interesting is that the games create depth without overwhelming the player. A beginner can just pick cool-looking Pokémon and still have fun, while someone more analytical can think about team composition, probability, routing, and optimization. That layered design is something I admire in software too. The best systems feel approachable at first, but reward deeper understanding over time.

I also like how those games create a strong memory of place: towns, routes, music, and small discoveries.

proudProject:
Growing up, I’ve wanted nothing more than to build my own Roblox games. The more I played, the more I was tempted to turn my own unique ideas into reality. I'd open up Roblox Studio, look up a quick YouTube tutorial on how to script, and then reality would hit fast. Scripting in Lua, sourcing 3D models, and GUI creation isn't something that you can do in an hour. Rather, it's something that takes months if not years of practice. Not only is the process long, but it is extremely intimidating for a 9 year old kid.
That frustration never really left me. Even now as a student who plays Roblox, I always have thoughts about building my own games but never found the time to do so. On top of that, my younger cousin who plays Roblox constantly asks me how to build one. So when I got the chance to build a meaningful project at a hackathon, I knew exactly what I wanted to solve.
I built Archie, an AI voice agent and Roblox Studio plugin that lets you describe what you want in plain English for it to build anything in Roblox Studio. By simply saying "build me a short horror game," Archie handles the logic, writes the Lua code, and sources the models and assets.
Archie has a core two-stage planner pipeline, where it first plans with the user before executing any tools. Archie will continuously ask you questions and then create a plan for itself to ensure that its outputs match what the user wants. From there, an agent loop with 28 custom tools handles everything from asset creation and script generation to GUI construction. In parallel, Deepgram's speech-to-text and Azure's neural text-to-speech bring voice into the agent.
What I'm most proud of isn't any of the technical aspects of the project, but that I built something that solved what my cousin and I struggled with for years. It's the magical tool I always imagined as a kid - something that lets a person with no coding experience but a head full of bright ideas just talk a game into existence.
Archie won 1st place out of 46 teams at Wilfrid Laurier University, but more than the result, it reminded me why I got into software in the first place. To build things that genuinely help people.

proudProject:
Narrify — HuskyHack 2026 (Winner, Google Gemini Prize Track)
I co-built Narrify, an app that transforms dense PDFs and topics into three parallel narratives: a Hero's Journey breakdown, an educational story, and a podcast-style dialogue — all powered by Google Gemini and narrated via ElevenLabs.
My role covered the frontend (React + Vite), including the UI layout, the starfield background, narrative cards, and audio playback — as well as integrating the backend APIs into a smooth single-page experience.
The biggest challenge was reliability — getting Gemini to return clean, consistent JSON every time, and handling edge cases like scanned PDFs, oversized files, and blank extracts. We also learned the hard way to never push API keys to GitHub, and to treat every repo as public by default.
What I'm most proud of is that it actually works end-to-end — upload a PDF, get three narratives, click play, and hear it narrated. That full loop, built under 24 hours, felt like a real breakthrough.
Narrify taught me to design for failure, not just success — and that the best ideas come from asking why learning feels hard, not just how to make it faster.

proudProject:
One project I am particularly proud of was the development of a fire suppression rover as part of a second-year capstone project. The rover was designed to remotely navigate through outdoor terrain, collect environmental data, and activate a water pump to extinguish a simulated fire. I took initiative to build and integrate the electrical subsystem, so I was responsible for the wiring, sensor integration, wireless communication, and troubleshooting hardware issues.

Although the final rover successfully met its design objectives, it was a rocky path to a working prototype. A significant challenge we encountered was electrical interference. Our rover used multiple DC motors, motor drivers, a wireless transceiver, environmental sensors, and a water pump, all powered within a compact system. During testing, seemingly unrelated components would behave unpredictably whenever the motors or pump were activated. For example, environmental sensor readings became corrupted, wireless communication would occasionally fail, and our Hall effect sensor on our DIY cup anemometer registered false measurements despite the absence of a magnetic field.

Diagnosing these issues had to be modular: I isolated subsystems and tested them individually. From iterative debugging I discovered that voltage fluctuations and electromagnetic interference generated by the motors and pump produced noise that triggered sensitive electronics. To problem-solve, I researched best practices for embedded systems and implemented solutions including decoupling capacitors, grounding strategies, and revised wiring layouts. These seemingly minor changes added up to improve system stability.

Another challenge involved coordinating work across the team. Different members were responsible for mechanical design, software, and electronics, and progress in one area often depended on decisions made in another. At times, communication gaps led to mismatched assumptions about component dimensions, wiring requirements, and software interfaces. To keep the team on track for deadlines, I took initiative to document design decisions and share test results. If others understood how changes in one subsystem affected the others, integration would be smoother as the project progressed.

This project taught me technical and social lessons: I learned the importance of systematic debugging since engineering problems are often caused by interactions between components. Perhaps more importantly, I recognized that true engineering necessitates collaboration and communication more than technical knowledge. A well-designed subsystem is only useful if it can function effectively within the larger system.

Seeing the rover navigate, collect data, communicate wirelessly, and activate its fire suppression system after months of design, testing, and troubleshooting made me ecstatic. The experience strengthened my confidence as an engineer and reinforced my interest in solving complex, multidisciplinary problems.
chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity in the modern era is no longer defined solely by the ability to manually produce art, write complex code, compose music, or master technical skills. Instead, creativity is increasingly about developing ideas, solving problems, and telling a story. While some argue that generative AI diminishes the value of human creativity, I believe it does the opposite. AI enables more people to express themselves freely by lowering barriers to learning, reducing the difficulty of getting started, and empowering individuals to transform ideas into reality.

One of the most common challenges in any field is simply beginning. The hardest part of any important task is often getting started in the first place. Whether someone wants to learn programming, write a novel, create digital art, or compose music, the initial learning curve can be intimidating. Many people have ideas they want to pursue but become discouraged by the years of training required before they can produce meaningful results. As a result, countless creative projects never move beyond the idea stage.

Generative AI helps lower this threshold. Instead of facing a blank page, an aspiring writer can generate outlines and examples. A beginner programmer can receive explanations and code suggestions that make unfamiliar concepts more approachable. Someone interested in graphic design can experiment with visual ideas without first mastering professional software. AI acts as a guide that helps users take the crucial first steps, transforming overwhelming tasks into manageable ones. By reducing the barriers to entry, AI encourages more people to engage with creative and technical fields that they may otherwise have avoided.

Importantly, lowering the barrier to participation does not eliminate the value of expertise. Professional artists, writers, engineers, and musicians still possess years of experience, judgment, and domain knowledge that AI cannot replicate. What AI changes is who gets to participate. Just as calculators did not eliminate mathematics and cameras did not eliminate painting, AI does not eliminate creativity. Instead, it expands access to it. Beginners can learn faster, experiment more freely, and develop confidence that may eventually lead them to deeper mastery.

AI also enables greater experimentation. Because users can quickly generate drafts, prototypes, and variations, they are more willing to explore unconventional ideas without fear of failure. This freedom encourages innovation and allows creativity to flourish. Rather than replacing human imagination, AI provides a platform through which imagination can be expressed more easily.

Ultimately, creativity in the age of AI should be defined not by how difficult a task is to perform manually, but by the originality, intent, and meaning behind the ideas being communicated. By reducing the barriers that prevent people from getting started, generative AI enables more individuals to learn, create, and express themselves. Far from diminishing human creativity, it makes creativity more accessible and inclusive than ever before.

A project of this magnitude will always come with some challenges, especially for us first-time interns. One challenge we had was the ambiguity of the requirements of this project. Another challenge was working in parallel with the other interns. Overall, I learned how to work with a team, how to communicate ideas effectively, how to plan projects, and how to present the entire project to executives.

What is a random topic you could ramble on and on about?

During my free time, I like to listen to podcasts. One of my favourites has been Acquired, a podcast that dives deep into the biggest companies, analyzes their history, business models, and explains why they are so successful. In January, they covered Costco, and it was the first time that knowing more about a business made me more inclined to support it. Did you know Costco caps their gross margins for Kirkland products at 15% and 14% for all other products? For reference, the average gross margin across the whole economy is around 35% to 40%. Costco’s average is 12.5%. They prioritize building trust and a good relationship with not only their members but also their suppliers. Since Costco has a low skew count, they are able to buy the relatively few products they do sell in larger quantities. This often makes Costco one of the customers for most of its suppliers. So, they are able to negotiate fair prices for their members. Around 60% of Costco’s profit is from its memberships, and they have a membership renewal rate of 93%, which attests to its effective business model. I could talk for hours about how their business is continuously able to thrive during a recession or about how effective their wages paid to employees are to their business, but I would recommend you listen to the episode, because there is a lot more to Costco than their $1.50 hotdogs.

proudProject:
A project I am proud of is Research-Synth: https://github.com/Syed-Ahmed02/Research-Synth. It is a chat-first research application that turns a natural-language question into a grounded report with real citations. I built it as a full-stack TypeScript/Next.js app with Convex for durable sessions, messages, research jobs, events, documents, passages, claims, citations, and reports.

The workflow is multi-stage: plan, gather, extract, critique, cross-validate, and synthesize. The UI streams progress in one conversation timeline while the final report is saved and reloadable, so the app feels live without losing state. My role was to design and implement the core product flow, data model, and AI pipeline.

The hardest challenge was reliability. Research assistants can look impressive while still making unsupported claims, so I focused on citation guardrails: synthesis is constrained to the evidence packet, and the UI surfaces sources beside the final report. Another challenge was coordinating streaming responses with Convex persistence. I had to separate live tokens, durable messages, job status, and final artifacts so the user would not see duplicated or stale output.

I also learned how important scope is. I wanted to add Chroma semantic search over a pre-indexed document store, and the repo has schema/provider work for that path, but I did not fully integrate it in time. That taught me to separate the core user promise from nice-to-have architecture. The biggest lesson was that good AI products are not just model calls. They need UX, state, evidence handling, failure modes, and clear boundaries around what the system actually knows.
chooseOneAnswer:
I do not think  AI diminishes human creativity by default; I think it changes where creativity shows up. Before AI, a lot of creative work was gated by execution speed, technical fluency, or access to tools. AI lowers that barrier, which means more people can turn ideas into drafts, prototypes, visuals, songs, or apps. That is a real expansion of expression.

At the same time, prompting a model is not the same as having taste, judgment, or a point of view. The human part is deciding what is worth making, what emotion or problem matters, what to cut, what to keep, and how to make something feel honest. I have felt this while building projects like Data-Analysis-Agent, where agents can explore data and generate analysis, and Research-Synth, where agents gather and synthesize evidence. The model can accelerate the work, but the product only becomes useful when a human defines the workflow, constraints, quality bar, and user experience.

So my definition of creativity today is the ability to combine tools, context, taste, and constraints into something meaningful. AI can help more people reach the first draft. It can also make shallow work easier to produce. The difference is whether the creator uses it as a shortcut to stop thinking or as a collaborator that helps them think more ambitiously.

proudProject:
LyraLearn is a language learning platform I built that teaches through emotionally connected music. The idea is pretty simple: you learn vocabulary and grammar by singing along to songs that actually mean something to you, karaoke-style.
It started from a really personal place. I have Romanian heritage, and I always wanted a way to connect more deeply with the language, but every app I tried felt sterile, like I was just memorizing words with no soul behind them. Music felt like the missing piece.
I built the whole thing myself, from ideation to the actual MVP. The frontend is React with a karaoke-style UI that syncs lyrics to audio, and it supports English-to-Spanish and English-to-Romanian learning pairs. 
The hardest part honestly wasn't the technical side. It was figuring out how to make the emotional core of the idea come through in the product. Building a feature is one thing, but making someone feel like a tool genuinely gets them is a whole different challenge. That pushed me to think way more like a product designer than just a developer.
The biggest lesson I took away was learning how to build with intention. Every decision, what songs to surface, how lyrics are highlighted, which language pairs to prioritize, had to connect back to why I built it in the first place, and that habit of staying anchored to a "why" is something I carry into everything I work on now.

proudProject: I don't even know if this counts as a "project" by definition, but something I've done in the past year that I'm super proud of is winning 1st place at [case]Hacks with my team (who are now some of my closest friends), and the journey it took for us to get there. It was the first hackathon that 3/4 of my team (including me) had done. We were super nervous, but we still won first. In this team, my role is the jack-of-all-trades. While a couple of my other team members have skillsets that focus on a speciality (design/technical mockups), I find that my strengths are evenly spread out. I am someone who works on whatever is being left behind. This could be pressure-testing ideas during the idea generation phase when everyone else is tired. Or helping out with the research when my teammates feel stuck. Our team has faced a lot of challenges, and I personally have faced a lot of them too in our journey. For most of our team, this was our first hackathon. We had done case competitions together before, but we never placed higher than finalist, and even that took a while to achieve. The challenges I faced the most were: how do I find my place on my team, and how can I not let them down. As I had a more generalist role, I struggled with imposter syndrome. I often felt like I had to over-compensate or be constantly perfect to not be a burden on the team. I was not like my other teammates who had more specialized roles. I also had significantly more trouble with articulating myself under pressure compared to my teammates, stemming from my fear of public speaking I've had since I was a kid. However, I overcame these challenges by learning that it will take practice and time for me to get better at speaking, and that seeking out perfection from the start will be more destructive than productive. Once I let go of the fact that even though I may not have a specialized strength, there is still value in being able to be strong in many tasks. I decided to focus on how I can help my team and turn my imposter syndrome into learning experiences. I often learn a lot about how to make a prototype or what makes a design look good after talking with my teammates. proudProject:
One project I'm particularly proud of was an Exercise Motion Tracking System that my team built during a Health Tech Innovation Challenge. We developed a wearable solution using an Arduino and a 9-axis IMU sensor to automatically detect and count exercise repetitions.

My role focused on the software side, where I analyzed sensor data and developed the motion-detection algorithm in Arduino C/C++. The biggest challenge was accurately distinguishing real exercise movements from random arm motions. Through testing and iteration, I refined the detection thresholds and filtering logic to improve accuracy while keeping the system lightweight enough to run on the device.

This project taught me the importance of validating solutions with real-world data and iterating quickly when initial assumptions don't hold up. It was rewarding to see how a combination of hardware and software could be used to solve a practical problem in a meaningful way.
chooseOneAnswer:
A random topic I could ramble on and on about is what might have happened if Alena Kostornaia had made it to the 2022 Winter Olympics.

For context, Kostornaia is a Russian figure skater who entered the senior level alongside the eventual Olympic gold and silver medalists, Anna Shcherbakova and Alexandra Trusova. During the 2019–2020 season, Kostornaia dominated the major international competitions and was widely considered the strongest of the three. While Shcherbakova and Trusova were known for their quadruple jumps, Kostornaia stood out for her consistency, artistry, and her triple axel.

Unfortunately, a combination of the COVID-19 pandemic, injuries, and coaching changes led to a decline in her technical abilities, and she ultimately failed to qualify for the 2022 Olympic team. Meanwhile, Shcherbakova and Trusova went on to win gold and silver.

I find it fascinating to think about how the Olympic results might have changed if Kostornaia had remained at her peak. Every few months, I find myself rewatching old competition videos, comparing performances, and wondering how different the outcome might have been if she had made it to Beijing.

proudProject:
https://github.com/xfoscarz/nodeblock

Minecraft servers were traditionally coded in Java (like the official server, Paper, and Spigot implementations). With this project, I've decided to break the status-quo and implement the full server protocol in TypeScript. Choosing to write in this language means handling low-level networking, parsing binary packets, and re-framing the game logic in an ecosystem that typically isn't meant for heavy server shenanigans. The main goal of this project is to master the NodeJS Buffer system as well as raw byte handling. Due to this, I've set a constraint to use as little libraries as possible. The Minecraft NBT and packet parser have all been written with only the Minecraft Wiki as the source of truth.

Minecraft's network protocol is an everchanging system, and every new update brings massive refactoring and changes to the serialization process. A significant barrier was how the Minecraft Wiki is not officially maintained, so a lot of information tended to be outdated, This forced me to decompile the source code plenty of times to understand the new protocol changes.

Attempting a project of this scale made me understand how important optimization was. Every step of the way forced me to move away from high-level features and think about better ways to implement features and loops.

proudProject:
I have an unconventional answer. Hear me out.

A close friend recently ended our friendship after significantly misreading my intentions over several interactions. They cited reasons that described a version of me I didn't recognize. I wanted to correct their idea of my intentions in a way that left the door open for our friendship to continue. They had the right to step away from our relationship, but I wanted to give them the facts about what they had stepped away from. So over a month, with Claude as a thinking partner, I spent upwards of 100 hours writing them a letter.

My roles: author, rhetorician, psychologist, lawyer, person with strong feelings and a deep need to be understood correctly, and honest ex-friend.

Challenges:

Simply saying "you got me wrong,” wasn't going to work. I needed to SHOW my intentions, not tell—which opened a lot of questions. Which interactions should I revisit, in what order, with what framing? How am I going to convince someone who isn't particularly motivated to understand me better?

While editing, I had to find the balance between competing priorities: Write honestly from the anger I feel, but use wording they can receive without becoming defensive. Be specific enough to be credible, without it reading like a prosecution. Write something that feels complete, but not so long that they won’t finish reading it.

On top of all this, I needed the letter to respect both me and its recipient. It had to be something I would defend unashamedly to anyone.

Things I learned:

Having personal stakes in a project can fuel some of your best work. I’m proud of the letter as a piece of writing—the organization is deliberate, the word choice precise, and the ideas clear in spite of the complexity underneath. I was also surprised by how intellectually stimulating this was—I applied psychology, rhetoric, and philosophy, and found the editing process especially absorbing. And I literally committed 100 hours of effort to this, largely involuntarily. The takeaway: don’t shy away from work that blurs the line between your personal and professional (or academic, or hackathon-pilled) lives.

AI can lengthen a process instead of speeding it up—and that’s good. Having Claude as a never-tired thinking partner let me aim for something not only honest but objectively likely to be effective. It was invaluable for navigating an important, open-ended task I’d (obviously) never done before. I see myself using AI for similar work in the future (hopefully not too similar, though).

I discovered a love for letter writing. I’ve been journaling for years, and letter writing is like journaling for an audience of two. I’ve since started writing a letter to my high school choir teacher (with very different content, of course). It’s a form I want to keep exploring.

---

The letter worked, by the way. My friend read it and explained back to me their new, correct understanding of my intentions. That was the goal. 100 hours spent well.
chooseOneAnswer: I could talk all day about my passion for the UW course INTEG 120: The Art and Science of Learning. It covers a range of topics from pscyhology and learning science, with the goal of holistically helping students to become more effective learners in university. It's not surface-level either. It gives you a mechanism-level, yet purposefully practical, understanding of things like cognitive biases, notetaking strategies, cognitive load, active listening, implicit bias, habit formation, the upsides of stress, psychological safety, the Feynman technique, when you should sleep and exercise relative to when you study for optimal info retention, and more. And I listed those topics off the top of my head, which shows you how much this course stuck with me. I've always been someone who usually goes to class, but in spite of that, I think the biggest meaningful change—an immediate and lasting one—that this course motivated in me is that I started attending the lectures and tutorials, of all my courses, with this new hunger to make the most out of them as I possibly could, from a science-based perspective. Make sure my phone is tucked away, don't have my laptop open, sit in the front, process every sentence, rephrase notes in my own words, ask questions when they come up or write them down for later, stick around afterwards to ask lingering questions. Lectures started going by faster, and I felt like I was taking greater ownership of my learning. I'm not claiming that everyone will come out of INTEG 120 with this type of relationship with school, but I am really glad that this enhanced engagement I have with learning—which I find largely enjoyable—has been realized. I also really liked INTEG 120 because on top of the foundation of very interesting and immediately useful content, it's held in a nostalgic, middle-school-like classroom and it's largely discussion- and reflection-based. My prof was also really nice, not to mention award-winning. I'm sure there are many people out there like me who would find this course just as fulfilling as I did, if not more, and I wouldn't be surprised if I graduate from Computer Science considering this 1st-year Knowledge Integration course to have been the best 0.5 credits of my degree.

proudProject:
I’m incredibly proud of my Haptic Feedback Edge AI Glove. As a solo project, it was a massive challenge to orchestrate cross-communicating hardware, software, firmware, and machine learning systems. One of the biggest technical hurdles was optimizing latency. By iterating through various models, I eventually achieved sub-100ms end-to-end latency by leveraging an iOS monocular depth estimation algorithm and applying a spatial pooling algorithm with Gaussian weighted averages to translate the data into discrete haptic stimuli.

The most valuable technical lesson I learned was the absolute necessity of drawing out strict system architectures and clearly defining input/output requirements for every node before writing any code. On a personal level, I learned how collaborative the engineering community is. I spent hours discussing depth-perception algorithms with a machine-learning researcher from Switzerland just to find the right mathematical approach. It taught me that even when building a system solo, engineering is never truly done in isolation.

proudProject:
At a previous hackathon I helped build CADence, which is an agentic 3D CAD editor that allows you to create complex CAD designs by just describing it in natural language via voice or text. I designed the core agent loop, which streams each operation to the frontend over SSE, so you watch the model build in real time, with immediate retries on failed tool calls.

The hardest challenge was the constraint pipeline. We wanted users to base designs on existing spec sheets so new parts fit into a larger system, but uploading raw documents bloated context and gave poor retrieval. I designed a custom processing pipeline that parses spec sheets into structured constraints in a searchable store, so the agent pulls only what's relevant and validates against it. Through this project I learned a lot about context engineering and how to design a robust agent loop that reduces hallucinations and validates its outputs, something which is necessary for designing reliable AI agents.

proudProject:
This summer my project had nothing to do with code. I organized and captained a softball team for a nightly league, and I never expected that getting a group of people together for some weeknight games would turn into one of the hardest things I've taken on.
The logistics came first. I had to find a league that was a fair commute for everyone, handle the budgeting, set up practices, and keep all the little organizational pieces from falling apart. I also had to teach some of my teammates how to throw, how to catch, and hardest of all, how to swing a bat. Beyond that, I had to understand each person well enough to put them in a position that leveraged their skills and build a batting order that used everyone effectively.
You can prepare for all of that, but nothing prepares you for the speed of an actual game. Things go off course and you have to adjust on the spot. There's no calling a timeout to run another practice or sleep off your nerves. Once you step into the batter's box and a pitcher is staring you down trying to get you to swing and miss, it's just you.
I gave pregame talks and helped my teammates make the small adjustments that turned them into the players they always could be. But with so much focus on managing everyone else, you forget about yourself. You can't make the same mistakes you were just correcting in others, because you're supposed to be the role model who stays positive when things go wrong. When you finally get a second for yourself, it all hits at once. I'd get my turn to bat with my nerves at an all time high, and I couldn't show any of it, because my team trusts me and I trust them. If I show a lack of confidence in myself, what does that say about the trust I'm asking from them.
I'm a very analytical person who likes to think his way through every problem, and this went directly against how I usually approach things. As Yogi Berra once said, "You can't think and hit at the same time." Sometimes letting go really is the best thing you can do.
I learned all the obvious things that come with leading a team, the planning, the patience, the communication. But the most important lesson was that leading other people only works if you can also trust yourself. You can manage every detail and still come up short if you can't quiet your own head when it matters. A team will only believe in you as much as you believe in yourself, and learning to let go and trust the work we put in was what made me the captain.
chooseOneAnswer:
To me creativity means free thinking. It's the process of taking a problem or a blank canvas and working through it in a way that's entirely your own. The value was never just in the final product but in how you got there, the mistakes you made along the way, the fixes you decided on, and the moments where something finally clicked. That process is what makes art feel human and what makes solving a hard problem actually worth something. 

LLMs are impressive and I use them every day, but they're offsetting the exact thing that makes creativity meaningful. They take on the critical thinking and the working through for you. You still get a result, but you skip the part where you actually grow. You can only wonder about the implications of LLMs on the generations born into it. LLMs have subtly become everything from therapists to personal fitness coaches to graphic designers, which means more of our thinking is getting handed off without us really noticing. 

I don't think AI kills human art or enables more freedom; It does both. It lowers the barrier for people who never felt they could make anything, but it also makes it easy to confuse generating something with creating it. One is a process you live through and the other is a result you requested. AI needs to be treated as a tool instead of a substitute. If you send it the tedious parts while you keep the thinking, decisions, and the mistakes that come with figuring something out, you’ll get the help you need without giving up the process that makes the work yours. 

In my opinion and many others, AI doesn't lower the value of human creativity, it just makes it rarer, which gives you more opportunity to differentiate yourself. The ability to sit with a problem and think it all the way through is now becoming an important skill. The ones who still do the hard part and use AI as a tool instead of a replacement, are the ones whose work is going to stand out.

rambleTopic:
Advanced baseball statistics. Baseball might be the most measured sport on earth right now. You start with the simple stuff, like how often a guy gets a hit, and somehow you end up looking at how fast the ball leaves his bat or how long it takes a catcher to get the throw down to second base.
