proudProject:
April 13th, 2026. I'm standing on a stage in front of 300 students, professionals, teachers, and sponsors, leading what felt like a stand-up comedy show while an awards-printing disaster unfolds behind the scenes.

Not exactly how I imagined ending a two-day conference.

For the previous 304 days, I'd been leading Canada Model United Nations (CANMUN) as Secretary-General. Alongside an executive team of 12 and more than 60 staff members, I helped organize every aspect of the conference, from operations and logistics to sponsorships and delegate experience. By the time Closing Ceremonies arrived, we'd successfully brought together hundreds of attendees for one of the largest student-run Model UN conferences in Canada.

Ironically, the hardest challenge wasn't operational. It was personal.

Growing up, I was incredibly shy. The idea of speaking in front of a classroom was terrifying; speaking in front of 500 people seemed impossible. Yet throughout the year, leadership constantly pushed me outside my comfort zone: running meetings, pitching sponsors, speaking with school administrators, and addressing large groups of students.

So when I found myself on stage with no script, no backup plan, and a crowd waiting for awards, I did the only thing I could think of: I cracked a joke. Then another. And another. I shared stories from the year, highlighted my executive team's work, and connected with the audience like I was speaking to friends rather than a room full of strangers.

That moment represented not only the success of the conference, but moreover how much I'd grown through building it.

The biggest lesson I learned is that confidence is something you build by repeatedly putting yourself in uncomfortable situations. Sometimes the best way to overcome a fear is to give yourself a responsibility that's bigger than the fear itself.
chooseOneAnswer:
Answering Question 2:

Here’s some of my thoughts, essentially me going back and forth because I couldn’t settle on an answer…

Thought #1: Cambridge Dictionary defines ‘creativity’ as “the ability to produce or use original and unusual ideas”.
Counter-thought #1: Generate AI struggles to both produce and use original/unusual ideas. It never produces original ideas as all its knowledge comes from somewhere. It struggles to use unusual ideas as it always cross-references ideas with its existing knowledge base; an unusual idea would go against its existing knowledge and thus, the AI would begin hallucinating and overall struggle to use the original idea adequately. 

Thought #2: Thus, by this definition of creativity, generative AI is not creative; anyone who uses generative AI is not exercising their creativity.
Counter-thought #2: But is anyone ever really creative anymore? You may watch a new movie and think that it’s really creative as you’ve never seen anything like it before. Perhaps it films in a dynamic way that hasn’t quite been done before. But really, it has been done before. Perhaps subconsciously, the filmmaker saw an old film that they took one component of, and mixed it with another component that they saw as an advertisement somewhere. This ties into the idea that creativity is learned, not inherent. I think that creativity in the modern day consists of being exposed to many different things, and making unusual connections between these diverse ideas. Generative AI in many ways by this definition is creative, as it has such an expansive knowledge base and is able to make random connections that humans oftentimes have not thought of before.

Thought #3: So if AI can also be creative, then does AI diminish the value of human art?
Counter-thought #3: With generative AI, I feel like art is now more so about the process of creating it rather than the output itself. With any sort of digital art, generative AI can recreate the output easily. But what it cannot recreate is the human’s reason and methodology of creating it. Art is powerful because it reflects humans’ unique lived experiences and feelings, which is something that AI cannot replicate. So overall, I don’t think that AI diminishing the value of human art. Rather, it changes the value of human art to be more creation-focused rather than output-focused.
summerHacksGoal: My goal is to have fun while also learning new skills/tech that I can leverage towards the future. proudProject: A project I am proud of is a game I started creating called One Summer Night. I have always enjoyed creativity and being able to include my own art in whatever I make, so this project felt really exciting to me. It gave me the chance to combine both my technical side and my creative side by building a game that uses characters, visuals, and ideas that I designed myself. My role in the project was to plan the concept, draw the characters, design the overall look of the game, and begin building the interactive parts through code. I wanted the game to feel nostalgic, playful, and story-based, while also including mini-games and puzzles that the player has to complete to move forward. For example, one idea I worked on was a skatepark puzzle where the player rides through a scene, as well as dress-up wardrobe sections where the player has to choose the correct outfit before continuing. I also wanted to experiment with tools like React and Three.js to make some parts of the game more interactive and visually interesting. One of the biggest challenges I faced was realizing how much work goes into turning a creative idea into a finished product. When I first started, I had a very big vision for the game, but I did not fully understand how many decisions, compromises, and details would be involved. I had to think about the dialogue, art, music, startup page, game mechanics, puzzles, and how all of the code would stay organized. As the project grew, the amount of code became harder to manage, and I started struggling with how to keep everything clean and structured while still meeting my deadline. Another challenge was accepting that the project was not going to be completely finished right away. At first, that felt frustrating because I wanted the final version to match exactly what I imagined. However, I learned that building something meaningful takes time, and sometimes it is better to step back and focus on one part at a time instead of trying to complete everything at once. I started using a “divide and conquer” approach, where I would work on one area, such as the dialogue, artwork, or game logic, and then switch to another section when I felt stuck or tired. This project taught me a lot about patience, planning, and problem-solving. I learned that creativity is important, but organization is just as important when building a technical project. I also learned that it is okay for a project to be unfinished if it is still helping me grow. One Summer Night is something I am proud of because it represents my interests, my willingness to learn, and my ability to keep improving even when the process becomes challenging.
chooseOneAnswer: I would define creativity in the present day as the ability to take ideas, emotions, experiences, and tools, including generative AI, and turn them into something meaningful. I do think AI has not fully reached the depth of human art and creativity, because human art often comes from personal experience, emotion, culture, and struggle. If someone uses AI to create a piece of “original” art, I do think it can diminish some of the value, especially if there is little human intention behind it. However, I do not think that automatically makes it less innovative or not art at all. At the same time, AI can enable more people to express themselves freely. proudProject:
One of the projects that I'm proud about is Conversa which my team built at nwHacks 2026. Around that time, I had been going to a lot of networking events and conferences where I would always meet new faces and make connections. However, I often found myself forgetting the specifics of certain conversations that I had with some individuals and honestly, struggled to connect names with faces. So we built a pair of smart glasses that act as a "second brain". They see what you see and hear your conversations then generate detailed summary with the full transcript and AI insights so you can walk out of a meeting and actually remember it. I didn't have enough money to buy actual smart glasses so I used a security camera from Temu and flashed it with open-source firmware to make it into a wearable device.

My role in this project was owning the entire backend service as well as the hardware to software connection. I engineered the system that handled the real-time video stream from the camera which pre-processed it our AI pipeline for summary generation. One of the largest challenges I ran into was making our system understand when to start listening into a conversation when the user is wearing our glasses and record it as useful insight. If the system was able to listen 24/7, it would not be effective at capturing useful interactions and would also be very costly due to factors like LLM usage. As a result, I came up with a solution to use voice-activity detection along with adding a buffer like in real video streaming. This allowed the system to analyze segments of audio that resembles like an actual conversation (since it's hard for a computer to know what a "conversation" is) and use AI to determine whether it's a conversation that may be useful to the user in order to start recording. From this I learned that good engineering often is not about doing more but being deliberate and purposeful about what you want to achieve. I was initially tempted to capture everything with the glasses but this would prove ineffective in surfacing what actually mattered to the user such as an introduction between two people. If I had pursued this approach, I would have spent a lot of compute trying to decipher through the large recordings what mattered. By having the system understand when not to listen, I was able to better design around the intent of our project and constraints that we had.

proudProject: One project I’ve been working on comes from a problem I kept running into while building machines like my laser engraver and CNC machine. I do a lot of CAD, and usually the design process itself is fine. The problem starts after the design is finished, when the parts are printed, the main components are ready, and I’m trying to assemble everything with stepper motors, ball screws, brackets, and hardware. The part that always gets messy is the screws. I almost never know exactly what screw lengths I need, how many of each screw the assembly requires, or whether I already have enough of them. I asked a few much smarter friends if there was a clean solution for this, and most of them basically said there was not really a great one. So I decided to try building one myself. For the backend, I’m using FastAPI with Neon as the database, SQLModel as the ORM, and Alembic for migrations. For the frontend, I chose React and Tailwind, with TanStack Query for server state and Zustand for client side state. I also started using React Three Fiber because I wanted users to be able to interact with a 3D model of their project. The idea is that when someone is assembling a project, they can take a screenshot of a specific part, use a  Konva.js to annotate where the screws go, and record how many of each screw type they need. Then, they can connect that information to an interactive 3D model, so while assembling the machine, they can click around the model and see exactly what hardware is needed for each part. It is still a work in progress, but I’ve already learned a lot from building it. On the technical side, I learned how important it is to structure data properly before building features on top of it, especially when dealing with parts, screws, assemblies, screenshots, and 3D model references. Honestly, the biggest challenge has not even been the technical part. The hardest part has been finding the courage and consistency to keep working on it. I really enjoy building projects, but when I have free time, I often end up gravitating toward easier things like cleaning, watching YouTube, or working on something else. That was not the technical challenge I expected, but it has probably been the biggest bottleneck. The main lesson I learned is that building something meaningful requires more than just having a good idea or knowing the right tools. It also requires consistency.
chooseOneAnswer:
I think about almost every day: how hard it is to keep track of all the SaaS tools and subscriptions I use. At this point, I’ve built a lot of side projects using a bunch of different services: Railway for deployments, AWS EC2, Neon for databases, UploadThing as a simpler S3 style bucket, and of course AI subscriptions that slowly drain my bank account. The problem is that all these tools are spread out across different dashboards, pricing models, and billing systems. Some charge monthly, some charge by usage, and some only really become scary when you accidentally cross a limit. Yes, I could use a spreadsheet, but that does not fully solve the problem because usage based billing changes constantly. Yes, billing alert emails help, but they only give little pieces of information at a time. I never get one clear view of everything I am subscribed to, what I am actually using, and what I am likely to pay this month.
I’ve thought about building a tool that automatically checks all of these dashboards and summarizes my current subscriptions, usage, and projected costs in one place. The hard part is security. My first idea was to have an AI agent log into each service through a browser, maybe using something like Browserbase and a password manager, but that feels risky because I would not want anything touching or leaking my credentials. A safer version might be a local desktop agent that runs on my own computer, where I am already signed in. It could interact with the websites locally, scrape the billing and usage data, and generate a dashboard without sending passwords anywhere. The AI part could help write small scripts to interact with each website, but the actual execution would happen locally. I’m still not fully sure what the best solution is, especially because local models probably are not strong enough unless I magically get a Blackwell GPU attached to my ancient ThinkPad.

proudProject:
One project I'm really proud of is ScholarMatch, an AI-powered scholarship discovery platform I built with three teammates.
The problem we wanted to solve was something a lot of us had personally felt. Finding scholarships is exhausting, whether you're bouncing between dozens of websites, reading through eligibility criteria, or half the time realizing you don't even qualify. We wanted to flip that experience entirely. With ScholarMatch, you upload your resume, our AI automatically extracts your profile, the platform scraps the web to find scholarships, and ranks you based on how likely you are to actually win them.
My role covered a lot of ground. On the frontend I built out the core React interface, which includes the dashboard, the scholarship match cards, the application tracker, and the AI chatbot experience. But I also spent a good chunk of time working across the stack, helping connect the frontend to our FastAPI backend and making sure the whole user journey from sign-up to seeing your matches felt seamless.
The hardest technical challenge was building the matching engine. It sounds straightforward, compare a student's profile to a scholarship's requirements, but making it feel genuinely intelligent rather than just a checklist took real work. We had to think carefully about how to weight things like GPA, program of study, location, financial need, and extracurriculars against each other, and then use Gemini to generate explanations that actually told students why a scholarship was a good fit for them, not just that it was.
Resume parsing was another beast. People format their resumes in completely different ways, and getting Gemini to reliably pull out structured data, like GPA, degree, activities, skills, across all those variations required a lot of prompt engineering and edge case handling. There were moments where it felt like two steps forward, one step back.
We also had to make some tough calls about scope. Early on we had ideas for features like essay feedback, deadline reminders, and a full recommendation roadmap. We had to be honest with ourselves about what we could actually ship well in the time we had, and cut accordingly. That was uncomfortable but it was the right call, as it kept us focused and meant what we did launch, was actually polished.
The biggest lesson I took away is that building something people genuinely need looks very different from building something that sounds cool in theory. Every time we got stuck, coming back to the actual user experience, a stressed student trying to fund their education, helped us make better decisions.
And the response validated all of it. On our very first launch day, we had over 50 people sign up and actively use the platform. Seeing real students engage with something we built from scratch, and knowing it was saving them real time and stress, was one of the most rewarding feelings I've had as a developer.
chooseOneAnswer:
Creativity used to have a bouncer.
For most of human history, expressing yourself artistically meant you needed to already have the skills, like years of piano lessons, a natural gift for painting, a way with words that most people just don't have. The idea in your head and your ability to execute it were two completely separate things, and the gap between them stopped a lot of beautiful things from ever existing.
Generative AI just fired the bouncer.
To me, creativity has never really been about technical execution, it's about the idea. The vision. The decision to make something that didn't exist before and the choices you make along the way. A filmmaker isn't creative because they know how to operate a camera. They're creative because of what they choose to point it at. AI doesn't change that, it just removes the part where you had to spend ten years learning the camera first.
Does that diminish human art? I don't think so. If anything, I think it raises the bar for what intentional human craft looks like, while simultaneously blowing the doors open for everyone else. A kid in a small town with a big imagination can now build a game, score a film, or illustrate a story, things that were previously locked behind expensive tools, expensive education, or just plain expensive time.
The people who worry AI kills creativity are usually thinking about creativity as a skill. But creativity is a perspective. And no model has one of those, at least not yet.
So no, I don't think AI makes human art less valuable. I think it makes access to expression more democratic than it's ever been.

rambleTopic:
Food combinations that shouldn't work but absolutely do.
Everyone has that one combo they're slightly embarrassed to admit they love. Fries dipped in a milkshake. Hot sauce on watermelon. Peanut butter and pickles. On paper these sound like crimes. In practice they're revelations.
It usually comes down to contrast, like fat and acid, sweet and salty, soft and crunchy. Your brain gets bored of a single sensation quickly, so when two opposing flavors hit at the same time it registers as something exciting rather than confusing.
But honestly the science isn't even the fun part. The fun part is that every culture on earth independently arrived at their own version of this, which is why we have so many cuisines. Some combination of ingredients that makes an outsider raise an eyebrow but a local close their eyes in satisfaction.
Food is really just humanity's most delicious form of creative expression.

Not being able to tell the difference between a muffin and a baby is a everyone problem. That's a lifelong affliction with catastrophic social consequences. Every time someone hands me their newborn I'm genuinely not sure if I should be saying congratulations or asking for butter. Baby showers become minefields. Bakeries become crime scenes.
The legal liability alone is enough to choose the bear.
At least with the shark-armed bear, people feel bad for me. With the muffin situation, there's no coming back socially. You don't recover from that reputation.

proudProject:
I am particularly proud of is PathPilot, a platform designed to help students plan their degrees around their career goals. The idea came from noticing that many students choose courses without fully understanding how those choices connect to internships, skills development, and long-term career opportunities.

As the project's creator, I was responsible for everything from researching the problem and defining the product vision to designing features and planning the technical implementation. One of the biggest challenges was translating a broad idea into a practical solution. Degree requirements, career paths, and student goals vary significantly, so I had to think carefully about how to create a system that could provide useful guidance without becoming overly complicated.

Another challenge was balancing ambition with feasibility. It was easy to keep adding features, but I learned that a successful project requires prioritization and focusing on the features that deliver the most value to users. Through this process, I improved my ability to break down large problems into smaller, manageable tasks and think from the perspective of the end user.

The project taught me valuable lessons about product design, planning, and iterative development. More importantly, it reinforced the importance of solving real problems that people actually face. Seeing an idea evolve from a concept into a structured project gave me confidence in my ability to take initiative, learn new skills, and turn challenges into opportunities for growth.
